name: Home
title: Home
description: Entry page that loads and displays highlighted conservation events, beginner learning resources, and primary action tools, and routes users to detailed pages such as Events, Articles, Tools, Projects, Volunteer, and Dashboard.

core_features: Fetch homepage highlight data (featured events, featured beginner articles, and primary tools) via getHomePageHighlights on page load.
Display a primary tools section using the returned primary_tools collection, with navigation to the lifestyle quiz, carbon calculator, and action planner pages based on tool_key.
Display a featured events section using featured_events with event cards that link to event_detail.html using the event_id, and provide a general "View all events" navigation to events.html.
Provide a curated navigation link to the Events listing filtered for ocean conservation webinars by sending URL parameters type=webinar and topic=ocean_conservation to events.html.

Display a featured articles section using featured_articles with article cards that link to article_detail.html using the article_id, and provide a direct navigation link to articles.html focused on beginner plastic pollution content using URL parameters.
Expose quick navigation actions to donation projects (projects.html), volunteer opportunities (volunteer.html), and the user dashboard (dashboard.html).
Handle empty or error states for the homepage highlights so that sections are conditionally rendered or show non-conflicting fallback messages without hardcoded content.
user_workflows:
Browse and open a featured event:
1. User accesses index.html (via header nav, footer link, or direct URL).
2. Page triggers getHomePageHighlights and receives featured_events.
3. User scans the featured events list.
4. User clicks on a specific featured event card.
5. Browser navigates to event_detail.html with URL parameter eventId set to the clicked event's event_id.

Go to the full events listing or filtered ocean webinars:
1. User is on index.html with highlighted events loaded.
2. To see all events, user clicks the "View all events" control in the featured events section.
3. Browser navigates to events.html without additional query parameters.
4. Alternatively, user clicks the curated ocean webinars link in the same section.
5. Browser navigates to events.html with URL parameters type=webinar and topic=ocean_conservation.

Start a primary action tool (quiz, carbon calculator, action planner):
1. User is on index.html; getHomePageHighlights has provided primary_tools.
2. User reviews the list of primary tools rendered from primary_tools.
3. User clicks a tool entry whose tool_key matches one of lifestyle_quiz, carbon_calculator, or action_planner.
4. Based on the tool_key, browser navigates to lifestyle_quiz.html, carbon_calculator.html, or action_planner.html respectively, without additional parameters.

Discover and read a featured article or learn about plastic pollution:
1. User is on index.html; getHomePageHighlights has provided featured_articles.
2. User scans the featured articles list, including summary, topic, reading time, difficulty, and bookmark status indicators.
3. User clicks a specific article card.
4. Browser navigates to article_detail.html with URL parameter articleId set to the clicked article's article_id.
5. Alternatively, user clicks the dedicated "Learn about plastic pollution" link in the learning section.
6. Browser navigates to articles.html with URL parameters query=plastic%20pollution%20basics and difficulty=beginner.

Navigate to donate, volunteer, or dashboard from Home:
1. User is on index.html.
2. User goes to the quick actions section.
3. User clicks the Donate/Support projects control, causing navigation to projects.html.
4. Or user clicks the Volunteer near you control, causing navigation to volunteer.html.
5. Or user clicks the Go to my dashboard control, causing navigation to dashboard.html.
interactions: On page load, trigger getHomePageHighlights and bind the response to the primary tools, featured events, and featured articles components; handle loading and error states without hardcoded content.
On click of a featured event card, navigate to event_detail.html with eventId set to the associated event_id from featured_events.
On click of the "View all events" control, navigate to events.html with no extra URL parameters.
On click of the curated ocean webinars control, navigate to events.html with type=webinar and topic=ocean_conservation as URL parameters.
On click of a featured article card, navigate to article_detail.html with articleId set to the associated article_id from featured_articles.
On click of the plastic pollution learning control, navigate to articles.html with query=plastic%20pollution%20basics and difficulty=beginner as URL parameters.
On click of a primary tool entry whose tool_key is lifestyle_quiz, navigate to lifestyle_quiz.html.
On click of a primary tool entry whose tool_key is carbon_calculator, navigate to carbon_calculator.html.
On click of a primary tool entry whose tool_key is action_planner, navigate to action_planner.html.
On click of the Donate control, navigate to projects.html.
On click of the Volunteer near you control, navigate to volunteer.html.
On click of the Go to my dashboard control, navigate to dashboard.html.

For sections dependent on featured_events or featured_articles (and primary_tools), conditionally render section content only when corresponding arrays from getHomePageHighlights are non-empty; otherwise render minimal, non-conflicting functional fallback states (e.g., a generic info message) without any static domain-specific examples.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page (index.html) does not read or depend on any incoming URL parameters; its state is determined entirely by the response of getHomePageHighlights and transient in-page logic. URL parameters are only used when navigating away from the page to pass context to other pages: (1) When a featured event card is clicked, navigation goes to event_detail.html with a query parameter eventId=<event_id> taken from the clicked featured_events entry. (2) When the curated ocean webinars link is clicked, navigation goes to events.html with type=webinar and topic=ocean_conservation. (3) When the "Learn about plastic pollution" link is clicked, navigation goes to articles.html with query=plastic%20pollution%20basics and difficulty=beginner. (4) Other navigations from Home (to events.html, lifestyle_quiz.html, carbon_calculator.html, action_planner.html, projects.html, volunteer.html, dashboard.html) do not set additional URL parameters. The in-page state includes: a loading state while getHomePageHighlights is pending; an error state if the call fails; and conditional visibility for each main section (primary tools, featured events, featured articles, and quick actions), based on whether the corresponding collections in the response exist and are non-empty. No state is persisted in localStorage or any other client-side storage; any bookmarking indicators (is_bookmarked fields) are read-only on this page and reflect only the data from getHomePageHighlights.

components.functionality: On initial render of index.html, invoke getHomePageHighlights, manage loading and error states, and provide the resulting featured_events, featured_articles, and primary_tools data to child components via the page's state or a shared data context.
Render a list of primary action tools returned by getHomePageHighlights.primary_tools, each with label and description, and route the user to the appropriate tool page based on tool_key when clicked.

Display the featured events list using getHomePageHighlights.featured_events, including each event's title, subtitle, date/time label, location summary, category_label, type_label, and is_bookmarked indicator. Provide controls to view all events and to navigate directly to a curated list of ocean conservation webinars.

For each entry in featured_events, render a clickable card that shows core metadata (title, subtitle, day_of_week_label, time_of_day_label, location_summary, category_label, type_label) along with a non-interactive is_bookmarked indicator, and navigates to the Event detail page when the card is clicked.

Display the featured articles list using getHomePageHighlights.featured_articles, including article title, subtitle, topic, summary, publication date, reading time, difficulty level, and bookmark indicator, and expose a dedicated navigation control to open a filtered view of beginner plastic pollution content on the Articles page.

For each entry in featured_articles, render a clickable card with core metadata (title, subtitle, topic, summary, publication_date, reading_time_minutes, difficulty_level) and a read-only is_bookmarked indicator, and navigate to the Article detail page when the card is clicked.

Provide concise navigation controls to secondary but important actions exposed from the Home page: Donate/Support projects, Volunteer near you, and Go to my dashboard. Each control performs a navigation to the respective page when clicked.

Observe loading, error, and data-empty conditions produced by homepage-data-loader and control whether to show each section (primary tools, featured events, featured articles, quick actions) or a minimal fallback message for that section, without using any hardcoded domain-specific content.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, max-width layout based on a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters (desktop breakpoint). Content appears within a central column spanning roughly 8–10 grid columns, with generous side margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a strictly vertical, editorial flow reminiscent of the mockup: a simple top navigation bar sits above a tall, pale-green hero panel with a large left-aligned H1 and introductory paragraph. Within this hero, the quick-actions strip sits directly beneath the intro text as a lightweight functional band, followed by a wide environmental image that visually anchors the lower portion of the first viewport. Below the hero panel, functional content stacks in a single central column: first a primary tools panel, then a featured events section, and finally a featured articles section. Each section is clearly separated by generous vertical spacing, maintaining a calm, linear reading experience.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a strictly vertical, editorial flow reminiscent of the mockup: a simple top navigation bar sits above a tall, pale-green hero panel with a large left-aligned H1 and introductory paragraph. Within this hero, the quick-actions strip sits directly beneath the intro text as a lightweight functional band, followed by a wide environmental image that visually anchors the lower portion of the first viewport. Below the hero panel, functional content stacks in a single central column: first a primary tools panel, then a featured events section, and finally a featured articles section. Each section is clearly separated by generous vertical spacing, maintaining a calm, linear reading experience.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content column spans roughly 70–80% of the page width (about 8–10 of 12 grid columns) and is centered on the page, with 10–12% gutters on each side. The hero panel (heading, paragraph, quick actions, and image) occupies approximately the first viewport height, with the text + quick actions taking the upper 50–60% and the supporting image taking the lower 40–50%. Below this, each major section (tools, events, articles) is treated as a distinct vertical block with 48–64px spacing between sections and 24–32px internal padding. Card-based content within sections uses 24px gaps between cards, preserving ample negative space.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally sparse: typography is large with high line height, paragraphs are allowed to break into multiple short lines, and cards avoid cramming too many elements. Each section highlights only a small set of items (e.g., a handful of events or articles) laid out in one or two rows, rather than dense grids. Surrounding whitespace, generous margins, and limited item counts keep the interface feeling editorial and reflective rather than transactional.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F-shaped pattern adapted to a calm editorial page. The user’s eye starts at the logo in the top-left, sweeps right across the minimal navigation, then drops to the large hero heading on the left. From there, it moves down through the introductory paragraph and into the quick-actions strip (a subtle functional focal point), then continues downward to the large environmental image that caps the hero. As the user scrolls, attention is drawn next to the primary tools panel as the first major block, then to the featured events section, and finally to the featured articles section. Loading and fallback states briefly dominate the center of this vertical flow when active, but otherwise remain invisible.

layout.overall_description: On desktop, the home page presents as a calm, centered editorial layout framed by generous side gutters. A slim top navigation spans the full width but stays visually light, with the logo on the left and understated text links on the right. Directly beneath, a tall pale-green hero panel stretches nearly a full viewport height. Within this panel, a large left-aligned heading (e.g., “Biodiversity”) anchors the upper-left of the central content column, with a spacious paragraph of introductory text beneath it, both sitting in the top half of the hero with ample breathing room above and below. Just below the paragraph, a horizontal quick-actions strip aligns with the same left edge, offering a row of key actions without breaking the minimalist feel. The lower portion of the hero is occupied by a wide, nature-focused image that spans nearly the full central column width, leaving soft margins on both sides; it visually closes the initial screen. Scrolling down, the background transitions back to a very light neutral or white, and the main content continues within the same centered column. First appears the primary tools panel as a softly emphasized block with a subtle tint and clear internal padding, introducing the main tools users can act on. Below it, separated by generous vertical whitespace, the featured events section appears as an editorial row of event cards under a simple “Featured Events” heading, the cards arranged in a light, airy grid. Further down, the featured articles section mirrors this treatment but with more text-centric cards arranged in fewer columns to support readability. Throughout, each section is clearly differentiated by vertical spacing rather than heavy lines or backgrounds, preserving a serene, environmental-science tone. When data is loading, a centered overlay gracefully sits atop this vertical stack; if something fails, a single centered fallback message replaces the content in the same calm, editorial manner.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The homepage-data-loader is not a constant visual element but an overlay state that temporarily sits above the main content column. When active (e.g., during initial page load), it covers the area from just below the top navigation down through the primary tools, events, and articles regions, effectively masking the hero content and subsequent sections without obscuring the fixed header. It spans the same central content width (roughly 70–80% of the viewport, aligned to the grid) and is horizontally centered, but a semi-transparent pale overlay can softly extend edge-to-edge to dim the background, preserving the calm aesthetic. At its center, within the central column, a simple loading indicator and a brief status line are vertically and horizontally centered between the top of the hero and the midpoint of the viewport. There is generous padding (at least 32–48px) between loader text and any edges of its container, and no additional clutter or borders, keeping the loading state minimal and consistent with the low-density editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: When inactive, it does not render. When active, it behaves as a fixed or absolutely positioned overlay anchored to the main content area, centered both vertically and horizontally relative to the viewport, allowing the top navigation to remain visible while subtly dimming the content beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-tools-panel appears as the first major functional section immediately below the hero image’s bottom edge. It occupies the central content column, spanning approximately 70–80% of the viewport width, with equal left and right gutters of about 10–12% each. There is a generous vertical gap (48–64px) above it separating it from the hero panel, creating a clear section break. The panel itself is a softly highlighted block with a very light green or neutral tint, rounded corners, and internal padding of roughly 24px top/bottom and 32px left/right. A section heading is aligned to the left edge of this panel, in line with the hero heading’s left alignment, followed by a brief line or two of explanatory text with comfortable line height. Below this, tools are arranged in a simple grid that respects the editorial spacing: on desktop, this might be a 2–3 column layout of tool items, each taking up about one third to one half of the panel width with 24px gaps between them, ensuring individual tiles have plenty of breathing room. Vertically, the panel height is determined by the content but typically fills around one third of a viewport height, maintaining spacious margins above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow, scrolling naturally with the page, always aligned to the same central grid column as the hero content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-events-section sits directly beneath the primary-tools-panel, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px. It spans the full page width but visually confines its content to the same central column used elsewhere, leaving wide side gutters. Inside this central column, a concise section label or heading such as “Featured Events” is left-aligned at the top, with a short descriptive sentence beneath it if needed, maintaining a 16–24px gap between heading and text. Below the heading block, event cards are arranged in a light grid: on desktop, typically a single row of 3 cards, each card occupying roughly one third of the central column width minus 24px horizontal gaps. If there are more than three events, a second row can appear, but total rows remain limited to preserve the low-density feel. Vertical spacing between rows is around 24px. The overall section height is content-driven, but the visual impression is of a broad, airy band occupying roughly one third to half a viewport height, with plenty of breathing room above and below the card grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scroll flow, adhering to the central grid alignment. Cards reflow into fewer columns at narrower widths while preserving generous gaps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the featured-events-section grid, each featured-event-card is a vertically oriented tile with a clear top-to-bottom hierarchy. On desktop, each card occupies roughly 30–32% of the central column’s width, with 24px horizontal spacing from neighboring cards. The card’s background is white or a very light neutral, subtly elevated from the page with a soft shadow or simple border, and it includes approximately 24px internal padding on all sides. At the top of the card, an event-related image or color block spans the full card width with a fixed aspect ratio (e.g., 16:9 or 4:3), occupying about the upper 40% of the card height. Beneath the image, the event title is left-aligned, taking 1–2 lines with a comfortable 4–8px gap below it. Below the title, secondary metadata (date, location) is arranged on one or two short lines with slightly lighter weight, followed by a short, 2–3 line description if available. Line heights are generous to match the editorial style, and the bottom of the card includes a subtle call to action (e.g., “View event”) aligned to the left, with at least 16px padding to the card’s bottom edge. The cards are designed so that even when several appear in a row, the overall impression remains airy rather than crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying fixed positions within the responsive grid of the featured-events-section; no floating behavior, but cards wrap to new lines on narrower screens while retaining consistent padding and gaps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-articles-section appears below the featured-events-section, separated by another 48–64px vertical space to clearly delineate the sections. Like the events section, it spans the full page width but visually centers its content within the main column. A left-aligned heading such as “Featured Articles” sits at the top of the section with a short descriptive line below it, mirroring the hierarchy used in the events section. Because articles are more text-heavy, the cards in this section are arranged in a slightly less dense configuration: on desktop, typically a 2-column layout where each column occupies about 45–48% of the central column width, with a 24–32px gap between columns. The cards form one or two rows at most to keep the section from feeling heavy. The overall section reads as a calm editorial block occupying roughly one third to half a viewport height, with notable whitespace at the top and bottom and between the cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow, aligned to the same central grid as other sections. Cards reflow to a single column on narrower viewports while retaining vertical spacing that preserves the low-density aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each featured-article-card is a text-first tile optimized for comfortable reading within the featured-articles-section. On desktop, a card occupies nearly half of the central column’s width in a 2-column layout, with 24–32px horizontal spacing between cards. The card has a white or very light background with subtle 24px padding on all sides. At the top, an optional small thumbnail image or category tag occupies a narrow horizontal strip, followed by the article title set in a slightly larger, bold type with 1–3 lines of text. There is a 8–12px gap between the title and a short meta line (author, date, or reading time) in a lighter weight. Beneath the metadata, a brief excerpt occupies 2–4 lines with generous line height, making the card feel more like a magazine snippet than a dense list item. At the bottom, a subtle text link or small button such as “Read article” is left-aligned with 16–24px breathing room above the card’s bottom edge. The vertical stacking of elements and whitespace between them ensure that even multiple cards on a row remain visually light and easy to scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, arranged within the grid of the featured-articles-section. On smaller screens, cards stack vertically while preserving internal padding and vertical gaps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-actions-strip resides within the hero panel, directly supporting the introductory content. It is positioned beneath the main hero paragraph, with a vertical gap of about 24–32px from the bottom of the paragraph, and above the large environmental image with another 32–40px of space beneath it. Horizontally, it aligns with the central content column and the left edge of the hero heading, occupying roughly 60–70% of the page width on desktop, leaving the outer gutters intact. Visually, it appears as a low-profile horizontal band or row of pill-shaped buttons or compact tiles representing primary destinations (e.g., Events, Articles, Tools, Volunteer). Each quick action has generous left/right padding (16–24px) and a minimum touch height, with 16–24px gaps between actions to keep the row from feeling cramped. The background of the strip may either be transparent (actions appearing directly on the pale green hero background) or lightly tinted for subtle emphasis, but it avoids heavy borders or strong contrast to maintain the hero’s calm aesthetic. The strip is tall enough to be noticed but not so tall that it competes with the hero heading; overall it occupies roughly 15–20% of the hero’s vertical height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section, flowing with the content as the user scrolls. On narrower screens, the action items may wrap to multiple lines or convert into a horizontally scrollable row while remaining anchored in the same vertical position relative to the hero text.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The homepage-fallback-handler presents a graceful fallback when content cannot be loaded. When triggered, it replaces the main body content (hero image, primary tools panel, events, and articles sections) with a single, centered message area while leaving the top navigation intact. Visually, the fallback occupies the central content column, spanning about 70–80% of the page width and vertically centered within the visible viewport (or at least with 64–96px padding from the top to avoid feeling cramped). It appears as a simple, lightly elevated card or plain text block over the standard background, with 24–32px internal padding and a clear, concise messaging hierarchy: a short heading at the top, a brief explanatory paragraph beneath, and optionally one or two primary actions (e.g., “Retry” or “Go to Dashboard”) aligned to the left edge. The surrounding whitespace is generous, with no other competing elements, so the fallback message is easy to understand without visual clutter. When the fallback is dismissed or the error resolves, the regular content sections regain their place in the vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered; when active, it occupies the same central grid area as the main content, effectively replacing those sections in the document flow. It may also slightly adjust vertical alignment (e.g., center in viewport) but otherwise remains static with respect to scrolling.

name: Events & Programs
title: Events & Programs
description: Search, filter, sort, and browse environmental events and programs, including in-person activities and online webinars, with the ability to bookmark events and open detailed event pages.

core_features: Load available event filter options (types, categories, topics, date presets, day/time options, distance ranges, and sort options) using getEventFilterOptions, and initialize filter controls from these options and any incoming URL parameters.

Provide a filterable search form where users can refine events by keyword, location (postal code and distance), date range (via presets or explicit start/end), event type, category, topic, day of week, and time of day, then call searchEvents with the selected filters.
Allow sorting of event results using sort options returned by getEventFilterOptions, passing the chosen sort key as sort_by to searchEvents and reflecting the current sort in the URL.

Display a paginated list of event results from searchEvents, showing key details for each event such as title, dates, day-of-week label, time-of-day label, timezone, location summary, distance, type label, category label, topics, registration requirement, remaining spots, and bookmark/registration indicators.
Enable bookmarking of events using bookmarkEvent when a user toggles a bookmark control on an event card, and update the event’s is_bookmarked state in the listing based on the API response.
Provide navigation from each event card to the detailed event page (event_detail.html) using the event’s ID in the URL parameters.
Provide navigation to the dashboard (dashboard.html) via a 'View my events' shortcut so users can see their saved or registered events.
Maintain filter, sort, and pagination state via URL parameters so that event listings are shareable, restorable via browser navigation, and can be pre-populated from other pages.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Landing on Events & Programs from navigation without presets:
1. User clicks the main site navigation link for Events or Events & Webinars.
2. Browser opens events.html without query parameters.
3. Page controller parses URL (no relevant params present) and calls getEventFilterOptions.
4. Page initializes filter controls using the options returned by getEventFilterOptions (including distance range, date presets, categories, topics, etc.), and sets defaults based on those options.
5. Page calls searchEvents with an empty or default keyword, filters derived from default control values, default sort_by (from getEventFilterOptions.sort_options), and initial page/page_size values.
6. searchEvents returns results, total_count, applied_sort, and pagination info; the event results list renders these events.
7. URL is updated (if necessary) to reflect the initial applied filters and sort so that refreshing or sharing the URL recreates the same listing.

Workflow 2 – Arriving with pre-populated filters from Home or Dashboard:
1. User clicks a contextual link on index.html or dashboard.html that points to events.html with query parameters (e.g., type preset, category, datePreset, postalCode, etc.).
2. Page loads, parses all incoming URL parameters, and merges them with filter options from getEventFilterOptions (e.g., ensuring incoming type or category matches an available option).
3. Filter controls (type, category, topic, postal code, distance, date, dayOfWeek, timeOfDay, sort) are initialized to the values derived from URL parameters when valid; missing or invalid values fall back to defaults derived from getEventFilterOptions.
4. Page calls searchEvents using keyword (if present), filters constructed from the resolved control state, sort_by from the URL (or default), and an initial page/page_size.
5. Results render; the visible filter controls match the pre-selected values so the user can adjust them and re-run the search.
6. When the user changes any control or clears presets, the URL parameters are updated and a new searchEvents call is made, keeping the page state in sync with the URL.

Workflow 3 – Refining filters and applying search:
1. User types text into the keyword field (e.g., a topic or city) and adjusts location-related fields (postal code and distance radius).
2. User optionally selects or changes date range using a date preset control, or by manually setting explicit start and end dates.
3. User adjusts event type, category, and topic controls, and optionally selects a preferred day of the week and time of day.
4. On pressing an 'Apply filters' control or on filter change (depending on implementation), the filter form builds a filters object mapping UI values to the expected searchEvents filters structure.
5. The current page index is reset to the first page in component state and in URL parameters; the sort_by value is taken from the sort control or from the existing URL parameter.
6. Page calls searchEvents with the constructed keyword, filters, sort_by, page, and page_size.
7. Results list updates to reflect the new filters; total_count and any empty-state feedback are updated.
8. The URL querystring is updated to reflect the active filters (including postalCode, distanceMiles, type, category, topic, datePreset or startDate/endDate, dayOfWeek, timeOfDay, sort) so the user can bookmark or share the filtered view.

Workflow 4 – Sorting and pagination of results:
1. User changes the sort selection (e.g., from the default to another sort option provided by getEventFilterOptions.sort_options).
2. Sort control updates the sort_by value in the page state and updates the sort parameter in the URL.
3. Page resets the current page index to the first page and calls searchEvents with the existing keyword and filters, but using the new sort_by.
4. searchEvents returns a new result set ordered by the applied_sort; results list re-renders.
5. For pagination, when the user clicks a specific page number or next/previous controls, the pagination component updates the page index in state and in the URL, then calls searchEvents with the unchanged keyword/filters/sort_by and the new page.
6. Results list updates to reflect the new page of events while keeping all filters and sort options intact.

Workflow 5 – Viewing event details and returning to the listing:
1. In the results list, user clicks an event title or the main clickable area of an event card.
2. The page navigates to event_detail.html with the selected event’s ID passed as a URL parameter (event_detail.html?eventId={event_id}).
3. After viewing details and possibly registering, the user returns via browser back or a link back to Events & Programs.
4. Because filters, sort, and page index are encoded in the URL, the Events & Programs page restores the same query parameters, re-applies them to filter controls, and calls searchEvents to re-display the same result context.

Workflow 6 – Bookmarking an event:
1. User sees a bookmark control (e.g., an icon or button) on an event card, whose initial state reflects the is_bookmarked value returned by searchEvents for that event.
2. User clicks the bookmark control for an event that is not yet bookmarked.
3. The event card component calls bookmarkEvent with the event’s ID and a source label that identifies the origin of the action (e.g., a generic label representing this page’s bookmark control).
4. When bookmarkEvent returns success, the card updates its local is_bookmarked state (and optionally re-syncs via another searchEvents call, if desired) so the bookmark control reflects the saved state.
5. If bookmarkEvent indicates the event is already bookmarked, the component ensures the UI shows the bookmarked state but does not create duplicates.
6. User can click a 'View my events' shortcut, which navigates to dashboard.html using normal navigation; dashboard.html will read SavedEvent data and/or the user’s registrations independently.

Workflow 7 – Clearing filters and resetting the view:
1. User clicks a global 'Clear filters' control or removes individual filter chips in an active-filters-summary component.
2. The page controller resets the corresponding filter control values to defaults determined by getEventFilterOptions and removes relevant parameters from the URL.
3. Page resets the page index to the first page and calls searchEvents with the updated, less restrictive filters and current sort_by (or default, if sort is also cleared).
4. The results list updates to show events based on the cleared filters, and the filter controls reflect the reset state.
interactions:
On page initialization (onInit or equivalent):
- Parse URL query parameters for postalCode, distanceMiles, type, category, topic, datePreset, startDate, endDate, dayOfWeek, timeOfDay, sort, and any pagination-related parameter.
- Call getEventFilterOptions to fetch dynamic filter configuration.
- Validate and normalize incoming URL parameter values against the options returned by getEventFilterOptions; map invalid or unsupported values to defaults.
- Initialize filter controls, sort control, and pagination state based on this merged configuration.
- Call searchEvents with the resolved keyword and filters, sort_by, page, and page_size, then render the results.

On filter value change (onChange within filter controls):
- Update the internal filter state for the changed control (e.g., postal code, distance, type, category, topic, date preset or dates, day of week, time of day).
- Optionally debounce changes if search should not be executed on every keystroke (particularly for keyword inputs).
- When the user submits filters or when auto-apply is triggered, rebuild the filters object and reset the page index to the first page, update the URL parameters to reflect the new filter values, and call searchEvents with updated filters and existing sort_by.

On date filter interactions:
- When a user selects a date preset, the component updates the internal state to include the selected datePreset key and clears or ignores explicit startDate and endDate values in both state and URL.
- When a user chooses explicit start and/or end dates, the component sets startDate and endDate in state and URL, and clears any datePreset field.
- Each time date-related controls change, a new searchEvents call is made (on submit or auto-apply) with the appropriate date-related filter values derived from the control state.

On sort change (onChange in sort control):
- When a user selects a new sort option, update the sort_by state to the selected option’s key.
- Update the sort parameter in the URL to the same key.
- Reset pagination to the first page and call searchEvents using existing keyword and filters, but with the updated sort_by value.
- When searchEvents returns, ensure the applied_sort value from the response is used to confirm any UI indicators of current sort.

On pagination interactions (onPageChange):
- When user chooses a different page index, set the new page index in state.
- Update the URL with the new page value.
- Call searchEvents with the current keyword, filters, sort_by, and the updated page; page_size remains constant for the session.
- Scroll or focus to the top of the results container if required by UX, without altering filters or sort.

On clicking an event card or title (onClickEventCard):
- Capture the selected event’s ID from the searchEvents.results item.
- Navigate to event_detail.html passing eventId as a URL query parameter.
- No additional API calls are made from Events & Programs for this interaction; event_detail.html is responsible for loading its own data.

On bookmarking an event (onClickBookmark):
- When the bookmark control is toggled for an event, call bookmarkEvent with the event’s ID and a source label tied to this component.
- On success, update the corresponding event item’s is_bookmarked state in the results list (either optimistically or based on the response).
- Optionally display feedback using the message returned by bookmarkEvent, without hardcoding that message content.
- If already_bookmarked is true, ensure the UI reflects a bookmarked state without creating any redundant backend entries.

On clicking 'View my events' (onClickMyEventsShortcut):
- Trigger navigation to dashboard.html without additional parameters.
- No direct API calls from this click; dashboard.html is responsible for loading the user’s saved/registered events.

On clearing filters (onClickClearFilters or onRemoveFilterChip):
- When clearing all filters, reset the filter state to defaults derived from getEventFilterOptions and remove filter-related parameters from the URL.
- When clearing a single filter (e.g., removing one active filter chip), unset the corresponding filter control state and URL parameter, while leaving other filters intact.
- Reset pagination to the first page and call searchEvents with the updated, cleared filter set while keeping the sort_by parameter as-is (unless specifically cleared).
page_functionality.state_logic: The Events & Programs page’s state is fully driven by URL parameters plus the dynamic configuration from getEventFilterOptions. On initial load, the page parses all incoming URL parameters: postalCode maps to the location postal code filter; distanceMiles maps to distance radius; type maps to the event type filter; category maps to event category; topic maps to an event topic filter; datePreset maps to a date preset selection; startDate and endDate map to explicit date range filters; dayOfWeek maps to a day-of-week filter; timeOfDay maps to a time-of-day filter; sort maps to the active sort key; and an additional pagination parameter (e.g., page) is used internally for determining offset. The page then calls getEventFilterOptions to obtain valid filter and sort choices, and normalizes any URL values against those options (e.g., ignoring or replacing values that aren’t in the returned options). The normalized values are applied to filter controls and used to construct the filters parameter for searchEvents. Any interaction that alters filters, sort, or pagination updates both the in-memory state and the URL query parameters, ensuring that a reload or shared link will reconstruct the same state. When datePreset is present and valid, datePreset is used and startDate/endDate are not sent in filters; when explicit startDate or endDate are set, they take precedence and datePreset is cleared from URL and filters. When a parameter is cleared via UI, it is removed from the URL and a new searchEvents call is made with that filter omitted. The applied_sort value returned by searchEvents can be used to validate and reflect the actual active sort state in the UI; if it differs from the requested sort_by, the UI should update to reflect applied_sort and the URL should be updated accordingly. Bookmarking events does not rely on URL parameters; it uses bookmarkEvent and the is_bookmarked value returned in searchEvents results to represent bookmark state. The dashboard navigation (View my events) does not pass filters; it simply navigates to dashboard.html and that page manages its own state.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, filter and sort state management, and orchestration of calls to getEventFilterOptions, searchEvents, and bookmarkEvent for child components.

Provides keyword, location (postal code and distance), event type, category, topic, date range (via presets or explicit dates), day-of-week, and time-of-day controls. It initializes available options using getEventFilterOptions and current selections from URL parameters, and emits consolidated filter state when users apply or change filters.

Displays the currently applied filters as removable chips or labels, based on the page controller’s filter state, and offers a global 'clear all' control. Removing a chip updates filter state and URL, triggering a new searchEvents call via the page controller.

Renders sort options retrieved from getEventFilterOptions.sort_options. The currently active sort option is derived from the sort URL parameter or applied_sort from searchEvents. Changing the selection updates the sort parameter in the URL and requests a new searchEvents call with the chosen sort key.

Displays a list of event cards based on searchEvents.results, including all key event fields. Handles empty states using total_count and page information, and passes individual event entries to event-card subcomponents.

Represents a single event in the results list. Binds to a single searchEvents.results item and displays title, subtitle, start_datetime, end_datetime, day_of_week_label, time_of_day_label, timezone, location_summary, postal_code, distance_display, type_label, category_label, topics, registration_required, remaining_spots, is_webinar, is_bookmarked, and is_registered as provided. It exposes click targets for navigating to the event detail page and a bookmark toggle.

Renders pagination UI (such as current page, total pages, and navigation controls) based on total_count, page, and page_size from searchEvents. When the user changes the page, it notifies the page controller to update the page parameter in the URL and re-call searchEvents.
Provides a direct navigation control labeled to indicate viewing the user’s events. When triggered, it navigates to dashboard.html without altering or relying on any API data on this page.

Synchronizes filter, sort, and pagination state between the UI and the URL parameters. On initialization or browser navigation events, it parses the URL and updates the page controller state. When filters, sort, or page change in the UI, it updates the URL querystring accordingly, without storing any state in localStorage.

Listens for bookmarkEvent responses from event-card interactions, interprets success, already_bookmarked, and message fields, and exposes this information to the UI (such as transient feedback) without hardcoding response messages or states.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, max-width layout based on a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters (desktop breakpoint). Content appears within a central column spanning roughly 8–10 grid columns, with generous side margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a centered, single-column editorial layout inside a full-width shell. A slim top navigation (handled elsewhere) sits at the very top. Directly beneath, the Events & Programs page-controller creates a pale green hero-like panel with the page title and a brief intro aligned to the left within a central content column. Below this text, a compact filter band (filters, active filters, sort, my-events shortcut) acts as a functional extension of the hero. Beneath the pale green panel, the main results section flows vertically as a stack of full-width event cards with pagination at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a centered, single-column editorial layout inside a full-width shell. A slim top navigation (handled elsewhere) sits at the very top. Directly beneath, the Events & Programs page-controller creates a pale green hero-like panel with the page title and a brief intro aligned to the left within a central content column. Below this text, a compact filter band (filters, active filters, sort, my-events shortcut) acts as a functional extension of the hero. Beneath the pale green panel, the main results section flows vertically as a stack of full-width event cards with pagination at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content column spans about 70–80% of the viewport width, leaving 10–15% gutters on each side on desktop. The pale green hero + filter band occupies roughly the top 50–60% of the initial viewport height, with generous top and bottom padding (48–64px) so the heading and intro feel airy. The filters and sort controls are compact but given 16–24px vertical breathing room. The event results list fills the remaining vertical space and scrolls, with each card separated by 24–32px and a 32–48px gap before pagination. Side gutters remain consistent from hero through to the bottom of the page.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally low-density: a single column of text and cards, short line lengths, and high line-height. The filter form is restrained to essential controls in one visually light band rather than a large, busy panel. Each event card is allowed room for whitespace around its contents, avoiding cramped text or overly dense metadata. Vertical spacing between sections (hero, controls, results, pagination) follows a 24–64px rhythm to preserve the calm, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top-left logo, moves across the navigation, then drops to the left-aligned 'Events & Programs' heading in the pale green hero. From there it flows down to the intro paragraph, then to the filter band immediately below. The active filters summary and right-aligned sort control guide attention from left to right along a subtle F-pattern before the user continues downward into the vertically stacked event cards. A floating bookmark feedback toast appears at the lower-right of the content area when needed, acting as a secondary focal point without competing with the main reading flow.

layout.overall_description: The Events & Programs page is framed within a full-width, very light background, with a centered content column that feels like a clean editorial article. At the top, a slim navigation bar spans edge-to-edge, while the core page content begins just below as a broad, pale green panel that stretches horizontally across the viewport. Inside this panel, the page title and a short descriptive paragraph sit on the left of the central column, with generous vertical padding above and below so the text appears calm and unhurried. Just beneath the text, the filter band runs across the same column: filters aligned left, a compact active-filters summary beneath them, and a subtle sort dropdown aligned to the right, with a quiet 'My events' shortcut occupying the upper-right area of this block. As the user scrolls past this hero area, the background transitions to the default near-white, and a single vertical stack of event cards fills the central column, each card separated by ample whitespace to evoke a magazine-like list rather than a dense directory. At the very bottom of this stack, centered pagination controls sit with comfortable top and bottom margins. On interaction, bookmark feedback appears as a small, floating toast near the lower-right of the viewport within the content column, complementing the overall layout without disrupting the vertical reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events-page-controller spans the full width of the viewport and defines the main content region beneath the global navigation. It creates a broad pale green background block from just under the nav down to slightly below the initial fold (roughly the top 50–60% of the viewport), within which the central content column is centered horizontally and capped at approximately 1100–1200px width. Inside that column, the 'Events & Programs' H1 appears top-left with about 64px of top padding from the nav and 24–32px of space above the intro paragraph, which runs multiple short lines with comfortable line-height. Below the intro paragraph, the controller positions the filter form, active filters summary, sort control, and my-events shortcut in a cohesive band, maintaining 24px vertical gaps between these elements. At the bottom edge of the pale green area, the background transitions smoothly to the base near-white page background, and the event results list begins, keeping the same column width and left alignment for continuity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container that scrolls with the page; defines the pale green hero section and central column but itself is not sticky or fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-filter-form sits immediately below the intro paragraph within the pale green section, aligned to the left edge of the central content column. It spans most of the column width (roughly 80–90% of the column), leaving some breathing room to the right for the my-events shortcut and visual balance. The form appears as a light, borderless horizontal band of key controls (e.g., date, location, type), with internal padding of about 16–24px and 24px of margin above and below. On wider screens, filters lay out in a single row where possible, wrapping to a second row only if necessary; controls are evenly spaced, but not edge-to-edge, maintaining comfortable gaps between each element. The filter area visually reads as a continuation of the hero content rather than a separate heavy module.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page, but on desktop it becomes sticky at the top of the viewport (just beneath the global nav) once the user scrolls past the hero heading, keeping filtering tools accessible while browsing results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active-filters-summary is positioned directly beneath the event-filter-form, sharing the same left alignment and central column width. It sits in a narrow band with approximately 16–24px of vertical spacing above and below, using compact pill-shaped chips or small labels to summarize selected filters. The summary content flows from left to right, wrapping to a second line if needed, but keeps the overall height modest to preserve the page’s lightness. Visually, it appears slightly subdued (smaller text, lower contrast) compared to the filters themselves, acting as a gentle status line between the filters and the results tooling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the content; when the filter form becomes sticky, the summary scrolls out of view and does not remain fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-sort-control is aligned to the right edge of the central content column, sharing a horizontal line either with the bottom of the active-filters-summary or sitting just below it with a small 8–16px offset. It occupies a compact footprint (roughly 20–25% of the column width), appearing as a minimalist dropdown or select component with a simple label. There is about 16–24px of vertical space between the sort control and the beginning of the event-results-list, ensuring the sort does not feel crowded by the first event card. This right alignment contributes to the F-pattern, drawing the eye from the left-aligned filters across to the right before dropping down into the results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page; not sticky or fixed, and remains anchored to the right side of the central column across responsive breakpoints (stacking beneath filters on smaller screens).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-results-list begins directly beneath the event-sort-control with a 24–32px vertical gap, marking a clear transition from controls to content. It occupies the full width of the central content column, maintaining the same left alignment and side gutters as the hero section but now against the near-white background. Events are presented in a single vertical column of event-card components, each separated by 24–32px of vertical space to create a relaxed, editorial-style list. The list extends downward as needed, forming the primary scrollable area of the page, and ends with a 32–48px gap before the pagination controls. The overall section feels like a continuous article of event entries rather than a dense grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally within the page; no fixed or sticky behavior, but expands vertically as more events are loaded or paginated.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each event-card is a horizontally centered block within the event-results-list, matching the width of the central content column but inset with internal padding of about 24px top/bottom and 32px left/right. The card background is white or slightly off-white, with a soft shadow or faint border and subtle corner rounding to distinguish it from the page background while remaining minimalist. Content inside is stacked vertically: the event title appears at the top-left in a larger, bold style, followed by a line of key metadata (date, time, location) with comfortable 8–12px spacing, then a short descriptive snippet with generous line-height. At the bottom-right of the card, the bookmark action (icon and/or label) is aligned horizontally with the bottom of the description, while any secondary actions sit to its left, all with 16px padding from the edges. The card height adjusts to content, but spacing ensures that no card feels cramped; each is visually separated by 24–32px of whitespace from the next card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element within the scrolling list; no special sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-pagination-controls are placed at the bottom of the event-results-list, centered within the central content column. There is a generous 32–48px gap between the last event-card and the pagination controls, allowing the user to clearly perceive the end of the current result set. The controls themselves form a low-profile horizontal row of page numbers and previous/next buttons, occupying a relatively small vertical height with 16–24px of internal padding and ample whitespace above and below. This area aligns visually with the calm, minimal aesthetic, avoiding heavy borders or large buttons so it doesn’t overpower the event cards above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page and appears only when the user reaches the bottom of the current results; not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The my-events-shortcut appears as a subtle text link or small pill-shaped button labeled along the lines of 'My saved events,' positioned within the pale green hero area. It is right-aligned within the central content column, horizontally aligned near the baseline of the H1 or slightly below it, so that the hero’s left-aligned title balances with this right-side utility link. There is at least 24px of space between this shortcut and any nearby elements (such as the top of the filter form), ensuring it remains unobtrusive and does not compete with the primary heading. On narrower screens, it can stack below the heading but maintain right alignment within the column to keep its utility, lightweight feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally with the hero section; once the hero scrolls out of view, the shortcut is no longer visible unless the user scrolls back up.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync component has no visible UI footprint; it operates behind the scenes within the bounds of the events-page-controller. Conceptually, it is attached to the filter form, sort control, pagination, and bookmark state, ensuring that changes to these elements are reflected in the browser URL and restored on reload. There are no pixels, spacing, or alignment associated with this component; it simply supports the interactive behavior of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; does not affect layout and has no spatial behavior on the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark-feedback-handler manifests as a small, temporary toast-style notification when the user bookmarks or unbookmarks an event. Visually, it appears over the content near the lower-right corner of the viewport but inset so that it aligns with the right edge of the central content column rather than the absolute screen edge, with about 24–32px margin from the bottom and right of that column. The toast has a modest width (around 280–360px), a subtle shadow, and 16–24px of internal padding, using concise text to confirm the action. It overlays above the event cards and pagination but does not cover critical controls, and it auto-dismisses after a short delay to maintain the clean, unobstructed layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport relative to the central column, floating above the scrolling content; appears only on bookmark actions and then fades out.

name: Event Detail
title: Event Detail
description: Displays full details for a specific environmental conservation event or webinar, and allows users to bookmark and register for the event.

core_features: Load and display complete event information using getEventDetails based on the eventId URL parameter.
Show formatted date, weekday, time range, timezone, event type, category, topics, and location information including distance using getEventDetails.display fields.
Display event requirements, capacity, and remaining spots, and reflect whether registration is required using getEventDetails.event fields.
Indicate whether the user has already bookmarked or registered for the event using getEventDetails.user_state.
Allow the user to bookmark/save the event to My Events by invoking bookmarkEvent with the current eventId and a source label.
Support event registration by collecting registrant_name, registrant_email, and an add_to_dashboard preference, and invoking registerForEvent.
After successful bookmarking or registration, provide navigation to the dashboard (My Events) and reflect success messages from bookmarkEvent and registerForEvent.
Provide navigation back to the events listing (events.html) while keeping the event detail addressable via the eventId URL parameter for deep links.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View event details
1) User navigates to event_detail.html with a query parameter eventId.
2) On page initialization, the page reads eventId from the URL.
3) The page calls getEventDetails(eventId).
4) When getEventDetails returns, the page populates event title, subtitle, description, type_label, category_label, topics, date_display, weekday_label, time_range_display, timezone, location_name, full_address_display, distance_display, requirements, registration_required, capacity, remaining_spots, and user_state.is_bookmarked / user_state.is_registered.
5) The user can scroll through all event information including schedule, location, distance, and requirements.

Workflow: Bookmark an event
1) The user is viewing an event on event_detail.html with valid getEventDetails data loaded.
2) The page shows a bookmark control that reflects user_state.is_bookmarked (e.g., enabled if not yet bookmarked, visually marked as saved if already bookmarked).
3) If the event is not already bookmarked, the user clicks the bookmark control.
4) The page calls bookmarkEvent with parameters eventId (from URL) and source (a constant string indicating this page, such as "event_detail_bookmark").
5) When bookmarkEvent returns success, the page updates local UI state to reflect that the event is now bookmarked (equivalent to user_state.is_bookmarked = true), optionally displays the message from the response, and reveals or enables a "View my events" navigation control that links to dashboard.html.
6) If bookmarkEvent indicates already_bookmarked, the page reflects this state without creating duplicate bookmarks and may show the corresponding message.

Workflow: Register for an event or webinar
1) The user is viewing an event where registration_required is true and (remaining_spots is either undefined or greater than 0).
2) The registration form is visible and enabled, showing fields for registrant_name, registrant_email, and a checkbox or toggle for add_to_dashboard.
3) The user fills in registrant_name and registrant_email and optionally selects add_to_dashboard.
4) The user submits the registration form.
5) The page validates that registrant_name and registrant_email are not empty (and email appears valid). If validation fails, the form shows inline errors and does not call the interface.
6) On successful validation, the page calls registerForEvent with eventId (from URL), registrant_name, registrant_email, and add_to_dashboard (boolean from the form).
7) When registerForEvent returns success, the page shows confirmation using the returned registration data and message, updates UI to reflect that the user is registered (similar to user_state.is_registered = true), may disable or hide the registration form, and displays a call-to-action link to go to dashboard.html.
8) If registerForEvent returns an error (success is false or status not registered), the page displays the message and keeps the form available for correction.

Workflow: Navigate back to event listings
1) The user is on event_detail.html.
2) The user clicks the "Back to events" navigation link.
3) The page navigates to events.html without additional parameters, relying on that page to manage its own state.
4) The event detail page can still be revisited via its deep-link URL with the same eventId.

Workflow: Navigate to My Events after action
1) After bookmarking or successfully registering for an event, the page reveals or highlights a "View my events" or similar call-to-action.
2) The user clicks this call-to-action.
3) The page navigates to dashboard.html without adding extra URL parameters.
4) The dashboard page can then surface the saved or registered event using its own data sources (e.g., SavedEvent or EventRegistration), which are indirectly populated by the bookmarkEvent and registerForEvent calls.
interactions:
Interaction: Page initialization
- Trigger: Page load.
- Behavior: Read eventId from the querystring of the current URL. If eventId is present, call getEventDetails(eventId). While waiting for the response, show a loading state. If eventId is missing or getEventDetails fails, show an error state and avoid rendering event-specific interactive controls.

Interaction: Bookmark button click
- Trigger: User clicks the bookmark/save control when user_state.is_bookmarked is false.
- Behavior: Disable the control or show a pending state, then call bookmarkEvent(eventId, source). On success, mark the event as bookmarked in page state, update visual state (e.g., label change), and display any confirmation message. On failure, re-enable the control and show an error message. If already_bookmarked is true in the response, align UI state accordingly.

Interaction: Registration form input
- Trigger: User types into registrant_name or registrant_email fields or toggles the add_to_dashboard control.
- Behavior: Update in-memory form state. Clear field-level error messages as the user corrects inputs. The add_to_dashboard control is bound to a boolean value used as the add_to_dashboard parameter on submission.

Interaction: Registration form submission
- Trigger: User clicks the registration submit button.
- Behavior: Perform client-side validation (e.g., required fields, simple email format check). If validation passes, disable the form submit button and call registerForEvent(eventId, registrant_name, registrant_email, add_to_dashboard). On success, show a success state with details from registration and message, mark the user as registered in page state, and optionally hide or disable the form. On failure, show the returned message, re-enable the form, and keep the existing input values.

Interaction: Handling full or non-registrable events
- Trigger: After getEventDetails returns, or after registration attempt updates remaining_spots.
- Behavior: If registration_required is false, hide or disable the registration form. If remaining_spots is defined and less than or equal to 0, show a non-interactive message indicating the event is full and disable or hide the registration form while still displaying other event details.

Interaction: Back to events navigation
- Trigger: User clicks a "Back to events" link or button.
- Behavior: Navigate to events.html using standard link navigation without invoking any interface on this page.

Interaction: View my events navigation
- Trigger: User clicks the "View my events" call-to-action shown after bookmarking or registration.
- Behavior: Navigate to dashboard.html using standard link navigation without additional parameters or interfaces.

Interaction: Error handling banner
- Trigger: Any error from getEventDetails, bookmarkEvent, or registerForEvent.
- Behavior: Display an error message region bound to the latest error state. Dismissing or changing inputs can clear error state in memory; no persistence in URL or localStorage is used.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary persistent state for this page is the eventId URL parameter passed in the querystring (e.g., event_detail.html?eventId={id}). On page load, the page-init logic reads eventId from the URL: if it is present, getEventDetails(eventId) is invoked; if it is missing, the page shows an error state and does not attempt to call any interfaces.

The response from getEventDetails provides three sets of data that drive page behavior:
- event: Raw event fields (Event model), including timing, location, and registration/capacity details.
- display: Preformatted strings used for date/time, address, distance, category_label, and type_label.
- user_state: Flags is_bookmarked and is_registered, used to initialize interactive controls (bookmark button availability and registration status display).

No cross-page state is stored in localStorage, cookies, or other client-side persistence; instead, cross-page effects are realized on the server side via bookmarkEvent and registerForEvent modifying SavedEvent and EventRegistration data models. When the user later visits dashboard.html, that page can query these models independently.

Within the page, transient UI state (such as form field values, loading flags, or error messages) is maintained in memory only and is reset if the page is reloaded or revisited via a new URL. If the user navigates away and returns via the same event_detail.html?eventId={id} URL, the page again calls getEventDetails to reconstruct state from authoritative data.

Navigation to events.html and dashboard.html does not depend on additional URL parameters from this page. Deep links from emails or shares simply point to event_detail.html with the appropriate eventId, and the page behaves identically, rebuilding its state solely from the query parameter and getEventDetails.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and top-level state for the event detail page. On load, parses the eventId query parameter, calls getEventDetails(eventId), stores the returned event, display, and user_state objects in memory, and exposes loading and error flags to other components.

Displays the primary identifying information for the event, including event title, subtitle, category_label, type_label, date_display, weekday_label, time_range_display, and timezone. It also reflects whether the user is already registered (user_state.is_registered) by showing a registration status indicator.
Shows the full event description and any requirements text so users can understand what the event involves and what they need to bring or prepare.

Displays supplemental information such as category_label, topics, time_of_day_label, registration_required, capacity, remaining_spots, and any other relevant meta fields from the Event and display objects. It can highlight whether registration is required and if the event is close to capacity.

Presents location-related information including location_name, full_address_display, and distance_display. For in-person events, this section makes it clear where the event takes place and how far it is. For online events/webinars, it can indicate virtual location details derived from the type and location fields.

Allows the user to bookmark/save the event to My Events. The control initializes its visual state using user_state.is_bookmarked. When clicked for non-bookmarked events, it calls bookmarkEvent(eventId, source) and updates the UI to reflect the bookmarked state upon success. If the event is already bookmarked, the button can be disabled or shown as non-interactive to prevent duplicate calls.

Collects registrant_name, registrant_email, and add_to_dashboard preference for events that are open for registration (registration_required is true and remaining_spots is not exhausted). It validates required inputs, and on submission, calls registerForEvent(eventId, registrant_name, registrant_email, add_to_dashboard). The form is hidden or disabled if registration_required is false or the event is full.

Shows the current registration state for the event. It initializes from user_state.is_registered and updates after a successful registerForEvent call using the returned registration object and message. When the user is registered, this panel confirms registration and exposes navigation to view the event in the dashboard.

Provides a "View my events" call-to-action after successful bookmarking or registration. It becomes visible when either the bookmarkToggleButton or registration-form reports success. Clicking it navigates the user to dashboard.html.
Offers a simple way to return to the event listing page. When activated, it navigates the browser to events.html without making additional API calls.

Displays error messages related to loading event details, bookmarking, or registering. It subscribes to error states produced by page-init-controller, bookmark-toggle-button, and registration-form, and surfaces the latest relevant message to the user.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, max-width layout based on a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters (desktop breakpoint). Content appears within a central column spanning roughly 8–10 grid columns, with generous side margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow with a centered main content column. At the very top sits the global site navigation (logo left, nav right – implied from the system), followed directly by any error banner. Below that, the main event content begins with a hero-like event-detail-header in a pale green panel containing the event title and key introductory details. Immediately beneath this textual hero, an event-location-section behaves like the mockup’s wide image band, providing a strong visual block. Under the visual comes the detailed narrative and facts in a single primary column: first the event description, then meta information, followed by the registration status and registration form, then a post-registration CTA, and finally a back-to-events link at the bottom. Bookmarking is handled via a compact button visually attached to the hero, echoing the small, unobtrusive floating control in the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow with a centered main content column. At the very top sits the global site navigation (logo left, nav right – implied from the system), followed directly by any error banner. Below that, the main event content begins with a hero-like event-detail-header in a pale green panel containing the event title and key introductory details. Immediately beneath this textual hero, an event-location-section behaves like the mockup’s wide image band, providing a strong visual block. Under the visual comes the detailed narrative and facts in a single primary column: first the event description, then meta information, followed by the registration status and registration form, then a post-registration CTA, and finally a back-to-events link at the bottom. Bookmarking is handled via a compact button visually attached to the hero, echoing the small, unobtrusive floating control in the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The layout uses a centered max-width content column (about 70–80% of the viewport width) with generous outer gutters on desktop (roughly 10–12% of the width on each side). The event-detail-header in its pale green panel occupies roughly the top 50–60% of the initial viewport height, with substantial vertical padding (around 48–64px) above and below the title and supporting text. The event-location-section directly beneath behaves like a wide visual strip that takes up roughly 40–50% of the viewport height on first load. Subsequent sections (description, meta info, registration) are stacked with clear section gaps of about 48–64px, while headings and their related text within each section are separated by smaller 24–32px gaps. The registration form is given card-like emphasis in the lower third of the page, spanning about 60–70% of the content column width to feel substantial but not overwhelming.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains deliberately low and editorial. Each block (header, location visual, description, meta, registration, CTA, back link) has ample breathing space. Text lines are kept reasonably short due to the central column, with generous line height and no more than a few paragraphs per section. Key UI elements (bookmark toggle, status panel, CTAs) are visually compact and do not crowd the main text. Padding within major blocks sits around 16–24px, ensuring the content has room without feeling sparse. Overall the page feels calm and spacious, prioritizing readability and reflection over dense functional controls.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F-shaped pattern similar to the mockup. The eye starts at the top-left (logo in the global nav), glances across the top navigation, then drops down to the large left-aligned event title in the event-detail-header hero. From there the eye moves down to the supporting introductory text and any key status indicator. It then continues downward to the wide visual band of the event-location-section, which anchors the middle of the page. After scanning the visual, the user flows down into the event description, then quickly over the structured meta info, and further down to the registration status panel and the registration form where action is expected. Finally, the eye rests on the post-action dashboard CTA and the subtle back-to-events link near the bottom. The bookmark toggle, positioned unobtrusively near the hero’s top-right edge, acts as a secondary focal point after the main title, similar to the accessibility button in the mockup that catches the eye late in the scan without disrupting the core reading path.

layout.overall_description: The Event Detail page presents as a calm, editorial experience centered within a wide, pale background. A slim global navigation sits at the very top, stretching full-width with the logo on the left and lightweight navigation on the right. Immediately below, if present, a full-width error banner briefly interrupts the calm, then yields to the main content. The core content lives in a centered column framed by generous side gutters, giving the page an open and breathable feel. At the top of this column, a subtle back-to-events link sits left-aligned, followed by a large, pale green hero panel that occupies roughly half the initial viewport height. Inside this panel, the event title is large and left-aligned with plenty of space above and below, followed by a few lines of key event details and introductory copy. A small bookmark toggle button anchors to the upper-right corner of the hero, present but unobtrusive. Immediately beneath the hero, a wide location section acts as a visual band—either a map or an environmental image—stretching across the content column and occupying the lower half of the initial viewport, echoing the mockup’s image treatment. Scrolling down, the user moves into a white-background narrative zone: first a cleanly spaced event description section, then a slightly more structured meta info section, both maintaining ample vertical gaps. Below these informational blocks, a registration status panel and a registration form sit centrally as a coherent call-to-action area, styled like a soft card with comfortable padding. After successful registration, a dashboard CTA appears just beneath the form, nudging users forward in their journey. At the very bottom of the content column, a quietly styled back-to-events link closes the page. The entire layout reads as a structured vertical story—from event identity, to place, to detail, to action—delivered with minimalist visuals, soft greens, and generous whitespace.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual controller that conceptually sits at the root of the page, underlying the entire viewport. It does not render any visible UI but orchestrates the loading and state of all other components. Spatially, it occupies no physical area, allowing the navigation, banners, and main content column to use the full width and height of the page without adjustment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, occupies no space; purely structural and does not affect layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-detail-header is the primary hero section of the page’s main content. It is positioned near the top of the central content column, directly below any error banner and the back-to-events link. On desktop it spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, centered with large left and right gutters. The header is contained within a tall, pale green panel that visually stretches edge-to-edge within this content column, with rounded corners subtly separating it from the page background. Vertical padding inside the panel is generous—about 48–64px top and bottom and 32px left and right—so the event title has ample breathing room. The event title appears as a large, left-aligned H1 occupying the upper third of the panel, with high line height and limited line length to echo the editorial style.
