Immediately beneath, in the middle third, sits a concise cluster of supporting text such as the date, time, and a short summary, also left-aligned and allowed to wrap across multiple short lines. The lower third of the panel is mostly calm whitespace with only subtle elements (such as tags or a faint divider if used), reinforcing the spacious feel. The bookmark-toggle-button aligns to the upper-right corner of this panel, visually anchored to it. Below the panel’s bottom edge, there is a clear section gap of about 48px before the event-location-section begins, mirroring the hero-then-image structure of the source mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the main scroll; it scrolls with the page and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-description-section is placed in the main content column below the event-location-section. There is a generous vertical gap of approximately 48–64px between the bottom edge of the location visual and the top of this section, preserving the low-density editorial feel. It spans the full width of the central column (about 70–80% of the viewport), remaining centered with the same wide outer gutters as the hero. The section itself sits on a plain, very light background (typically white) with no strong border, keeping it visually subordinate to the pale green hero. At the top of the section, a left-aligned subheading or section title (e.g., “About this event”) appears with a 24px gap beneath it before the body text begins. The description paragraphs are stacked vertically, each separated by 16–24px, and kept relatively short in width thanks to the central column, creating comfortable, highly readable text blocks. The bottom of this section leaves a 24–32px gap before the event-meta-info, signaling that the meta content is related but structurally distinct.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the vertical flow; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-meta-info section follows immediately below the event-description-section within the same central column, separated by a modest vertical gap of around 24–32px to indicate close relationship. It spans the same width as the description, with left alignment for all labels and values. Visually it reads like a concise, structured panel of facts—such as date, time, audience, organizer—with either a simple single-column stack or a loose 2-column grid, but always keeping ample horizontal padding so the text remains light and uncluttered. The section may include a faint horizontal divider line at its top or bottom to delineate it subtly from the neighboring sections without adding heaviness. Vertical spacing between individual meta rows is about 8–16px, keeping the density slightly higher than the narrative description but still comfortable. The bottom of this section introduces a larger 48–64px gap before the registration-status-panel and registration-form, signaling a shift from informational content to action-oriented content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline section that scrolls normally with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-location-section sits directly below the event-detail-header hero, echoing the mockup’s large environmental image band. It fills the width of the central content column and can visually bleed almost edge-to-edge within that column, with only minimal horizontal padding. Vertically, it occupies a noticeable portion of the viewport—roughly 40–50% of the initial screen height—giving it the presence of a wide landscape photo or map. The top of this section sits about 24–32px below the bottom edge of the hero panel, creating a tight visual connection between the introductory text and the location visual. If it contains a map or image, that visual is centered, maintaining the calm, nature-oriented aesthetic, with any textual annotations (address, “Online event” label, map legend) sitting either overlaid in a subtle corner or aligned underneath within the same section using 16–24px padding. The bottom of the location section aligns vertically with the content column and introduces a 48–64px gap before the event-description-section, mirroring the mockup’s transition from image to body content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static visual band within the scroll; does not fix or parallax by default, though it spans a larger vertical slice for emphasis.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark-toggle-button is visually attached to the event-detail-header hero, occupying a small footprint similar to the accessibility button’s role in the mockup. On desktop it is positioned in the upper-right corner of the pale green hero panel, within the central content column. The button sits about 24–32px inset from the hero’s top and right edges, ensuring it does not feel cramped. It may appear as a compact pill or icon button (roughly 40–48px tall) with a simple bookmark icon and an optional short label. Its size and position make it easy to discover without competing with the main event title; the eye naturally notices it after reading the heading and introductory text. On scroll, it moves with the hero; once the hero has passed, the button scrolls out of view, reinforcing that it is contextual to this event view rather than a global control.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored relative to the event-detail-header; it is static and scrolls away with the hero section, not floating in the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-form is placed in the lower half of the page within the central content column, beneath the event-meta-info and registration-status-panel, serving as a primary action block. There is a 16–24px gap below the registration-status-panel, after which the form begins. It appears as a softly emphasized card spanning about 60–70% of the content column width, centered horizontally within that column to maintain visual balance and reduce line length. The card sits on a white or very light background with a subtle border or shadow, and has internal padding of about 24–32px on all sides, providing a calm, spacious feel around each form field. Form fields are stacked vertically, each separated by 16–24px, with labels left-aligned and plenty of vertical breathing room to keep the density low. The primary submit button is located at the bottom of the form, left-aligned or centered within the card, with a 24px top margin from the last input, standing out slightly but still respecting the minimalist aesthetic. Below the card, there is a clear 32–48px gap before the post-action-dashboard-cta (when present), so the form feels like a distinct unit of interaction.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card within the main scroll flow; no sticky behavior, but its centered card-like presentation makes it a clear focal point in the lower part of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-status-panel appears in the main content column directly above the registration-form, functioning as a compact status indicator and contextual lead-in to the form. It aligns left within the same central column, matching the width of the registration-form card (around 60–70% of the content column) to create a unified stack. There is a generous gap of about 48–64px above it from the event-meta-info, signaling a transition from information to action. The panel itself is low-profile—typically a horizontal bar or small card 48–64px tall—with a soft background tint that contrasts gently with the page (e.g., a light green or neutral tone) and rounded corners. Internal padding is about 16–24px, and text is left-aligned, describing the current registration state (e.g., “You are registered” or “Spots available”). Any icon is placed to the left, with text to the right, but the panel remains visually lighter than the hero and location sections. The bottom margin between this status panel and the top of the registration-form is about 16–24px, making them feel closely connected without touching.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline panel; it scrolls with surrounding content and remains tethered to the registration-form section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The post-action-dashboard-cta is positioned directly below the registration-form in the central content column, appearing only after a successful registration or similar primary action. It aligns left with the registration-form card and registration-status-panel, and typically matches the form’s width (about 60–70% of the content column). There is a vertical gap of roughly 32–48px above it, so it feels like a follow-on step rather than part of the form itself. Visually, it presents as a clean, horizontally oriented call-to-action: a concise line of text with a button or link-style button leading to a dashboard or account area. The CTA keeps a low-density layout, with 16–24px internal padding if carded, or simply occupying a single line of text plus button in a minimalist style. Below this CTA, a larger gap of around 48px leads into the final back-to-events-link, clearly marking the end of the main flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline CTA that appears within the scroll at the end of the interaction section; it does not float or stick but becomes visible when appropriate.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-events-link appears in two roles visually but as a single component in the layout: its primary placement is at the top of the main content column, just below any error banner and above the event-detail-header hero. There, it is left-aligned and given a modest top margin (about 24–32px below the nav or banner) and bottom margin (16–24px above the hero), styled as a light-weight text link with an optional back arrow icon. This placement provides an immediate way to return to the event list without drawing attention away from the hero. Optionally, the same link style may be repeated at the very bottom of the page for convenience, aligned left with a generous 48px gap above, though visually it remains subtle and understated. In both positions, the link keeps to the editorial tone: small, unobtrusive, and clearly secondary to the page’s main narrative and actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline text link; it scrolls with the page and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-detail-error-banner sits immediately below the global navigation and above all other main content, spanning the full available width of the viewport. It appears as a horizontal strip that runs from edge to edge, with a noticeable but not harsh background color (e.g., a soft red or amber) and a height determined by the message content—typically around 48–64px. Inside the banner, content aligns with the central column but the color band itself is full-width, matching the behavior seen in many modern editorial layouts to ensure the message cannot be missed. Text is left-aligned with 16–24px padding on all sides, and any icon appears to the left of the text. When visible, this banner pushes all other content downward, meaning the back-to-events-link and event-detail-header hero start just below it. Once dismissed or resolved, the banner disappears and the main content shifts up, maintaining the same generous spacing between nav and hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width band at the top of the content area; it occupies space in the normal flow and does not overlay other content.

name: Action Planner
title: Action Planner
description: Interactive page for browsing conservation actions, filtering by cost and impact, and building a personalized 5‑action conservation plan that can be named and saved.

core_features: Initialize available filter ranges (cost, impact), categories, tags, and sort options by calling getActionFilterOptions on page load.
Display a browsable, paginated action library using searchActions, driven by filters, sort selection, and URL parameters.
Filter actions by estimated monthly cost (with minimum and maximum inputs/slider) and impact score, and optionally by categories and tags from getActionFilterOptions.
Sort actions according to the sort_options returned by getActionFilterOptions, passing the chosen sort key to searchActions.
Add an action to the active action plan using addActionToActiveActionPlan, respecting the 5‑action limit before triggering the call.
Remove an action from the active plan using removeActionFromActiveActionPlan, updating both the plan overview and the action list.
Show the active action plan overview (name, description, actions, and summary metrics) using getActiveActionPlanOverview.
Allow users to edit and save the active plan’s name and description via saveActiveActionPlan.
Enforce and communicate the 5‑action plan limit using summary.total_actions from getActiveActionPlanOverview before allowing additional actions to be added.
Provide a “View in My Dashboard” control in the plan overview that navigates to dashboard.html while preserving relevant planner state via URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Initial load and state setup:
1. User navigates to action_planner.html, possibly with URL parameters for filters, sort, and pagination.
2. The page calls getActionFilterOptions to retrieve cost_range, impact_score_range, categories, tags, and sort_options.
3. The page parses URL parameters (e.g., costMin, costMax, impactMin, impactMax, category, tags, sort, page, pageSize) and normalizes them against the ranges and options returned by getActionFilterOptions.
4. The page calls getActiveActionPlanOverview to load the current active plan, its items, and summary metrics.
5. Using the derived filters, sort, and pagination, the page calls searchActions to populate the action library, ensuring that is_in_current_plan reflects the current active plan.

Adjust filters and browse actions:
1. User changes the maximum or minimum cost filter using a slider or numeric inputs; impact score, category, or tag filters can also be changed.
2. The filter component updates the URL parameters to reflect the new filter values (e.g., costMin, costMax, impactMin, impactMax, category, tags) using the browser history API.
3. The filter component triggers searchActions with the updated filters, preserving existing sort, page, and pageSize values from the URL.
4. The action list updates to show the filtered results, including updated total_count and pagination state.
5. User can change sort selection; the sort parameter in the URL is updated, and searchActions is called again with the same filters but new sort_by value.

Paginate through actions:
1. User clicks a pagination control to move to a different page of actions.
2. The pagination handler updates the page URL parameter (and optionally pageSize) to the selected page.
3. The handler calls searchActions with the current filters, sort_by, and updated page/page_size.
4. The action list refreshes with the new page of results, and pagination controls reflect the current page.

Add actions to the plan (respecting 5‑action limit):
1. In the action list, the user clicks an “add to plan” control for a specific action that is not currently in the plan (is_in_current_plan is false).
2. Before calling addActionToActiveActionPlan, the page checks the latest summary.total_actions from getActiveActionPlanOverview. If total_actions is at the 5‑action limit, the page shows a message explaining that no more actions can be added and does not call the API.
3. If the plan is below the limit, the page calls addActionToActiveActionPlan with the selected actionId.
4. On a successful response, the page refreshes the active plan data by calling getActiveActionPlanOverview.
5. To keep is_in_current_plan in sync, the page re-calls searchActions with the current filters, sort_by, page, and page_size, or locally updates the corresponding list item’s is_in_current_plan flag.
6. The plan overview updates to show the new number of actions, updated total_estimated_monthly_cost, and average_impact_score.

Remove actions from the plan:
1. User clicks a “remove from plan” control either in the action list (where is_in_current_plan is true) or in the plan items list.
2. The page calls removeActionFromActiveActionPlan with the actionId.
3. On success, the page calls getActiveActionPlanOverview again to refresh the list of items and summary metrics.
4. The page then either re-calls searchActions or locally updates the relevant action’s is_in_current_plan flag so that the action list UI reflects that the action is no longer in the plan.
5. The plan overview now shows the reduced number of actions and updated summary metrics.

Name and save/update the action plan:
1. In the plan overview area, the user edits the plan’s name and optional description in input fields pre-filled from getActiveActionPlanOverview.plan.
2. The user submits the form (e.g., clicks a Save Plan button).
3. The page calls saveActiveActionPlan with the current values of name and description from the form.
4. On success, the component updates the displayed plan name and description from the returned plan object and updates any last-saved timestamp shown in the UI using plan.updated_at.
5. The page may show a confirmation message indicating that the active plan has been saved.

Navigate to dashboard to view the plan:
1. User clicks the “View in My Dashboard” control in the plan overview.
2. The click handler constructs a navigation URL to dashboard.html and may append read-only context parameters (for example, a flag indicating that the dashboard should highlight the active plan) as query parameters.
3. The browser navigates to dashboard.html; the Action Planner page does not itself call any additional interfaces as part of this navigation.
interactions: On page load, automatically call getActionFilterOptions and getActiveActionPlanOverview, then parse URL parameters and call searchActions with derived filters, sort, and pagination.
When the user changes the cost range input or slider, update the costMin and costMax URL parameters and refresh action results via searchActions.
When the user changes the impact score range inputs or control, update impactMin and impactMax in the URL and refresh action results via searchActions.
When the user selects or deselects categories or tags, update category and tags URL parameters (supporting multiple values), then re-call searchActions.
When the user selects a sort option, update the sort URL parameter with the corresponding sort_options.key and call searchActions with this sort_by value.
When the user interacts with pagination controls (next/previous page or specific page number), update the page (and optionally pageSize) URL parameters and re-call searchActions.

When the user clicks an add-to-plan control for an action, check the current plan summary.total_actions; if below the 5‑action limit, call addActionToActiveActionPlan with the actionId, then refresh the plan overview and action list.
When the user clicks a remove-from-plan control, call removeActionFromActiveActionPlan with the actionId, then refresh the plan overview and action list.
When the user edits plan name or description and submits the form, call saveActiveActionPlan with the entered name and description, then update the displayed plan information from the response.
When any API call fails, display an error message in a shared notification area, allowing the user to dismiss it; do not modify URL parameters unless the error results from invalid URL state.
When the user clicks “View in My Dashboard”, navigate the browser to dashboard.html, optionally adding query parameters that reflect current planner context.

When the URL changes (for example, via back/forward navigation), re-parse the URL parameters and re-trigger searchActions (and optionally getActiveActionPlanOverview if needed) to keep the UI in sync with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Action Planner uses URL query parameters as the primary external state, combined with in-memory state derived from the assigned interfaces. No persistent client storage (such as localStorage) is used.

URL parameters:
- costMin, costMax: Represent the current minimum and maximum estimated monthly cost filters. On load, the page reads these values and clamps them to the numeric bounds defined by getActionFilterOptions.cost_range.min and cost_range.max. When the user adjusts cost filters, the page updates these parameters and re-calls searchActions.
- impactMin, impactMax: Represent the current minimum and maximum impact score filters. On load, they are clamped to getActionFilterOptions.impact_score_range.min and impact_score_range.max. Changes in the impact controls update these parameters and trigger a new searchActions call.
- category: Represents selected action categories. Multiple categories can be represented either by repeating the parameter or by a delimiter-based convention; the parsed set is passed into searchActions.filters. When the user changes category selections, the URL is updated to match the selected set.
- tags: Represents selected tags in the same manner as category. Parsed tags are passed into searchActions.filters.
- sort: Stores the selected sort key, matching one of the sort_options.key values returned by getActionFilterOptions. On load, if sort is missing or invalid, the UI selects a default sort based on getActionFilterOptions (e.g., the first available option). Changes to sort update this parameter and trigger a new searchActions call.
- page, pageSize: Control pagination. On load, if page or pageSize are missing or invalid, the UI chooses safe defaults without encoding specific numeric values into the design. Pagination interactions update these parameters and trigger new searchActions calls.
- Optional context parameters for navigation to dashboard (e.g., source=action_planner) may be appended when navigating to dashboard.html but are treated as read-only context and not used to modify planner logic.

State derivation:
- The current filter state object used by searchActions is derived by combining normalized URL parameters with the ranges and options from getActionFilterOptions; no default business values are hardcoded beyond constraints specified by the product (such as the 5‑action limit).
- The active plan state (plan metadata, items, and summary) is fully derived from getActiveActionPlanOverview and refreshed after any successful addActionToActiveActionPlan, removeActionFromActiveActionPlan, or saveActiveActionPlan call.
- The is_in_current_plan flag for each action result is taken from the searchActions response. After modifications to the plan, the UI synchronizes this flag either by re-calling searchActions with current URL-derived arguments or by minimally adjusting affected items based on the known added/removed actionId.
- Browser navigation events (back/forward) that change the URL cause the page to re-parse parameters and re-call searchActions with the updated query, ensuring the UI always reflects URL state.
- The 5‑action plan limit is enforced in the UI by consulting summary.total_actions from getActiveActionPlanOverview before making addActionToActiveActionPlan calls and by presenting the remaining capacity to the user; the limit itself is part of the business rule, not derived from API data.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and state synchronization with the URL. On page load, calls getActionFilterOptions and getActiveActionPlanOverview, derives the filter/sort/pagination state from URL parameters, and then calls searchActions. Also listens for URL changes (e.g., back/forward navigation) and re-issues searchActions with updated arguments.

Displays controls to filter actions by estimated monthly cost, impact score, categories, and tags, as well as a sort selector. Uses values and options from getActionFilterOptions. Any change updates the URL query parameters and triggers searchActions through the state manager.

Optional text-based search input that augments filtering (if supported via searchActions.filters). Captures user-entered search text and updates a corresponding URL parameter (e.g., query), then delegates to the state manager to call searchActions with the updated filters.

Renders the list of actions returned by searchActions, including name, short_description, estimated_monthly_cost, impact_score, category, tags, and an indicator or control reflecting is_in_current_plan. Provides add/remove controls for integrating actions into the active plan and pagination controls for traversing pages.

Shows a high-level summary of the active action plan, including the plan name, optional description, status, created_at, updated_at, number of actions, total_estimated_monthly_cost, and average_impact_score. Includes a control for navigating to dashboard.html to view the plan in the broader dashboard context.

Provides editable fields for the plan’s name and description, initialized from getActiveActionPlanOverview.plan. On submission, calls saveActiveActionPlan with the current form values and updates the displayed plan information based on the response.

Displays all actions currently in the active plan using the items array from getActiveActionPlanOverview, including each action’s name, short_description, estimated_monthly_cost, impact_score, category, and any existing notes. Provides remove controls to allow users to remove an action from the plan.

Communicates the 5‑action limit to the user and shows how many actions are currently in the plan versus the maximum. Before addActionToActiveActionPlan is called, this component (or its logic) checks getActiveActionPlanOverview.summary.total_actions; if the limit is reached, it blocks the addition and prompts the user accordingly.

Displays success, warning, or error messages related to calls to getActionFilterOptions, searchActions, addActionToActiveActionPlan, removeActionFromActiveActionPlan, getActiveActionPlanOverview, and saveActiveActionPlan. Messages may include API-provided texts, such as the message field in addActionToActiveActionPlan or removeActionFromActiveActionPlan responses.

Responsible for reading filters, sort, and pagination options from the URL and writing back any user changes to keep the URL synchronized with UI state. Delegates to action-planner-init and action-filter-panel to trigger searchActions when URL changes.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, max-width layout based on a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters (desktop breakpoint). Content appears within a central column spanning roughly 8–10 grid columns, with generous side margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow anchored in a centered max-width content column (about 72–80% of viewport width, on a 12-column grid). A slim global navigation bar sits at the very top (logo left, nav right, not part of these components). Below it, a spacious hero band introduces the Action Planner with a large heading and short descriptive text. The main interactive tools follow in two major sections: first, a 'Browse actions' section with search, filters, and results; second, a 'Your plan' section where the user names and reviews their 5-action plan. Each section reads as a distinct horizontal band, with the plan section visually echoing the hero to retain the editorial feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow anchored in a centered max-width content column (about 72–80% of viewport width, on a 12-column grid). A slim global navigation bar sits at the very top (logo left, nav right, not part of these components). Below it, a spacious hero band introduces the Action Planner with a large heading and short descriptive text. The main interactive tools follow in two major sections: first, a 'Browse actions' section with search, filters, and results; second, a 'Your plan' section where the user names and reviews their 5-action plan. Each section reads as a distinct horizontal band, with the plan section visually echoing the hero to retain the editorial feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero section occupies roughly the top 50–60% of the initial viewport height, with the heading and description centered in the main column and generous top/bottom padding (48–64px). The 'Browse actions' section sits below with a slightly shorter vertical footprint initially but can expand with results. Within this section, the left 30% of the content column is reserved for filters and the right 70% for results. The 'Your plan' section stretches across the same central width as the hero and uses a pale green background, with around 48–64px padding above and below the content. Vertical separation between sections is consistently 48–64px, while gaps between related elements (heading → paragraph, form → list) follow a 24–32px rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is kept low, mirroring the mockup’s editorial feel. Each major block (notification, hero intro, search, filters, results, plan builder) has generous whitespace around it; no area is overloaded with controls. Text lines are relatively short due to the central column and generous line height. Interactive components like the filter panel and results list are visually broken into calm, card-like groupings with 16–24px internal padding and 24–32px gaps between items, preventing the tools from feeling like a dense dashboard.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F-pattern. The eye moves from the global nav bar across the top, then down to the left-aligned 'Action Planner' hero heading and its descriptive paragraph. From there, the eye travels down to the centered search form at the top of the 'Browse actions' section, then down-left to the filter panel and across to the first row of actions in the results list. Scrolling continues vertically through the results until the eye reaches the 'Your plan' band, where the plan heading, name/description form, and plan overview panel form a secondary focal zone. The notification area, when present, appears just below the navigation, briefly catching the eye before the hero content.

layout.overall_description: On desktop, the Action Planner page is framed by wide, soft side gutters so that all content sits in a centered column that never feels cramped. At the very top is a minimal global navigation bar, after which a calm, pale green hero band fills over half of the initial viewport. In this hero, the 'Action Planner' heading is large and left-aligned, with a short, multi-line paragraph beneath it explaining how to browse actions and build a 5-action conservation plan. Plenty of space above and below this text lets it breathe. Scrolling reveals the main interaction zone: a white-background 'Browse actions' section where a wide, centered search bar sits at the top, followed by a two-column layout—filters in a narrower column on the left, and a spacious, vertically scrolling list of actions on the right. Each action appears as a well-spaced card, and the overall feel is more like an editorial listing than a dense app interface. Below this, a second pale green band mirrors the hero’s visual weight and houses the plan-building tools: a small heading and helper text, the plan name/description form, and a large plan overview panel containing the selected actions list and limit indicator. This final band visually anchors the experience, making the plan feel like a composed outcome of the browsing above. The entire page maintains generous spacing, soft tones, and clear vertical rhythm, so that despite the interactive complexity, it retains a calm, thoughtful, conservation-focused atmosphere.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state-manager component has no visual footprint on the page. It is conceptually attached to the main content wrapper that sits below the global navigation bar. It initializes the data and interactions for all other Action Planner components but does not render any visible element, occupy any width or height, or affect spacing. It functions behind the scenes within the bounds of the central content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, global controller tied to the page root; does not affect layout or scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification area is positioned directly beneath the global navigation bar and above the hero section, aligned with the same central max-width column. When visible, it appears as a slim, full-width (within the content column) banner or card—about 80–90% of the central column’s width—centered horizontally. It has 16–24px vertical padding and 24–32px of space above and below it, gently pushing the hero band downward without overlapping it. The background is a subtle variation of the page’s light tones (for example, a very pale green or neutral) so it is noticeable but not aggressive, with text left-aligned and ample line spacing. When no messages are present, this area collapses entirely, leaving the hero section immediately below the nav.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, non-sticky element that appears and disappears without affecting the fixed global navigation; it scrolls away with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form sits at the top of the 'Browse actions' section, which appears just below the hero. It is centered within the main content column and spans roughly 70–80% of that column’s width, leaving comfortable margins on either side. There is a vertical gap of about 48–64px between the bottom of the hero section and the top edge of the search form. Above the form, there may be a small, left-aligned section label like 'Browse actions' with 16–24px spacing to the form itself. The search field(s) are laid out horizontally if space allows, but the overall block remains compact, with 16–24px padding inside and around 24px of space below before the filter/results columns begin. This generous spacing preserves the editorial rhythm while clearly marking the search form as the entry point into the interactive section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the main flow; it stays fixed in its section and scrolls away naturally as the user moves down to filters and results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter panel appears directly below the search form within the 'Browse actions' section, occupying the left portion of a two-column layout. It is aligned to the left edge of the central content column and spans roughly 30–32% of that column’s width on desktop. The top of the filter panel aligns vertically with the first row of the results list to its right. The panel itself is a tall, card-like container with a white or very light background, subtle border or shadow, and internal padding of about 24px top/bottom and 24–32px left/right. Filter controls are stacked vertically with 16–24px gaps between groups, and the panel extends downward as needed, but remains visually light with ample whitespace. There is a lateral gap of 32–40px between the right edge of the filter panel and the left edge of the results list, preserving breathing room between the columns.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the panel can behave as a sticky column: it remains fixed to the top of the viewport once scrolled into place until the bottom of the 'Browse actions' section is reached, making filters consistently accessible. On smaller screens, it stacks above the results list and scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list is positioned to the right of the filter panel within the 'Browse actions' section, forming the wider column of the two-column layout. It starts at the same vertical position as the top of the filter panel and spans roughly 68–70% of the central content column’s width. The left edge of the first result aligns with the right edge of the filter panel, separated by a 32–40px gutter. Each action result appears as an individual, card-like block extending almost the full width of this right column, with 16–24px internal padding and 24–32px vertical gaps between consecutive cards. The list flows vertically and can extend well beyond the first viewport, with the page scrolling to reveal more items. The typography and spacing within each result card follow the editorial style: left-aligned text, generous line height, and clear hierarchy for action name, impact, and cost. The overall columns maintain a calm, low-density appearance despite potentially many items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, scrollable content within the main page flow; the list itself scrolls with the page. On smaller screens, it stacks below the filter panel and expands to nearly the full width of the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan overview panel resides in its own dedicated 'Your plan' section below the 'Browse actions' area, echoing the hero’s editorial style. This section is a wide, pale green band spanning the full width of the central content column with generous vertical padding (about 48–64px top and bottom). Within this band, the plan overview panel sits centered horizontally and spans roughly 80–90% of the content column’s width, leaving visible pale green margins at the sides. It appears as a prominent card with either a slightly lighter or white background to distinguish it from the band, featuring 24–32px internal padding. Vertically, the panel occupies much of the lower half of this section; above it sit the plan heading and form elements. At the top of the panel, there is a small header row summarizing the plan (e.g., 'Your 5 actions') and possibly showing a count, while the remainder of the panel is dedicated to the plan-items list. Overall, the panel has a calm, substantial presence, similar in visual weight to the image block in the original mockup, serving as the key visual anchor for the plan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block in its own section, centered and non-sticky. It scrolls into view only after the user moves past the search/filters/results section, reinforcing a sense of progression from exploration to composed plan.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan name and description form is positioned within the same pale green 'Your plan' band, above the plan overview panel. It is left-aligned within the central content column and sits just below a small section heading such as 'Your plan' or 'Name your 5-action plan.' There is about 16–24px of space between the section heading and the top of the form. The form itself spans roughly 60–70% of the content column’s width on desktop, leaving open space to the right to keep the layout airy. Form fields (e.g., plan name and optional description) are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing between them. The bottom of the form has 16–24px vertical space before the plan-limit-indicator, and an additional 24–32px gap before the top edge of the plan overview panel below, maintaining a clear vertical rhythm and preventing the section from feeling like a dense form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, non-sticky form block; it scrolls with the rest of the 'Your plan' section and remains immediately above the plan overview panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan items list is contained entirely within the plan overview panel. Inside that panel, it occupies the main vertical space below the panel’s header area. The list is left-aligned and extends nearly the full internal width of the panel, with 16–24px padding between the list edges and the panel border. Each selected action appears as a horizontal row or small card with ample vertical spacing (16–24px) between items. When fewer than five actions are present, the list shows only the existing items with empty space below, visually reinforcing the sparse, editorial feel rather than filling space with heavy UI. When no actions are selected, a single, centered placeholder message occupies the list area, maintaining generous padding around it. The list never feels cramped: even at the full limit of five items, the rows are clearly separated, with optional soft dividers or subtle backgrounds to maintain clarity without visual noise.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline subcomponent within the plan overview panel; it scrolls only as part of that panel and is never independently scrollable. Its height expands naturally as items are added, pushing the bottom edge of the panel downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan limit indicator is a small informational element that sits between the plan-name-description-form and the plan overview panel within the 'Your plan' section. It is left-aligned with the form fields and appears as either a compact line of text or a subtle pill/label, indicating the 5-action limit. It occupies minimal vertical space—about the height of a single text line plus 4–8px padding if styled as a pill—and has around 8–16px of space above (from the form) and 16–24px of space below before the plan overview panel begins. Its typography is modest and slightly softer in tone, ensuring it supports rather than competes with the main headings, but the placement makes it easy to notice when naming or editing the plan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline helper element; it scrolls with the 'Your plan' section and stays visually tied to the form and the plan overview through close vertical proximity.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state-manager component is non-visual and does not manifest as any visible UI element on the page. It is conceptually attached to the overall Action Planner content area to keep filters, search state, selected actions, and plan details in sync with the URL. It does not consume any visible width or height and does not alter the column layout or spacing of other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, global controller operating at the page level; has no impact on the spatial arrangement of visible components.

name: Carbon Footprint Calculator
title: Carbon Footprint Calculator
description: Page for entering household information to calculate a baseline annual carbon footprint, review the results, set a named reduction goal, and access any existing goal and latest assessment.

core_features: Load the latest carbon assessment and any active carbon reduction goal using getCarbonCalculatorSummary when the page is opened or the URL changes.
Provide a household information form that collects all parameters required by calculateCarbonFootprint and submits them to create a new CarbonAssessment and receive a results summary.
Display the baseline annual emissions, breakdown by category, and a comparison summary using the results from calculateCarbonFootprint.
Allow the user to define a carbon reduction goal (percentage and name) for the most recent assessment when can_set_reduction_goal is true, and save it using setCarbonReductionGoalForLatestAssessment.

Show the currently active carbon reduction goal (if any) associated with the latest assessment, including baseline and target emissions, using data from getCarbonCalculatorSummary or from the latest goal creation response.
Provide a control to navigate to dashboard.html to view the saved goal in the user dashboard, optionally including goal-related URL parameters for context.
Synchronize the current assessment and goal context with URL parameters so that reloading or sharing the URL restores the correct page state without using localStorage.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: First-time user calculates footprint and sets a goal
1. User navigates to carbon_calculator.html without URL parameters.
2. On page load, the page calls getCarbonCalculatorSummary. If no latest_assessment is returned, the results and goal panels remain empty.
3. User fills out the household information form with all available inputs required by calculateCarbonFootprint.
4. User submits the form. The page validates required fields and, on success, calls calculateCarbonFootprint with the entered values.
5. When calculateCarbonFootprint returns, the page displays baseline_annual_emissions_tons_co2e, emissions_by_category, and comparison_to_average in the results panel. The newly created assessment.id is treated as the current assessment, and the URL is updated to include an assessment_id parameter tied to this ID.
6. If can_set_reduction_goal is true, the reduction goal form becomes enabled. The form uses the returned baseline_annual_emissions_tons_co2e as the baseline context.
7. User enters a reduction_percentage and a goal name in the reduction goal form.
8. User submits the goal form. The page calls setCarbonReductionGoalForLatestAssessment with the entered reduction_percentage and name.
9. On successful response, the page displays the saved goal details (name, reduction_percentage, baseline_emissions_tons_co2e, target_emissions_tons_co2e, status) in the active goal summary panel. The URL is updated to include a goal_id parameter.
10. User clicks the "View this goal in My Dashboard" control, which navigates to dashboard.html, optionally passing the goal_id and/or a source indicator via URL parameters.

Workflow 2: Returning user viewing existing assessment and goal
1. User navigates to carbon_calculator.html, potentially with assessment_id and/or goal_id URL parameters.
2. On page load, the page calls getCarbonCalculatorSummary to obtain latest_assessment and active_goal.
3. If latest_assessment is returned, the page uses its baseline_annual_emissions_tons_co2e to populate the results panel and may optionally prefill the household information form with the known input fields (household_size, bedrooms, weekly_driving_miles, vehicle_fuel_type, monthly_electricity_kwh, heating_type, monthly_natural_gas_therms) if those values are available from the assessment.
4. If an active_goal is returned, the active goal summary panel displays its details. If a goal_id URL parameter is present and matches active_goal.id, the page treats this goal as the focused goal (for example, by ensuring the goal section is visible).
5. If the user wants a new assessment, they can edit the household form and submit it. The page calls calculateCarbonFootprint again, receives a new assessment and results_summary, and updates the current assessment context and URL assessment_id parameter.
6. After recalculation, the user can optionally create a new reduction goal for the latest assessment through the same goal form and saving workflow.

Workflow 3: Deep linking and restoring state via URL parameters
1. Another page (such as the dashboard) links to carbon_calculator.html with URL parameters (e.g., assessment_id, goal_id, and a view parameter indicating whether to focus on the form or the results).
2. On page load, carbon_calculator.html reads the URL parameters and then calls getCarbonCalculatorSummary.
3. If latest_assessment is present and its id matches the assessment_id parameter, the page confirms that the URL context aligns with the actual latest assessment and shows its results. If the IDs do not match (for example, if a newer assessment was created elsewhere), the page treats the latest_assessment returned from getCarbonCalculatorSummary as authoritative, updates the UI to reflect this assessment, and may adjust the URL parameters to match.
4. If active_goal is present and its id matches the goal_id parameter, the page focuses the active goal summary panel. If there is no match, the page still displays active_goal (if any) and can update the URL to reflect the actual active_goal.id.
5. The view parameter, if present, is used to determine which section should be emphasized on load (e.g., household form vs. results vs. goal summary) without changing any persisted data.
6. At any time, if the user recalculates or saves a goal, the URL parameters are updated to reflect the new assessment_id and/or goal_id so that the state can be restored on subsequent visits.
interactions: User edits household input fields (household_size, bedrooms, weekly_driving_miles, vehicle_fuel_type, monthly_electricity_kwh, heating_type, monthly_natural_gas_therms); the form captures and validates the values in preparation for a calculateCarbonFootprint call.

User submits the household form to trigger calculateCarbonFootprint; on successful response, the UI updates the results panel with baseline_annual_emissions_tons_co2e, emissions_by_category, and comparison_to_average, and updates the URL with the new assessment_id.

User may adjust household inputs after seeing results and resubmit the form to generate an updated assessment via calculateCarbonFootprint, replacing the current assessment context and updating the URL accordingly.

When can_set_reduction_goal is true, user interacts with the reduction goal controls (e.g., a percentage input and text input for the goal name); as the user changes the percentage, the UI can compute and display a preview target emissions value derived from the baseline emissions of the current assessment.

User submits the reduction goal form, triggering setCarbonReductionGoalForLatestAssessment; on success, the UI shows the saved goal (name, reduction_percentage, baseline_emissions_tons_co2e, target_emissions_tons_co2e, status), stores the goal context in page state, and updates the URL with goal_id.

If setCarbonReductionGoalForLatestAssessment returns an error or success is false, the page displays an error message associated with the goal form and does not update the goal summary or URL parameters.

User clicks the "View this goal in My Dashboard" control in the active goal summary panel; the page navigates to dashboard.html and can include URL parameters such as goal_id and a referrer indicator to preserve context.

User can reload or share the URL; on load, the page reads URL parameters (such as assessment_id, goal_id, mode, and view) and calls getCarbonCalculatorSummary to reconstruct the correct UI state without relying on localStorage.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page derives all persistent state from the assigned interfaces and the URL query parameters, explicitly avoiding localStorage or other browser storage mechanisms. The following URL parameters control behavior and are updated dynamically through history/state changes:

- assessment_id: Optional. Represents the ID of the CarbonAssessment currently in focus on this page. On initial load, the page calls getCarbonCalculatorSummary. If latest_assessment is present and its id matches the assessment_id parameter, the page treats this assessment as the current one. If they do not match, the page uses latest_assessment as the current assessment and may update the URL to set assessment_id to latest_assessment.id so that URLs always reflect the true latest assessment.

- goal_id: Optional. Represents the ID of the active CarbonReductionGoal the page should treat as focused. After calling getCarbonCalculatorSummary, if an active_goal exists and its id matches goal_id, the page highlights or scrolls to the goal panel. If there is a mismatch, the page uses active_goal.id as the authoritative goal identifier and may update the URL accordingly. After a successful call to setCarbonReductionGoalForLatestAssessment, the page updates goal_id to the returned goal.id.

- mode: Optional. Used to control how the household form interacts with any existing latest_assessment. For example, mode can indicate whether the form should be initialized with values from latest_assessment or start empty for a fresh calculation. If mode is absent, the default behavior is to use the presence of latest_assessment from getCarbonCalculatorSummary to decide whether to prepopulate the results panel and optionally the form. Changing mode updates only client-side UI behavior and does not affect data in the backend.

- view: Optional. Used to determine which section of the page is emphasized or scrolled into view on load (e.g., 'form', 'results', or 'goal'). This parameter does not affect any API calls; it only controls layout behavior and navigation within the page.

Page lifecycle:
1. On initial load or when the URL changes, the page reads assessment_id, goal_id, mode, and view from the query string.
2. The page immediately calls getCarbonCalculatorSummary to obtain latest_assessment and active_goal.
3. Based on the interface response and URL parameters, the page resolves a single current assessment context (using latest_assessment) and a single current goal context (using active_goal), then reconciles URL parameters if necessary (for example, updating assessment_id and goal_id to match the returned data via history.pushState or a similar mechanism).
4. When the user submits the household form, the page calls calculateCarbonFootprint. On success, it treats the returned assessment as the new current assessment context, overwrites any previous assessment_id in the URL with this new id, and replaces any result data on screen with the latest results_summary.
5. When the user saves a reduction goal, the page calls setCarbonReductionGoalForLatestAssessment. On success, it updates the current goal context with the returned goal, updates goal_id in the URL, and sets or confirms view to focus on the goal panel.
6. At no point does the page rely on localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies for state persistence; all restorable state is encoded in URL parameters and reconstructed via calls to getCarbonCalculatorSummary and the immediate results of calculateCarbonFootprint and setCarbonReductionGoalForLatestAssessment.

components.functionality: On page load and on URL change, reads URL parameters (assessment_id, goal_id, mode, view) and calls getCarbonCalculatorSummary to initialize the current assessment and goal contexts. It reconciles any mismatch between URL parameters and the latest_assessment/active_goal returned by the interface, updating the URL if necessary to keep it aligned with actual data.

Collects all parameters required by calculateCarbonFootprint (household_size, bedrooms, weekly_driving_miles, vehicle_fuel_type, monthly_electricity_kwh, heating_type, monthly_natural_gas_therms). Validates user inputs, optionally initializes values based on latest_assessment from getCarbonCalculatorSummary when appropriate, and on submission triggers calculateCarbonFootprint. After a successful response, it forwards the returned assessment and results_summary to other components and updates the URL assessment_id via the state manager.

Displays the baseline annual carbon footprint results. When calculateCarbonFootprint succeeds, it shows baseline_annual_emissions_tons_co2e, iterates through emissions_by_category to present category-level emissions and percentages, and renders comparison_to_average. If getCarbonCalculatorSummary.latest_assessment is available on page load (before any new calculation), it can display an initial summary consistent with that assessment if corresponding summary data is provided via page state.

Allows the user to configure a carbon reduction goal for the most recent assessment when permitted. It is enabled only when there is a current assessment context and can_set_reduction_goal from calculateCarbonFootprint is true or when getCarbonCalculatorSummary.active_goal exists. The form collects reduction_percentage and a goal name, and can display a computed target emissions preview derived from the current assessment's baseline_annual_emissions_tons_co2e and the entered percentage. On submission, it passes the form values to the goal-save-handler, which calls setCarbonReductionGoalForLatestAssessment.

Handles saving of a carbon reduction goal. When invoked by the reduction-goal-editor form submission, it calls setCarbonReductionGoalForLatestAssessment with the user-entered reduction_percentage and name. On success, it updates the active goal context in page state, notifies the active-goal-summary-panel to re-render, and updates the URL to include goal_id. On failure, it provides error details back to the reduction-goal-editor for display.

Presents the details of the currently active carbon reduction goal, including name, reduction_percentage, baseline_emissions_tons_co2e, target_emissions_tons_co2e, and status. It listens for data from getCarbonCalculatorSummary.active_goal on page load and from setCarbonReductionGoalForLatestAssessment on goal creation or update. The panel also hosts the "View this goal in My Dashboard" navigation control.

Implements the "View this goal in My Dashboard" interaction. When activated, it constructs a URL to dashboard.html and can include query parameters such as goal_id from the current active goal and an optional source indicator that identifies carbon_calculator.html as the referrer. It then navigates the browser to this URL.

Coordinates reading and updating of URL query parameters (assessment_id, goal_id, mode, view) based on user actions and API responses. After successful calculateCarbonFootprint calls, it updates assessment_id. After successful setCarbonReductionGoalForLatestAssessment calls, it updates goal_id and may adjust the view parameter to highlight the goal section. It listens for popstate or similar events to react when the URL changes and triggers reinitialization through the carbon-page-init-loader when needed.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, max-width layout based on a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters (desktop breakpoint). Content appears within a central column spanning roughly 8–10 grid columns, with generous side margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom flow with a full-width, low-profile navigation bar at the top and a large, pale-green main content panel beneath it. Within this panel, all calculator content is arranged in a single central column: first the household baseline form (acting as the editorial hero-style section), followed by the calculation results summary, then the reduction goal editor, and finally the active goal summary. Non-visual components (loader, handlers, state) sit logically around this column, influencing overlays and behavior but not occupying visible layout space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom flow with a full-width, low-profile navigation bar at the top and a large, pale-green main content panel beneath it. Within this panel, all calculator content is arranged in a single central column: first the household baseline form (acting as the editorial hero-style section), followed by the calculation results summary, then the reduction goal editor, and finally the active goal summary. Non-visual components (loader, handlers, state) sit logically around this column, influencing overlays and behavior but not occupying visible layout space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a centered content column spanning roughly 70–80% of the viewport width (about 8–10 of 12 grid columns) with generous side gutters (10–15% of width on each side) on desktop. The baseline form occupies roughly the upper half of the main content area on first view, with large vertical padding above and below. The results and goal-related panels stack beneath with substantial vertical separation (48–64px between major blocks, 24–32px between related elements) and internal padding of 24px top/bottom and 32px left/right. The page-init loader, when active, temporarily covers the entire main content column without changing its dimensions.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep density low and editorial: short line lengths, comfortable line heights, and plenty of whitespace around and inside each component. Forms and panels are grouped into card-like blocks with clear breathing room between field groups and text sections. No component spans full browser width edge to edge; everything is framed by the central column and wide gutters to preserve a calm, scientific tone rather than a packed dashboard feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye in a vertical F-pattern: start at the top-left with the logo area (outside this spec) and the right-aligned dashboard navigation link, then drop down to the left-aligned baseline form heading and description, continue down through the input fields, then to the results summary panel, then further down to the goal editor and active goal summary. When active, the loader overlay becomes the dominant focal point, sitting over this flow. Any transient feedback from the goal-save handler appears briefly above the lower-right area of the main column, acting as a secondary focal point after the main content scan.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a slim, full-width navigation bar at the very top, resting on a very light background. Within this bar, the content is centered: on the left sits the site identity (outside this spec), and on the far right a subtle ‘Dashboard’ navigation link. Beneath this, a broad, pale-green main panel fills most of the viewport height, bounded by wide left and right margins. Inside this panel, a single central column holds all calculator content. At the top of this column, the household baseline form behaves like an editorial hero: a clear, left-aligned title and short explanatory text, followed by vertically stacked form fields, all spaced generously. Below it, with a comfortable gap, a wide results summary panel stretches across the same central width, presenting the calculated carbon footprint in a clean, card-like container. Further down, another separated block hosts the reduction goal editor, followed by a lighter-weight active goal summary card. Everything aligns to the same invisible vertical axis, with consistent margins and card padding, creating a calm, structured reading rhythm from top to bottom. While data loads, a soft overlay covers this central content area, and any save feedback appears briefly near the lower-right of the column, without disrupting the overall serene layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init loader appears as a semi-opaque, pale overlay that sits directly on top of the entire main content column, beginning just below the top navigation bar and extending down to the bottom of the visible content area. It respects the same left and right gutters as the main layout, so it does not reach the very edges of the browser window. At roughly 40% down from the top of the viewport, centered horizontally within the content column, a simple loading indicator and short status message appear, aligned with the left edge of the underlying text column. When active, this overlay dims and softens the visibility of the baseline form, results panel, and goal sections beneath without shifting their positions; when loading completes, the overlay disappears entirely and leaves no residual space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute or fixed overlay anchored to the main content container; it covers the underlying components within the central column but does not alter their layout or spacing when shown or hidden.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The household baseline form is the first and most prominent block within the pale-green main content panel. It begins approximately 64px below the bottom edge of the top navigation bar, centered horizontally within the page, and spans about 70–80% of the page width, leaving wide, even gutters on both sides. Inside the form, a clear, left-aligned title (e.g., 'Carbon Footprint Calculator') sits at the top, with a short descriptive paragraph beneath it, separated by about 24px. Below this text, the input fields are arranged in a single column, each separated vertically by 16–24px to maintain the airy editorial feel. The entire form block has generous internal padding—around 24px on the top and bottom and 32px on the left and right—so that none of the content touches the card edges. Vertically, this section occupies roughly the upper half of the first scrollable viewport on desktop, with additional whitespace below it before the next panel to preserve the calm, spacious aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing as the first main content block in the normal document flow within the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calculation results summary panel sits directly below the household baseline form, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px to clearly indicate a new section. It is centered within the same content column and matches the form’s width, spanning roughly 70–80% of the page width with the same wide outer gutters. The panel appears as a distinct card or band—either a white card against the pale-green background or a slightly tinted version of that background—with soft, 24px top/bottom and 32px left/right internal padding. The main numerical result and key highlights are left-aligned near the top of the panel, with supplementary details or labels stacked beneath in short lines, maintaining a comfortable line length. Visually, it forms a solid lower anchor on the page, occupying roughly a quarter to a third of the viewport height when results are first shown, echoing the mockup’s large image band that follows the hero text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, appearing immediately after the baseline form in the vertical flow; it expands vertically based on content but always respects the central column and consistent margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reduction goal editor is placed beneath the results summary panel, with a vertical separation of about 48px to keep each section visually distinct yet clearly related. It uses the same central alignment and width as the components above, occupying approximately 70–80% of the page width and aligning perfectly with their left and right edges. Internally, the editor has card-like styling with around 24px of padding on the top and bottom and 32px on the sides. A compact, left-aligned heading introduces the goal-setting area, followed by a brief explanatory sentence, separated by 16–24px. Form controls for naming the goal and specifying targets are arranged in a single vertical column, using the same 16–24px spacing between fields as the baseline form. Overall, the editor’s height is slightly less than the results panel, giving it a comfortable but subordinate presence in the lower half of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying a distinct card within the main content column, directly following the results summary panel in the normal flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The goal-save handler does not occupy a fixed visual area in the baseline layout. Instead, when a goal is saved, it triggers a brief, lightweight feedback element—such as a small toast notification—that appears near the lower-right portion of the main content column, just above the bottom edge of the viewport and inside the right gutter. This toast spans roughly 30–40% of the content column width, with modest padding and a subtle background to remain visible but not disruptive. It overlays the content beneath it without shifting the positions of the form, results, or summary panels, and then fades away after a short duration, leaving no permanent space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual in the resting layout; when active, it temporarily overlays the main content column near the lower-right area, positioned fixed relative to the viewport without affecting underlying layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active goal summary panel is positioned below the reduction goal editor, separated by a slightly smaller gap of about 32–48px, indicating that it is closely related but somewhat less dominant. It continues the same central alignment and width as the other sections, spanning about 70–80% of the page width and aligning with their left and right edges. The panel is visually lighter than the results panel—perhaps using a subtle border or softer background—to signal its tertiary status. Inside, the content is compact: a short, left-aligned label or heading at the top, followed by a concise summary of the current active goal, arranged in one or two short lines or small blocks, all with around 16–24px spacing. The internal padding mirrors other cards (approximately 24px top/bottom and 32px left/right), but the overall height is modest, making it feel like a concluding note at the bottom of the main content flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, sitting as the last main card in the vertical stack within the central column, naturally following the reduction goal editor.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dashboard navigation link lives in the top navigation bar, which stretches full-width across the top of the page. Within this bar, content is centered in line with the main page grid. The link itself is positioned on the far right of this central row, horizontally aligned with the logo or site name on the left (not specified here). It appears as a clean, understated text link or pill-style button with ample horizontal padding (12–16px) and a comfortable click target height of around 40–48px. The bar has minimal vertical height, around 64px total, keeping it light so that the main attention shifts quickly down to the calculator content. The link maintains a calm color palette—perhaps a desaturated green or muted neutral—so it is discoverable without competing with the primary content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within the top navigation bar as part of the standard document flow; the nav bar itself may stay at the top of the viewport on scroll if the overall site design uses a sticky header, but the link does not have independent floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager has no direct visual representation in the layout. It operates in the background, synchronizing the state of the baseline form, results, and goal sections with the browser’s address bar or history. Any changes it triggers—such as navigating to a specific state of the calculator—are reflected by updating which of the visible components are populated or scrolled into view, but it does not consume any visible space or alter the spatial arrangement of elements on its own.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely behavioral with no spatial footprint; it interacts with component state and navigation without affecting layout flow or occupying space.

name: Conservation Projects
title: Conservation Projects
description: Browse, filter, and sort conservation projects that can receive donations or pledges, and navigate to detailed project pages to set up support.

core_features: Initialize available filter and sort options for conservation projects using getConservationProjectFilterOptions, then load an initial project list using searchConservationProjects based on URL parameters.
Allow users to filter projects by category, transparency rating, and suggested monthly donation using dynamic filter controls bound to getConservationProjectFilterOptions and URL parameters.
Allow users to sort projects by attributes such as suggested monthly donation, transparency, impact, or name using sort options returned from getConservationProjectFilterOptions.

Display a paginated list of conservation projects from searchConservationProjects, showing key attributes like name, short_description, category_label, geographic_focus, transparency_rating, suggested_monthly_donation/suggested_monthly_label, and impact_score.
Enable navigation to individual project detail pages (project_detail.html) by clicking on project names or cards, passing the projectId via URL parameters.
Provide a link to the user dashboard (dashboard.html) where users can view their pledges, without performing pledge operations on this page.
Synchronize all filter, sort, and pagination state with URL parameters so that the project list state is shareable and restorable on reload without using localStorage.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Arrive from Home/Dashboard with preselected filters:
1) User clicks a link on index.html or dashboard.html that leads to projects.html with one or more of the following URL parameters set: category, minTransparency, maxMonthly, sort.
2) On load, the page parses the URL parameters and normalizes them (e.g., coerces minTransparency and maxMonthly to numbers if valid; ignores invalid values).
3) The page calls getConservationProjectFilterOptions to obtain the available categories, transparency_rating_range, suggested_monthly_donation_range, and sort_options.
4) The filter controls are initialized using the returned options and pre-populated based on the URL parameters (for example, the category control highlights the category that matches the category URL parameter if it exists in the returned list).
5) The page builds a filters object and sort_by value from the URL-derived state and calls searchConservationProjects with filters, sort_by, page, and page_size.
6) Project results returned by searchConservationProjects are rendered in the project list with pagination controls.
7) The user can now adjust filters, sort order, or pagination; all interactions update the URL and trigger new calls to searchConservationProjects.

Workflow 2 – Filter and refine projects directly on projects.html:
1) User accesses projects.html directly (no pre-filters) or after completing a previous search.
2) On load, the page reads any existing URL parameters; if none are present, it treats all filters as unset and uses a default sort_by derived from getConservationProjectFilterOptions.sort_options.
3) The page calls getConservationProjectFilterOptions and then searchConservationProjects to display an initial project list.
4) User modifies filters in the filter sidebar (e.g., selects a category, adjusts minimum transparency rating, sets a maximum suggested monthly donation) and optionally uses active filter tags to remove specific filters.
5) Each change updates the corresponding URL parameters (category, minTransparency, maxMonthly) and resets the page parameter to the first page, then triggers a new call to searchConservationProjects.
6) The project list updates according to the new filters, and the header shows the updated total_count and filter summary.

Workflow 3 – Sort projects and navigate between pages:
1) After results are loaded, the user selects a sort option from the sorting controls populated from getConservationProjectFilterOptions.sort_options.
2) The selection updates the sort URL parameter and resets the page parameter to the first page.
3) The page calls searchConservationProjects again with the updated sort_by and existing filters.
4) The user uses pagination controls (e.g., next/previous or specific page numbers) to navigate through results.
5) Each pagination action updates the page (and optionally pageSize) URL parameters and triggers searchConservationProjects with the same filters and sort_by but different page/page_size values.
6) The list and header update to reflect the current page and maintain consistent URL state, so the user can bookmark or share the exact result view.

Workflow 4 – View a project in detail and return to the list:
1) From the project list, the user clicks on a project name or card.
2) The page navigates to project_detail.html with a projectId URL parameter derived from the clicked project’s project_id.
3) After viewing details or creating a pledge on project_detail.html, the user returns to projects.html via browser back navigation or a dedicated link.
4) Because filters, sort, and pagination are preserved in the URL, projects.html restores the previous state by re-reading the URL parameters and re-calling searchConservationProjects with the same filters, sort_by, page, and page_size.

Workflow 5 – Jump to pledges overview:
1) The user clicks a "View my pledges" link in the project list header or navigation area.
2) The page navigates to dashboard.html without additional parameters (or with only those parameters defined for dashboard.html, if any are added elsewhere).
3) The user can return to projects.html through navigation; URL parameters, if preserved by the link, determine whether the previous project list state is restored or a new default search is performed.
interactions: On initial page load or when the browser URL changes (e.g., via back/forward), the page-init logic parses URL parameters (category, minTransparency, maxMonthly, sort, page, pageSize) and triggers getConservationProjectFilterOptions followed by searchConservationProjects.

When the user selects or changes the category filter, the filter-sidebar component updates the category URL parameter (adding, changing, or removing it), resets the page URL parameter to the first page, and triggers a new searchConservationProjects call with the updated filters.

When the user adjusts the minimum transparency rating (for example, via a slider or input), the filter-sidebar component updates the minTransparency URL parameter, resets the page parameter, and triggers searchConservationProjects.
When the user adjusts the maximum suggested monthly donation, the filter-sidebar component updates the maxMonthly URL parameter, resets the page parameter, and triggers searchConservationProjects.

When the user clears all filters, the filter-sidebar (or an active-filters component) removes category, minTransparency, and maxMonthly from the URL, resets the page parameter, and triggers searchConservationProjects with an empty filters object.

When the user changes the sort option (e.g., selects a different sort label returned from sort_options), the sorting-bar component updates the sort URL parameter, resets the page parameter, and triggers searchConservationProjects with the new sort_by value.

When the user clicks pagination controls (next, previous, or a specific page number), the pagination component updates the page URL parameter (and pageSize if changed by the user), then triggers searchConservationProjects with the same filters and sort_by but the new page/page_size values.

When searchConservationProjects returns results, the project-list component binds the results array to individual project list items/cards and updates the header summary with total_count, page, and page_size.

If searchConservationProjects returns zero results (total_count is zero), the empty-state component displays a message and may offer a control to reset filters; activating that control clears filter-related URL parameters and triggers a new search.
When the user clicks on a project name or card, the project-list component navigates to project_detail.html with the projectId URL parameter set to the clicked item’s project_id.
When the user clicks the "View my pledges" link, the header or navigation component navigates to dashboard.html without invoking any additional interfaces.

If getConservationProjectFilterOptions or searchConservationProjects return an error or invalid structure, the page displays an error state, and the user can retry the operation via a retry control that re-invokes the relevant interface call.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for filters, sorting, and pagination state. No state is persisted in localStorage.

URL parameters used on this page:
- category (string): Optional. A value intended to match one of the categories returned by getConservationProjectFilterOptions.categories. If present and valid, it is applied as a category filter when calling searchConservationProjects. If present but not found in the available categories, it is ignored for filtering but left in the URL unless explicitly removed by user action.
- minTransparency (number): Optional. Parsed as a numeric minimum transparency rating.
