If parsing fails or the value is outside the transparency_rating_range returned by getConservationProjectFilterOptions, it is ignored for filtering but left unchanged in the URL until filters are reset or the user modifies it. When valid, it is passed in the filters object to searchConservationProjects.
- maxMonthly (number): Optional. Parsed as a numeric maximum suggested monthly donation. If parsing fails or the value is outside suggested_monthly_donation_range, it is ignored for filtering but left in the URL. When valid, it is passed in the filters object to searchConservationProjects.
- sort (string): Optional. Intended to match one of the keys in getConservationProjectFilterOptions.sort_options. If present and valid, it is used as the sort_by parameter when calling searchConservationProjects. If missing, the page uses a default sort_by derived from the filter options (for example, the first sort_options entry) but does not need to write that default into the URL unless a user actively changes sorting. If present but invalid, it is ignored for sorting while remaining unchanged in the URL until the user selects another sort option.
- page (number): Optional. Indicates which page of the results is currently shown. If missing or invalid, the page defaults to the first page. Whenever the user changes filters or sort options, page is reset to the first page. Pagination controls update this parameter and trigger new searchConservationProjects calls.
- pageSize (number): Optional. Indicates the desired number of results per page. If provided and valid, it is passed as page_size to searchConservationProjects; otherwise, an application-level default page size (defined outside this specification) is used. If the user changes page size via pagination controls, pageSize is updated in the URL, page is reset to the first page, and a new searchConservationProjects call is made.

State lifecycle:
1) On every entry to projects.html (including reloads and navigation via back/forward), the page-init logic reads these URL parameters and normalizes them into an internal state object representing filters, sort, and pagination.
2) The page calls getConservationProjectFilterOptions once per load (or when a full refresh is triggered), then uses both the returned options and the normalized URL parameters to derive the effective filters and sort_by for searchConservationProjects.
3) When any UI interaction modifies filters, sort, or pagination, the corresponding URL parameters are updated immediately (using pushState/replaceState or equivalent navigation), and page is reset appropriately (usually to the first page). A new searchConservationProjects call is then made with the updated state.
4) searchConservationProjects results (results, total_count, page, page_size) are treated as derived state only, not persisted elsewhere; they are refreshed exclusively through interface calls initiated by URL or UI changes.
5) Because all user-configurable state is represented in URL parameters, links to projects.html (including those from index.html and dashboard.html) can preconfigure the page simply by setting category, minTransparency, maxMonthly, and sort in the query string.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and URL-state synchronization for the Conservation Projects page. On load and on URL changes, it parses URL parameters into a normalized state object, calls getConservationProjectFilterOptions to retrieve available filters and sort options, and then calls searchConservationProjects with filters and sorting derived from both the URL and the filter options.
data_binding: URLParameters: category, minTransparency, maxMonthly, sort, page, pageSize
event_handlers: onLoad: Parse URL parameters, call getConservationProjectFilterOptions, then call searchConservationProjects with derived filters, sort_by, page, and page_size.
onURLChange: Re-parse URL parameters (e.g., when user uses browser navigation), then re-call searchConservationProjects with updated filters, sort_by, page, and page_size.
onFilterChange: Receive updated filter state from filter-sidebar, update URL parameters, then invoke searchConservationProjects with new filters.
onSortChange: Receive updated sort key from sorting-bar, update sort URL parameter, reset page parameter, then invoke searchConservationProjects.
onPaginationChange: Receive updated page and/or pageSize from pagination-controls, update corresponding URL parameters, then invoke searchConservationProjects.

components.functionality: Provides interactive controls to filter conservation projects by category, minimum transparency rating, and maximum suggested monthly donation. It initializes its options from getConservationProjectFilterOptions and current selections from URL parameters, and notifies the page-init component of any filter changes.
event_handlers: onInit: Read filter options from getConservationProjectFilterOptions results and pre-fill control values based on URL parameters.

onCategoryChange: When the user selects or deselects a category, emit a filter-change event with the new category value; projects-page-init updates the category URL parameter and re-queries searchConservationProjects.

onMinTransparencyChange: When the user adjusts the minimum transparency rating, emit a filter-change event; projects-page-init updates the minTransparency URL parameter and re-queries searchConservationProjects.

onMaxMonthlyChange: When the user adjusts the maximum suggested monthly donation, emit a filter-change event; projects-page-init updates the maxMonthly URL parameter and re-queries searchConservationProjects.

onClearAllFilters: When the user clicks a 'clear filters' control, emit a filter-change event with all filters unset; projects-page-init removes category, minTransparency, and maxMonthly URL parameters and re-queries searchConservationProjects.

components.functionality: Displays and manages sorting options for the project list. Sort options are populated dynamically from getConservationProjectFilterOptions.sort_options, and the current selection is bound to the sort URL parameter.
event_handlers: onInit: Populate the sort control with getConservationProjectFilterOptions.sort_options and select the option matching the sort URL parameter if valid; otherwise, select a default option without necessarily updating the URL.

onSortOptionChange: When the user selects a different sort option, emit a sort-change event with the selected sort key; projects-page-init updates the sort URL parameter, resets the page parameter, and calls searchConservationProjects.

components.functionality: Shows a summary of the current project results (such as total_count and current page range) and a concise representation of the active filters. Also provides a "View my pledges" navigation link to the dashboard.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: When new results from searchConservationProjects are available, update total-count and page summary display.
onFiltersUpdate: When filter state changes, recompute and display a human-readable summary of active filters based on current URL parameters.
onViewPledgesClick: Navigate to dashboard.html via a standard link or routing mechanism without invoking additional interfaces.

components.functionality: Displays removable tags or badges representing each active filter (category, minimum transparency, maximum monthly donation). Allows users to remove individual filters quickly, updating URL parameters and triggering a refreshed search.
event_handlers: onInit: Read URL parameters and render one tag per active filter.

onRemoveCategoryTag: When the category tag is removed, emit a filter-change event with category unset; projects-page-init removes the category parameter from the URL, resets page, and calls searchConservationProjects.
onRemoveMinTransparencyTag: When the transparency tag is removed, emit a filter-change event with minTransparency unset; projects-page-init updates the URL and re-queries searchConservationProjects.
onRemoveMaxMonthlyTag: When the maximum monthly tag is removed, emit a filter-change event with maxMonthly unset; projects-page-init updates the URL and re-queries searchConservationProjects.

components.functionality: Renders the list of conservation projects returned by searchConservationProjects. Each list item/card displays key project information and provides a click target that navigates to the project_detail.html page for that project.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: When new searchConservationProjects results arrive, render one list item/card for each result and bind all relevant fields.
onProjectClick: When a user clicks a project name or card, navigate to project_detail.html with projectId set to the clicked result’s project_id in the URL parameters.

onInactiveProjectRender: If is_active is false for a project, adjust the item’s available interactions (e.g., disable click navigation) according to application rules while still displaying informational data, without altering the underlying result data.

components.functionality: Provides controls to navigate through pages of project results. It uses searchConservationProjects.total_count, page, and page_size to compute page counts and bind UI controls, and updates URL parameters when the user changes page or page size.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: When new searchConservationProjects results arrive, recompute pagination state (number of pages, whether next/previous are enabled) based on total_count, page, and page_size.
onPageClick: When a user selects a specific page, emit a pagination-change event with the selected page; projects-page-init updates the page URL parameter and calls searchConservationProjects.

onNextClick: When a user clicks the next-page control, emit a pagination-change event with page incremented by one (if allowed); projects-page-init updates URL and re-queries searchConservationProjects.

onPreviousClick: When a user clicks the previous-page control, emit a pagination-change event with page decremented by one (if allowed); projects-page-init updates URL and re-queries searchConservationProjects.

onPageSizeChange: If the user selects a different page size, emit a pagination-change event with updated pageSize and reset page to the first page; projects-page-init updates pageSize and page URL parameters and re-queries searchConservationProjects.

components.functionality: Displays a message and optional controls when searchConservationProjects returns no results (total_count equals zero), allowing the user to adjust or clear filters to broaden the search.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: When total_count is zero, show the empty-state message; when total_count is greater than zero, hide this component.

onResetFiltersClick: When the user clicks a control to reset filters, emit a filter-change event indicating that all filters should be cleared; projects-page-init removes category, minTransparency, and maxMonthly from the URL, resets page, and calls searchConservationProjects.

components.functionality: Handles and displays errors from getConservationProjectFilterOptions or searchConservationProjects, providing the user with an option to retry the failed operation.
event_handlers: onFilterOptionsError: When getConservationProjectFilterOptions fails, display an error message and show a retry button.
onSearchError: When searchConservationProjects fails, display an error message and show a retry button.
onRetryFilterOptionsClick: Re-invoke getConservationProjectFilterOptions and, on success, trigger a new searchConservationProjects call using the current URL-derived state.
onRetrySearchClick: Re-invoke searchConservationProjects using the current filters, sort_by, page, and page_size derived from URL parameters and filter options.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, max-width layout based on a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters (desktop breakpoint). Content appears within a central column spanning roughly 8–10 grid columns, with generous side margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow anchored by a calm hero introduction for the 'Conservation Projects' page, followed by a structured results area. Under the global navigation, a full-width, pale-green hero section introduces the page with a left-aligned H1 and a short descriptive paragraph, mirroring the mockup’s title + intro block. Below this, the main content splits into a two-column layout within a centered max-width container: a narrow filter sidebar on the left and a wider project-results column on the right that holds sorting, result summaries, active filter tags, the project list, and pagination. Empty and error states occupy the project-results column where the list would normally appear, while the filter sidebar remains visible.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow anchored by a calm hero introduction for the 'Conservation Projects' page, followed by a structured results area. Under the global navigation, a full-width, pale-green hero section introduces the page with a left-aligned H1 and a short descriptive paragraph, mirroring the mockup’s title + intro block. Below this, the main content splits into a two-column layout within a centered max-width container: a narrow filter sidebar on the left and a wider project-results column on the right that holds sorting, result summaries, active filter tags, the project list, and pagination. Empty and error states occupy the project-results column where the list would normally appear, while the filter sidebar remains visible.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The layout preserves wide side gutters and a central content column: the overall page content spans about 70–80% of the browser width, centered, leaving 10–15% gutters on each side. The hero section (page-init) uses generous vertical space (roughly 35–45% of the initial viewport height) with spacious top and bottom padding. Below, the main content area allocates roughly 30% of the central width to the filter sidebar and 70% to the project-results column. Vertical spacing between the hero and main content is around 48–64px, and between stacked elements in the results column (sorting bar, header, tags, list, pagination) is in the 16–32px range, maintaining the airy, non-dense feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low, following a minimalist editorial style. Text blocks (hero intro, list summary, error/empty messages) use short line lengths and comfortable line spacing, never stretching full browser width. Each functional block (filters, sorting, list, pagination) has enough internal padding (around 16–24px) to feel distinct and readable. The project list shows a moderate number of projects per viewport scroll, with generous vertical space between cards, prioritizing clarity and scan-ability over packing in as many items as possible.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F-shaped pattern adapted from the mockup: users first see the logo and top navigation, then their eye drops to the left-aligned hero heading 'Conservation Projects', then down to the descriptive paragraph. From there, the eye moves into the main content: first to the left where the filters begin, then across to the right where the sorting controls and list summary sit at the top of the results. Scanning continues down through active filter tags and the vertical stack of project cards. A floating accessibility button (global pattern) remains in the lower-right, acting as a secondary focal point without competing with the main content. Error and empty states, when present, briefly become the primary focus within the right column by occupying the central content area there.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s global navigation, the page opens with a calm, full-width hero band on a very pale green background. Centered within the page’s max-width column, a large left-aligned heading 'Conservation Projects' sits with ample breathing room above and below, followed by a short, multi-line paragraph that introduces the purpose of the project directory. The hero area feels spacious and editorial, occupying roughly the upper third to half of the initial viewport. Scrolling slightly (or just below the hero), the layout transitions into a clean two-column content region framed by wide side gutters. On the left, a vertically oriented filter panel runs down the page, occupying about a third of the central content width; on the right, a wider column contains a slim sorting bar at the top, then a line summarizing the number of projects, then a row of active filter tags, and finally a relaxed vertical list of project cards. Pagination controls sit centered below the list with generous top margin, giving a clear sense of completion at the bottom of each page. When there are no matching projects or an error occurs, the right-hand column swaps the list for a clear message panel that sits comfortably within the same spatial rhythm, while the filter panel remains steady on the left. The overall effect is a calm, organized, editorial browsing experience that feels more like reading a well-designed report than using a commerce catalog.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init component forms a hero-style introductory section directly beneath the global navigation, spanning the full width of the central content container and sitting within the same pale green background seen in the mockup. It occupies roughly 35–45% of the initial viewport height on desktop. Inside, content is aligned to a central column (about 70–80% of the page width) with wide gutters on both sides. The main H1 'Conservation Projects' is left-aligned, positioned in the upper third of this band, with at least 64px of top padding from the bottom edge of the nav and 48–64px of bottom padding to the next section. A multi-line introductory paragraph sits directly below the H1, separated by around 24–32px, with a comfortable line length (approximately 50–65 characters) so it wraps into several short lines. There are no strong dividing borders; instead, negative space defines the section. The overall feel is airy and editorial, echoing the 'Biodiversity' hero from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width block within the main content flow, anchored immediately below the global navigation. Its height adapts to content but targets roughly 35–45% of viewport height on desktop, shrinking naturally on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter sidebar sits in the main content region directly below the hero (projects-page-init), aligned to the left side of the central content column. The entire main content area below the hero is a two-column layout: the filter sidebar occupies roughly 28–32% of the central width, while the project-results column to its right takes the remaining 68–72%. Horizontally, the filter panel starts after the inner left gutter, so it never touches the edge of the viewport. Vertically, the top edge of the filter sidebar aligns visually with the top of the project-sorting-bar on the right, both separated from the hero by about 48px of vertical space. The sidebar itself is a tall, scrollable column with light internal padding of 24px on all sides, and individual filter groups are separated by 16–24px. It feels like a light, unobtrusive vertical card rather than a heavy panel, maintaining the minimalist style. As the user scrolls down through the project list, the filter sidebar remains visible near the top portion of the viewport, extending downwards as needed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a left-side column within the main content. On desktop, the inner content of the filter panel is sticky, remaining pinned near the top of the viewport with a small offset (around 24px below the global nav or top of viewport), so users can adjust filters while scrolling the project list on the right. On smaller screens, it may collapse into an off-canvas or top-sheet, but in the default desktop layout it stays fixed in the left column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-sorting-bar is the topmost element of the right-hand project-results column, horizontally aligned with the top of the filter sidebar. It spans the full width of the right column (about 68–72% of the central content area) and sits approximately 48px below the bottom edge of the hero section, preserving generous vertical rhythm. The bar has a low visual height (roughly 48–56px), feeling like a slim control strip rather than a bulky toolbar. Inside this strip, the main label or secondary information (e.g., 'Sort by') is left-aligned, while the sort control (dropdown, toggles, etc.) is right-aligned, creating a subtle left-to-right scanning motion consistent with the F-pattern. There is 16–24px of internal vertical padding so controls are comfortably spaced but not oversized. The bar subtly anchors the top of the results area, clearly separating interactive controls from data content without heavy borders.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the right column, positioned directly under the hero and above the list header. It scrolls away with the rest of the content; it is not sticky by default in this layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-list-header appears immediately below the project-sorting-bar within the right-hand column. It is a full-width, single-line or short multi-line text element that summarizes the result set (e.g., 'Showing 24 conservation projects'). It is separated from the sorting bar by a modest vertical gap of around 16–24px. The text is left-aligned, aligning with the left edge of the right column’s content block, and its line length is kept short to feel like a subtitle, not another hero. There is ample white space above and below (16–24px), allowing this summary to be quickly scannable and clearly distinct from both the controls above and tags/list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static textual block within the right column, moving with the page scroll. It does not have any sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active-filter-tags component is positioned just below the project-list-header, still within the right-hand column, separated by about 16px of vertical space. The tags are left-aligned and start at the same left edge as the header text, forming a visually continuous vertical line with the heading above and the project cards below. Tags are displayed as a horizontal row of compact chips that can wrap onto multiple lines as needed. There is a small vertical gap (8–12px) between wrapped lines of tags, preserving the airy feel. The entire tag block is treated as a slim strip occupying only as much vertical space as needed, with a further 24–32px gap before the start of the project-list so that the tags feel like a light, optional layer rather than crowding the cards. When no filters are active, this area collapses entirely, and the list moves up to sit closer to the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the right column that expands or contracts vertically depending on the number of active tags. It scrolls with the page and does not fix or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-list occupies the main vertical portion of the right-hand column beneath the active-filter-tags (or directly beneath the list header when no tags are active). It starts after a vertical gap of around 24–32px, giving a clear visual separation between filters/tags and content. The list spans the full width of the right column and is composed of individual project cards stacked vertically. Each card uses generous internal padding (around 24–32px) and is separated from the next by roughly 24–32px of vertical space, keeping the layout uncluttered and easy to scan. The cards themselves adhere to the editorial style: clear hierarchy, ample whitespace, and limited visual noise. As the user scrolls, the left filter sidebar stays relatively fixed, while the right column with its list scrolls, reinforcing the sense of the right column as the main reading surface. The list area typically occupies the middle to lower half of the viewport on first load and extends downward as more projects are listed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A vertically scrolling stack within the right column. It expands as more items are loaded or paged, pushing the pagination controls downward. It does not have infinite scroll by default; pagination controls at the bottom define page boundaries.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-pagination-controls component sits at the bottom of the right-hand column, directly beneath the project-list. It is separated from the last project card by a generous vertical gap of around 32–48px, creating a clear visual break that signals the end of the current page of results. The pagination controls are horizontally centered within the right column, yet still constrained by the same left and right margins as the list content. The controls themselves occupy a relatively low vertical height (roughly 40–56px), with sufficient internal spacing between page numbers/buttons to maintain the minimalist, non-cluttered feel. Below the controls, an additional 48–64px of bottom margin provides breathing room before the page ends or before any footer content, reinforcing a sense of calm completion at the end of a scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored at the bottom of the right column content. It scrolls into view after users move through the list and remains in place relative to the list; it does not stick to the bottom of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The projects-empty-state occupies the same spatial region as the project-list when there are zero matching results after filtering. In this scenario, the filter sidebar remains in place on the left, and the right-hand column still shows the sorting bar, list header, and active-filter-tags (if any). Directly beneath the tags (or header when no tags), after about 32px of vertical space, the empty state appears as a centered panel within the right column. It uses ample internal padding (around 32–40px) and is vertically generous enough to feel like a self-contained message—roughly taking up 40–60% of the visible area of the right column, with whitespace above and below. The message is center-aligned or subtly left-aligned within the panel, but the panel itself is centered horizontally. If the viewport height is tall, the panel appears approximately in the middle of the right column’s vertical space, so the experience feels deliberate rather than like a small error note floating at the top.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional static block that replaces the project-list area within the right column when no results are available. It scrolls with the page, but its height is usually sufficient that it is mostly visible without requiring additional scrolling after it appears.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The projects-error-state appears within the right-hand column when there is a loading or data error for the project list. When active, it takes precedence over the list: the list itself is hidden, and the error message occupies the top of the results area. The error panel is positioned just below the project-sorting-bar and above the project-list-header, or it can temporarily replace both the header and list region to keep the message closer to the top of the viewport. It spans the full width of the right column and uses generous padding (around 24–32px) with a clear, concise message and possibly a retry action. Vertical spacing around the panel (24–32px above and below) ensures it feels intentional and readable, not cramped. The filter sidebar on the left remains visible and unchanged, reinforcing that the issue is with loading results rather than the entire page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional static block that appears within the right column. It pushes the list content area down or replaces it entirely while active, and it scrolls with the page. Once the error is resolved, the error component disappears and the normal list layout is restored.

name: Project Detail
title: Conservation Project Detail
description: Displays full details for a specific conservation project and allows users to create one-time or recurring donation pledges for that project.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for a conservation project based on the projectId URL parameter using getConservationProjectDetails.

Show project metadata such as category, geographic focus, transparency rating, impact indicators, and external website link using the project and display data returned by getConservationProjectDetails.
Indicate whether the user already has an active pledge for this project using user_state.has_active_pledge and user_state.active_pledge from getConservationProjectDetails.
Provide a donation pledge form where users can enter donor name, donor email, donation amount, and pledge frequency (one_time, monthly, yearly).
Optionally suggest a donation amount using the suggested_monthly_donation and display.suggested_monthly_label from getConservationProjectDetails as non-binding hints in the pledge form.
Create a new donation pledge for the current project using createDonationPledge with the user-entered donor_name, donor_email, amount, and frequency.
On successful pledge creation, display a confirmation summary including amount, frequency, and status from the createDonationPledge response.
Provide navigation back to the project list (projects.html) and to the dashboard (dashboard.html) after a pledge is created.
user_workflows:
View project details and existing pledge status:
1) User arrives on project_detail.html with a projectId in the query string.
2) The page reads projectId from the URL and calls getConservationProjectDetails(projectId).
3) While data is loading, a loading state is shown.
4) On success, the project’s name, short and detailed descriptions, category_label, geographic_focus, transparency_label, suggested_monthly_label, and other metadata are displayed.
5) If user_state.has_active_pledge is true, the page shows information about user_state.active_pledge (pledge_id, amount, frequency, status) so the user understands their current support for this project.

Create a new donation pledge:
1) User reviews project information and scrolls to the pledge form.
2) The pledge form automatically associates the pledge with the current projectId and optionally displays suggested_monthly_label as guidance.
3) User enters donor_name and donor_email.
4) User enters a numeric donation amount and selects a frequency from the available options (one_time, monthly, yearly).
5) User submits the form.
6) The form validates required fields (name, email, amount, frequency) and that the amount is a positive number; if invalid, inline errors are shown and no API call is made.
7) On valid submission, the page calls createDonationPledge(projectId, donor_name, donor_email, amount, frequency).
8) While waiting for the response, the form is disabled and a pending state is shown.
9) If createDonationPledge returns success = true, the confirmation component is populated with the returned pledge object (id, amount, frequency, status, start_date, created_at) and displayed to the user.
10) If the API returns success = false, an error message from the response message field is shown and the form is re-enabled.

Post-pledge confirmation and navigation:
1) After a successful pledge, the confirmation panel summarizes the pledge and indicates that a recurring pledge has started if frequency is monthly or yearly.
2) The user can click a link or button labeled to view the pledge in their dashboard.
3) On click, the page navigates to dashboard.html without additional URL parameters as specified.
4) Alternatively, the user can navigate back to the list of projects via a "Back to projects" interaction, which navigates to projects.html.
5) Optionally, the page may update the URL (for example, by adding a pledge-related query parameter) to reflect that a pledge has just been created so that reloading the page can restore the confirmation state.
interactions: Initial page load: On page initialization, read the projectId from the query string and invoke getConservationProjectDetails with that value.
Project reload on URL change: If projectId in the URL changes (e.g., via internal navigation), the page re-runs getConservationProjectDetails to update displayed project data.
Back to projects: Clicking the "Back to projects" element navigates to projects.html.
External project website: If project.website_url is present, clicking the external link opens the project’s own website in a new context (e.g., new tab or window).

Pledge status banner: If user_state.has_active_pledge is true, a banner or status component is shown; if false, the component remains hidden or displays an appropriate message. The banner is purely informational on this page.

Donation amount helpers: In the pledge form, the amount field can use suggested_monthly_label and suggested_monthly_donation (if provided) as placeholder or helper text; the user can still enter any numeric amount.
Frequency selection: User selects the pledge frequency using an input control bound to the DonationPledge.frequency enum values (one_time, monthly, yearly).
Form field input: User types donor_name, donor_email, and amount; changes are stored in component state and validated on input or on submit.

Form submission: Submitting the pledge form triggers client-side validation, then calls createDonationPledge if validation passes. During the API call, inputs are disabled and a loading indicator is shown.

Pledge creation success handling: On a successful createDonationPledge response, the page stores the returned pledge data in state, optionally updates the URL query to reflect that a pledge has been created, hides or locks the pledge form if desired, and shows the confirmation component populated from the pledge data.

Pledge creation failure handling: If createDonationPledge returns success = false or an error occurs, an error component displays the message field (if present) or a generic error, and the form is re-enabled for correction and resubmission.
"View this pledge in My Dashboard": Clicking this control on the confirmation component navigates to dashboard.html.

Page refresh with pledge state: If the URL contains any pledge-related query parameter defined by this page (e.g., indicating a recently created pledge), the page uses that information in combination with getConservationProjectDetails.user_state to decide whether to auto-display the pledge confirmation or just the status banner.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the projectId query parameter in the page URL. On every page load and whenever projectId changes, the page calls getConservationProjectDetails(projectId) and refreshes all project-related UI components. If projectId is missing or empty, the page shows an error state and does not attempt the API call. The getConservationProjectDetails response populates three main state branches: project (core project data), display (derived labels for category, transparency, and suggested monthly donation), and user_state (flag and details for any active pledge). The presence of user_state.has_active_pledge controls visibility of the pledge status banner and can influence whether the pledge form shows informational messaging. The pledge form maintains its own local form state (name, email, amount, frequency), which is reset after a successful pledge creation. When createDonationPledge successfully returns, the returned pledge object is stored in a dedicated in-memory state slice for the current page; optionally, the page may append a query parameter such as pledgeCreated or pledgeId to the URL using the History API to allow the confirmation state to persist across refreshes without using localStorage. No state for this page is persisted in localStorage or similar APIs; all durable state comes from URL parameters and the server-side user_state and pledge data returned via getConservationProjectDetails and createDonationPledge.

components.functionality: Reads projectId from the URL query string, calls getConservationProjectDetails(projectId), and exposes loading, error, project, display, and user_state data to child components. Handles re-loading when projectId changes.
Displays the project's primary identifying information including name and short_description, along with the category_label from the display object. Provides a "Back to projects" navigation control.
Shows key metadata for the project such as geographic_focus, transparency_rating with transparency_label, impact_score, and an optional external website link if website_url is provided.
Displays the project's detailed_description if available. If detailed_description is missing or empty, it can fall back to showing short_description only.

Indicates whether the user currently has an active pledge for this project. When user_state.has_active_pledge is true, shows summary information such as active_pledge.amount, active_pledge.frequency, and active_pledge.status so the user can see their existing commitment.

Collects donor_name, donor_email, amount, and frequency for a pledge tied to the current project. Performs client-side validation, then calls createDonationPledge(projectId, donor_name, donor_email, amount, frequency). Uses suggested_monthly_donation and suggested_monthly_label for non-binding guidance. On success, updates local pledge state and triggers display of the pledge-confirmation-panel.

Shown after a successful createDonationPledge call. Summarizes the newly created pledge using the returned pledge object (amount, frequency, status, start_date, created_at). Provides an action to view the pledge in the dashboard and may also provide an action to dismiss the confirmation or start another pledge flow.

Centralizes navigation actions for this page, including navigating back to projects.html and to dashboard.html after pledge creation. May be used by other components (e.g., project-header, pledge-confirmation-panel) to perform routing.

Displays error messages for the page, including failures from getConservationProjectDetails (e.g., missing projectId, project not found, or API errors) and generic fallbacks when detailed messages are not available.
Shows a loading state while getConservationProjectDetails is in progress and hides when the project data has loaded or an error has occurred.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, max-width layout based on a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters (desktop breakpoint). Content appears within a central column spanning roughly 8–10 grid columns, with generous side margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom flow anchored by a full-width hero panel for the project summary. Immediately under the site header, a pale green hero section houses the project-header: the project title (H1) and a concise intro paragraph in the upper portion, with a wide hero image or visual (if present in that component) directly beneath within the same section. Below the hero, the rest of the page follows as stacked editorial sections: project-meta-info, project-description-section, then a dedicated donation area composed of pledge-status-banner and donation-pledge-form (with pledge-confirmation-panel occupying the same area after success), ending with navigation-links at the bottom. Data-loading, loading-indicator, and error-notification components appear above or over this flow to communicate state without breaking the primary vertical rhythm.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom flow anchored by a full-width hero panel for the project summary. Immediately under the site header, a pale green hero section houses the project-header: the project title (H1) and a concise intro paragraph in the upper portion, with a wide hero image or visual (if present in that component) directly beneath within the same section. Below the hero, the rest of the page follows as stacked editorial sections: project-meta-info, project-description-section, then a dedicated donation area composed of pledge-status-banner and donation-pledge-form (with pledge-confirmation-panel occupying the same area after success), ending with navigation-links at the bottom. Data-loading, loading-indicator, and error-notification components appear above or over this flow to communicate state without breaking the primary vertical rhythm.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Adopt a centered content column within a full-width page, roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, capped at ~1120–1200px max-width, with generous side gutters (~10–15% per side). The hero panel occupies the top 60–70% of the initial viewport: about the upper 35–40% for heading and intro, and the lower 25–30% for the hero visual. Each major section below the hero (meta, description, donation, navigation) is separated by 48–64px of vertical whitespace. Internal component padding is typically 24px top/bottom and 32px left/right on desktop, ensuring textual content never touches edges. The donation area is visually substantial but not overwhelming—roughly one viewport height combined for banner + form + confirmation.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, editorial feel: short line lengths (~60–75 characters), high line height, and ample whitespace around and within blocks. Text elements (titles, paragraphs, labels) are given clear breathing room, with 24–32px vertical gaps between headings and their associated text. Interactive elements (form fields, buttons) are grouped but spaced with 16–24px gaps so the donation process feels calm and considered rather than transactional or crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve an F-shaped scanning pattern: the eye starts at the top-left logo and traverses the top navigation, then drops to the large project title at the left of the central column, continues down to the intro paragraph, then to the hero image. From there, attention naturally moves down through the project-meta-info and project-description-section, before reaching the pledge-status-banner and donation-pledge-form, which serve as the main action area but remain consistent with the quiet environmental tone. The pledge-confirmation-panel replaces or overlays the form in the same vertical region, keeping the user anchored. The page-error-notification appears near the top of the content column, while the project-loading-indicator can temporarily center itself in the viewport, both briefly interrupting but clearly aligning with the central column.

layout.overall_description: The Project Detail page is organized as a calm, editorial experience on a soft, nature-inspired canvas. A slim site header with logo on the left and understated navigation on the right spans the full width at the top. Directly beneath, a full-width pale green hero panel stretches across the page, but its content is constrained to a centered column. In this column, the project title appears in large, left-aligned type near the top, followed by a generous gap and a multi-line intro paragraph. Below this text, still within the hero panel, a wide, edge-to-edge project image or visual fills the lower part of the hero, grounding the story visually. As you scroll down, the background returns to a very light neutral, and the main content continues in the same central column: first a compact metadata panel card, then a spacious narrative description section. Further down, a dedicated donation area appears as a clean panel: a subtle status banner followed by a generous, well-spaced pledge form card; upon successful pledge, a confirmation panel occupies this same region. At the very bottom of the column, simple navigation links allow users to return to the project list or move between projects. Error and loading states appear either as slim bands near the top of the content column or as centered overlays, always aligned to the same central grid, preserving the quiet, structured, nature-focused mood throughout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and conceptually wraps the entire main content area below the global site header. It occupies the same central column as the other components and controls whether the project-loading-indicator, page-error-notification, or the full stack of content (project-header through navigation-links) is rendered. Spatially, you can think of it as spanning the full width of the content column from just below the header to the bottom of the page, without adding its own padding or spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logical wrapper; not rendered visually. It dictates which visual components appear but does not occupy layout space itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned immediately beneath the global navigation, the project-header forms the primary hero section and sets the overall tone. It is contained within a full-width pale green background band that stretches edge-to-edge across the viewport, while its content is centered in a max-width column (about 70–80% of the page width, with large side gutters). Inside this column, the project title (H1) sits near the top left of the hero with a generous 48–64px margin from the site header. Directly under the H1, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space, a multi-line intro paragraph spans roughly 60%–70% of the column width, resulting in short, readable lines with ample line height. Below the intro text, after another 32–40px gap, a wide project visual (image, map, or illustration, if included in this component) spans almost the full width of the content column, with subtle rounded corners and perhaps a soft shadow, visually occupying the lower 40–50% of the hero panel in the initial viewport. The overall height of this hero section on desktop typically fills 60–70% of the first screen before scrolling, with generous top and bottom padding (48–64px) to keep the layout airy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section that scrolls with the page. The pale green background extends full width; the inner content is constrained to the centered column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-meta-info appears directly beneath the hero (project-header), with a clear 48–64px vertical gap that visually separates the green hero panel from the neutral main content area below. It is placed in the same centered content column, spanning roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, and rendered as a light, bordered or subtly elevated card. The card has 24px top/bottom and 32px left/right padding. Inside, metadata such as location, project category, timeline, and key metrics are arranged in a loose grid of rows and columns, but kept low-density: labels and values are grouped with 8–16px gaps and 24px vertical separation between rows, aligning left within the column. The card’s height is moderate—visually about 1/5–1/4 of the viewport height—so it feels like a concise summary that bridges the hero and the deeper content below. A 32–48px space beneath this card leads into the project-description-section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block card within the central column; scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-description-section sits directly below the project-meta-info card, separated by about 32–48px of vertical whitespace. It occupies the same centered column and extends across most of its width (around 70–80% of the viewport), with no heavy framing—just a soft background color (very light neutral or white) that blends with the page. Internally, it uses 24–32px top and bottom padding to give the text room to breathe. A section heading (e.g., “About this project” or similar) aligns left, with 24–32px space before the first paragraph. The main body text can run multiple paragraphs; each paragraph is separated by 16–24px of vertical space, and the line length is intentionally limited (around 60–75 characters) to maintain an editorial reading feel. The overall height of this section may span 1–2 viewport heights depending on content length, but whitespace at the top and bottom ensures it never feels dense. A generous 48–64px gap below this section leads the eye to the donation area (pledge-status-banner and donation-pledge-form).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard flowing content block within the central column; scrolls naturally with no fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pledge-status-banner introduces the donation area and appears directly below the project-description-section, separated by about 48px of vertical space to establish it as a distinct but related section. It spans the full width of the central content column (same width as the description), but is relatively shallow in height—approximately 1/8–1/10 of the viewport height. Visually it reads as a slim, horizontal band or pill-shaped card with a slightly tinted background (perhaps a soft green or blue accent) that contrasts gently against the page. Inside, left-aligned text succinctly summarizes the funding status (e.g., amount pledged, goal, timeline), with a simple horizontal progress indicator or concise stat cluster. Content inside the banner is spaced with 16–24px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. There is a 24–32px gap below the banner leading into the more substantial donation-pledge-form, making the banner feel like a preface rather than a dominant element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline horizontal band within the central column; scrolls with the page and does not stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The donation-pledge-form sits immediately below the pledge-status-banner, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical space, forming the main interaction area for pledging. It appears as a substantial yet calm card within the same centered content column, spanning roughly 70–80% of the viewport width with a capped max-width for comfortable reading. The card has generous internal padding—around 32px top/bottom and 32–40px left/right—to keep fields and labels from feeling crowded. At the top of the card, a concise section title and supporting line (e.g., “Support this project” with a short, neutral explanation) are left-aligned, with a 24px gap before the form fields. Fields (amount selection, frequency toggles, contact info) are arranged in a single-column layout to maintain clarity and low visual density, each field group separated by 16–24px vertically. Primary buttons (e.g., “Create Pledge”) are placed toward the bottom right of the card but still within the central column’s alignment, with at least 24px space above them. The overall height of the form card is roughly 1/3–1/2 of the viewport height on desktop, depending on field count, and is followed by a 48–64px vertical gap before any subsequent content (such as the navigation-links at the page’s end).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block card in the central column. No sticky behavior; the entire form scrolls naturally as part of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pledge-confirmation-panel occupies the same spatial region as the donation-pledge-form and is shown after a successful pledge. It aligns to the same central column, with identical width and outer margins to the form card, reinforcing the sense of continuity. The panel’s overall dimensions are similar to or slightly shorter than the form, maintaining the same 32px top/bottom and 32–40px left/right padding. Internally, a confirmation icon or subtle visual (such as a small checkmark or minimal illustration) appears toward the top-left, followed by a bold confirmation heading (e.g., “Pledge created”) and a short paragraph explaining what happens next. Content elements are spaced vertically by 16–24px. If any summary of the pledge details is presented, it is laid out as a simple list or compact card within the panel, aligned left and separated with light dividing lines or spacing. The panel replaces the form inline, so the vertical flow of the page remains unchanged: project-description-section → pledge-status-banner → confirmation-panel → navigation-links. There is a 48–64px gap below the confirmation panel before the navigation-links.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same block position as the donation-pledge-form. It may either fully replace the form in the DOM or overlay it, but visually it reads as a standalone card within the central column and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-links component is positioned at the very bottom of the main content, after the donation area (either beneath the donation-pledge-form or the pledge-confirmation-panel, depending on state), with a 48–64px vertical gap separating it from the card above. It resides in the same centered column but uses a lighter, more minimal presentation: typically a horizontal row of text links (e.g., “Back to all projects”, “Next project”) or a combination of a left-aligned primary link and a right-aligned secondary link. The links are spaced 16–24px apart horizontally, with minimal additional decoration—perhaps simple underlines on hover—so they feel like quiet wayfinding rather than calls-to-action. The component’s vertical footprint is small, around 1/10 of the viewport height or less, with 24px padding above and below to keep it from feeling cramped against the page’s end. It visually signals the conclusion of the page’s narrative while maintaining the clean editorial style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline navigation component at the end of the central column; scrolls normally and does not stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-error-notification appears at the top of the main content column, directly below the global site header and above the project-header hero, or—if the error is specific to a sub-section—immediately above the affected component. In the primary page-level error case, it spans the width of the central column but is visually set off as a horizontal band or alert card with a muted but noticeable color (e.g., soft red or amber) against the pale background. The alert has modest vertical height, roughly 1/12–1/10 of the viewport height, with 16–24px padding on all sides. The error message text is left-aligned and may include a short title in bold followed by a single paragraph of explanation. Any action (e.g., “Retry”) is placed inline or on the right but kept visually minimal. A 24–32px gap separates this alert from the next component below (such as the project-header). The alert should respect the same central alignment and side gutters as other content, maintaining the editorial feel even when an error occurs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline dismissible band within the central column. It scrolls with the page; if multiple errors can occur, they stack vertically with consistent spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the project data is loading, the project-loading-indicator appears over the main content area, centered both horizontally and vertically within the viewport, while the background remains the pale page color or shows skeletal placeholders. The indicator itself is compact—about 1/8–1/6 of the viewport height and width—a simple spinner or subtle animated motif with a short loading label beneath it. It does not stretch full width; instead, it is anchored to the central column, so on large screens it appears aligned with the content column rather than at the absolute center of the browser window’s width. Surrounding it is ample negative space, reinforcing the calm, non-intrusive feel. If skeleton UI is used, the hero’s approximate shapes (title bar, paragraph lines, hero image block) mirror the final layout but with soft, greyed-out rectangles and 16–24px spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces the main content region while data is loading. It remains centered in the viewport and scroll is typically disabled or unnecessary until loading completes.

name: Articles & Guides
title: Articles & Guides
description: Search, filter, sort, and bookmark educational articles about environmental conservation topics.

core_features: Keyword-based article search using the searchArticles interface, with the query synchronized to the URL parameter "query".
Configurable filters for topic, difficulty level, publication date range, and maximum reading time, populated dynamically via getArticleFilterOptions and applied via searchArticles.

Sorting controls for ordering results (e.g., by recency, relevance, or popularity) using sort options from getArticleFilterOptions and applied via the searchArticles sort_by parameter, synchronized with the URL parameter "sort".
Paginated article results using searchArticles page and page_size parameters, with the current page reflected in the URL (e.g., "page" and optionally "pageSize").
Per-article actions to bookmark using bookmarkArticle, updating the is_bookmarked state on each result item.
Navigation from a result item to the article detail page (article_detail.html?articleId=...) using the article_id returned by searchArticles.
Navigation from the page to the My Reading Lists page (my_reading_lists.html) for managing saved reading lists.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Arrive from another page with pre-set filters
1. User clicks a link on index.html or dashboard.html that points to articles.html with one or more URL parameters (e.g., query, topic, difficulty, maxReadingTime, dateRange, sort).
2. Articles & Guides page reads all URL parameters on load.
3. Page calls getArticleFilterOptions to retrieve the available topics, difficulty levels, reading time presets, date range presets, and sort options.
4. Page sets the initial state of search query, filters, and sort based on the URL parameters and the returned filter options (e.g., selecting the matching topic, difficulty, or date range key if present).
5. Page determines the current page and page size from URL parameters (if present) or application defaults.
6. Page calls searchArticles with the current query, a filters object constructed from the active filter selections, the selected sort key, and the derived page and page_size.
7. Page displays the results list using the returned results, total_count, page, and page_size values; each result shows its is_bookmarked state.
8. User optionally adjusts filters, query, or sort, triggering updates to the URL and subsequent searchArticles calls.

Workflow 2: Search by keyword from within the page
1. User focuses the search input in the Articles & Guides page and types a keyword phrase.
2. User submits the search via the search form (e.g., pressing Enter or clicking a search button).
3. The page updates or sets the "query" URL parameter to the entered text, resets the "page" parameter to the first page, and leaves other filter-related URL parameters unchanged.
4. The page calls searchArticles with the updated query, the current filters, current sort_by, and page reset to the first page.
5. The results list updates to reflect the new search results and total_count.
6. If the user modifies the search again and resubmits, steps 2–5 repeat.

Workflow 3: Apply or change filters
1. User opens or views the filter panel, which has been populated with options from getArticleFilterOptions.
2. User changes one or more filters (e.g., selects a topic, adjusts difficulty, selects a date range preset, or sets a maximum reading time via a preset or numeric input).
3. For each change, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters (topic, difficulty, dateRange, maxReadingTime) to reflect the new filter state; if a filter is cleared, its URL parameter is removed.
4. The page resets the "page" parameter to the first page.
5. The page constructs a filters object from the current filter state and calls searchArticles with the unchanged query, updated filters, current sort_by, and the first page.
6. The results list and the total_count are refreshed based on the filtered searchArticles response.

Workflow 4: Change sorting
1. User opens the sort control and selects a different sort option from those provided by getArticleFilterOptions.sort_options.
2. The page updates the "sort" URL parameter to the selected sort key and resets "page" to the first page.
3. The page calls searchArticles using the current query, current filters, the new sort_by value, and the first page.
4. The results list is re-rendered in the new order while preserving current filters and search query.

Workflow 5: Paginate through results
1. After a search, the results include pagination controls based on total_count, page, and page_size from searchArticles.
2. User clicks a pagination control (e.g., next page, previous page, or a specific page number).
3. The page updates the "page" URL parameter (and optionally "pageSize" if user-selectable) to the new page value.
4. The page calls searchArticles with the current query, filters, sort_by, and the new page and page_size.
5. The results list updates to show the articles for the selected page.

Workflow 6: Bookmark an article
1. User identifies an article in the results with a bookmark control that reflects its current is_bookmarked state.
2. User clicks the bookmark control for a specific article.
3. The page calls bookmarkArticle with the corresponding articleId.
4. When bookmarkArticle returns, the page checks the success and already_bookmarked flags:
   - If success is true, the page updates that article's is_bookmarked state in the current results dataset to true.
   - If already_bookmarked is true, the page may still ensure the UI reflects a bookmarked state but does not need to alter URL parameters.
   - If success is false, the page can display the message from the response and leave the UI state unchanged.
5. User can repeat for other articles; each bookmark action is independent and does not require a full searchArticles refresh unless desired by the implementation.

Workflow 7: Open article details
1. User clicks on an article title or card area in the results list.
2. The page reads the article_id associated with that result.
3. The browser navigates to article_detail.html with a query string parameter articleId set to that article_id (e.g., article_detail.html?articleId=<article_id_value>).
4. The article_detail.html page then uses that articleId (outside the scope of this specification) to display the full article content.

Workflow 8: Go to My Reading Lists
1. User locates the "My Reading Lists" navigation link provided on the Articles & Guides page.
2. User clicks the link.
3. The browser navigates to my_reading_lists.html without additional parameters (or with application-defined parameters, if needed outside this spec).
4. The user manages reading lists there; returning to Articles & Guides can be done via site navigation.
interactions: Typing into the search input updates the internal query state; submitting the form updates the "query" URL parameter and triggers a searchArticles call.
Changing a topic selection in the filter panel updates the "topic" URL parameter, resets "page" to the first page, and triggers searchArticles with updated filters.
Changing the difficulty filter updates the "difficulty" URL parameter and re-runs searchArticles.
Selecting a date range preset updates the "dateRange" URL parameter and re-runs searchArticles.
Selecting or adjusting a maximum reading time updates the "maxReadingTime" URL parameter and re-runs searchArticles.
Selecting a sort option updates the "sort" URL parameter, resets the "page" parameter, and triggers searchArticles with a new sort_by value.
Clicking pagination controls updates the "page" URL parameter (and optional "pageSize") and triggers a new searchArticles call.
Clicking an article title or card navigates to article_detail.html with articleId set to the clicked article's identifier.
Clicking an article's bookmark button calls bookmarkArticle with that article's identifier and, on success, updates the article's is_bookmarked state in the results.
Clicking the "My Reading Lists" link navigates to my_reading_lists.html.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Articles & Guides page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for the current search and filter state. On initial load, the page parses the following URL parameters: "query", "topic", "difficulty", "maxReadingTime", "dateRange", "sort", and optionally "page" and "pageSize". These values are used to construct the initial UI state and searchArticles request.

1) Initial State Derivation:
- query: If present, sets the search input value and is passed as the query argument to searchArticles. If absent, the search input is empty and searchArticles receives an empty or null query value.
- topic: If present, the filter panel selects the matching topic option (if one matches a topic value retrieved from getArticleFilterOptions.topics). This value is also included in the filters object passed to searchArticles.
- difficulty: If present, the difficulty filter is set to this value, assuming it matches one of getArticleFilterOptions.difficulty_levels. It is also included in filters for searchArticles.
- maxReadingTime: If present, it is treated as the maximum reading time in minutes. The filter panel may map this to a preset (if one matches getArticleFilterOptions.reading_time_presets.max_minutes) or treat it as a custom numeric value. It is passed within filters to searchArticles.
- dateRange: If present, it is matched to a date_range_presets.key from getArticleFilterOptions. The matching preset is selected in the filter panel and the key is included in filters.
- sort: If present, it is matched to a sort_options.key from getArticleFilterOptions. If no matching key is found, the implementation can fall back to a default (such as the first available sort option). The resulting key is used as sort_by in searchArticles.
- page and pageSize: If present, they are parsed as numbers; if absent, the implementation uses configured defaults. These values are passed to searchArticles as page and page_size.

2) Building the filters Object for searchArticles:
The filters argument to searchArticles is constructed from current UI/URL state, for example:
- filters.topic is set if a topic is selected.
- filters.difficulty is set if a difficulty is selected.
- filters.maxReadingTime is set if a maximum reading time is specified.
- filters.dateRange is set if a date range preset is selected.
Any filters that are not set are omitted or set to null, depending on the interface expectations.

3) URL Synchronization Rules:
- Any change in search input submission, topic, difficulty, maxReadingTime, dateRange, or sort updates the corresponding URL parameters. If a filter is cleared, its URL parameter is removed.
- Each filter or sort change also resets "page" to represent the first page.
- Pagination changes update only the "page" parameter (and optionally "pageSize" if the user changes that setting) without altering query or filters.
- After the URL is updated, the page immediately issues a searchArticles call reflecting the new state. The internal UI state is always derived from the current URL parameters so that refreshing the page or sharing the URL reproduces the same search results.

4) Interplay with getArticleFilterOptions and bookmarkArticle:
- getArticleFilterOptions is called once on page load (and optionally on refresh) to populate filter controls and sort options; its results influence how URL parameter values are mapped to specific filter/sort items.
- bookmarkArticle does not modify URL parameters. Instead, its success response is used to update the in-memory representation of the corresponding result item's is_bookmarked state, so the UI reflects bookmark changes without altering the search state.

Through this logic, the combination of URL parameters, getArticleFilterOptions data, and searchArticles results fully defines the visible state of the Articles & Guides page at any time.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, state management, and communication between the search form, filter panel, sort controls, results list, pagination, and bookmark actions.
Provides a text input for the user to enter a keyword query, displays the current query derived from the URL, and triggers a new search when submitted.

Displays selectable filters for topic, difficulty level, publication date range presets, and maximum reading time. It is populated using getArticleFilterOptions, maps current URL parameters to selected options, and notifies the controller when filters change.

Displays the available sort options retrieved from getArticleFilterOptions.sort_options, shows the currently active sort derived from the URL parameter "sort", and triggers resorting when the user selects a new option.
Shows a concise summary of currently active filters (topic, difficulty, date range, maximum reading time) based on URL parameters and allows users to clear individual filters or all filters.

Renders the list of article results returned by searchArticles, including each article's title, subtitle, topic, summary, publication date, reading time, difficulty level, featured state, and bookmark state. Delegates bookmark actions and navigation to article_detail.html when an item is clicked.

Represents a single article within the results list, showing core article fields and an interactive bookmark control. Clicking the main area triggers navigation to the article detail page; clicking the bookmark control triggers the bookmarkArticle action through the controller.
Displays pagination elements based on total_count, page, and page_size from searchArticles, and notifies the controller when the user requests a different page or page size.
Provides a direct navigation link to the My Reading Lists page so that users can manage grouped saved articles; has no data dependency beyond standard navigation.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, max-width layout based on a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters (desktop breakpoint). Content appears within a central column spanning roughly 8–10 grid columns, with generous side margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a vertically stacked, editorial-style flow. At the top of the main content (inside the page controller), place a calm, spacious header section with the page title 'Articles & Guides' and a short descriptive paragraph, mirroring the mockup’s title + intro block. Directly beneath this, center a prominent search form that spans most of the central column. Below the search, introduce a slim controls band that holds the active filter summary on the left and the sort control on the right. Under that band, create a two-column content area: a light, unobtrusive filter panel on the left and the article results list on the right. Pagination sits below the list, centered. The Reading Lists link appears as a subtle, right-aligned text link in the header area, visually secondary to the page title.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a vertically stacked, editorial-style flow. At the top of the main content (inside the page controller), place a calm, spacious header section with the page title 'Articles & Guides' and a short descriptive paragraph, mirroring the mockup’s title + intro block. Directly beneath this, center a prominent search form that spans most of the central column. Below the search, introduce a slim controls band that holds the active filter summary on the left and the sort control on the right. Under that band, create a two-column content area: a light, unobtrusive filter panel on the left and the article results list on the right. Pagination sits below the list, centered. The Reading Lists link appears as a subtle, right-aligned text link in the header area, visually secondary to the page title.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain wide outer gutters so the main content sits in a centered column spanning roughly 70–80% of the viewport width (around 8–10 columns of a 12-column grid). The header (title + description) plus search form should consume about the top 35–40% of the first viewport, with generous top padding after the global navigation and ample spacing between the title, description, and search. The controls band (filter summary + sort) occupies a shallow strip with comfortable 16–24px padding above and below. The filter panel and results list share the main vertical area: the filter panel uses roughly 25–30% of the central column width, while the results list uses the remaining 70–75%. Vertical spacing between major sections is in the 48–64px range, and between related elements (e.g., between search and controls band, between controls and list) in the 24–32px range, following the spacing system.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep density low and airy. Each component has generous internal padding (around 16–24px) and high line height, with no overly compact clusters of controls. The search area is allowed to be visually dominant but not crowded. The filter panel displays a modest number of options at once with clear separation, avoiding a dense, faceted-search look. Each article item reads like a small editorial card: large title, readable summary, and ample white space around text. Elements are allowed to run across multiple short lines with comfortable line spacing.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Reinforce an F-pattern. The eye starts at the top-left with the site logo (in the global nav), then crosses to the right across the subtle navigation. Below, the eye drops to the large left-aligned 'Articles & Guides' heading, down into the descriptive paragraph, then lower to the central search form. From there, the eye moves into the controls band, scanning left-to-right across active filters and over to the sort control on the right. Then it drops into the article list on the right-hand side of the content area, scanning vertically through article cards. The filter panel on the left is present but visually softer so it’s perceived as a supportive column. The Reading Lists link is a light secondary focal point in the top-right of the header band, similar in hierarchy to the accessibility button in the mockup: noticeable but not competing with the main content.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a calm, centered editorial layout on a very light, nature-tinted background. A slim global navigation bar runs full-width at the top with the logo on the left and primary navigation links on the right, occupying minimal vertical space. Below this, the main content area sits within a wide, centered column framed by generous left and right gutters. The top of this column opens with a spacious header block: the page title 'Articles & Guides' appears large and left-aligned, with a short, two- to three-line descriptive paragraph below it, all resting on a subtly pale green or very light neutral panel that echoes the mockup’s Biodiversity hero section. On the same horizontal line as the title but aligned to the far right, a small, understated 'Reading Lists' link provides a quiet navigational affordance. Beneath the introductory copy, a single, wide search bar sits centered in the column, visually anchoring the functionality of the page without feeling cramped. A bit lower, a slim horizontal band presents active filter chips left-aligned and a sort dropdown right-aligned, acting as a soft divider between the header and the browsing area. Below this band, the layout opens into a light two-column arrangement: a narrow, softly colored filter panel on the left and a taller column of article cards on the right. Each article card spans the full width of its column and reads like a compact editorial feature, with plenty of breathing room between cards. At the bottom of the list, centered pagination controls provide gentle next/previous navigation without heavy chrome. The overall effect is a quiet, structured, and readable page where search and filtering tools support, rather than overpower, a serene content-first experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller spans the full viewport width and acts as the main scaffold for the content below the global navigation bar. It introduces a centered content column with a max width of roughly 1120–1200px, leaving wide gutters (about 10–12% of the viewport width on each side) to echo the mockup’s spacious framing. Top padding of approximately 64px creates a comfortable gap between the bottom of the navigation and the start of the 'Articles & Guides' header block. The main content area inside the controller is divided vertically into: (1) a top header zone (title, description, reading lists link) occupying about 30–35% of the first viewport height, (2) a search and controls zone (search form and controls band) occupying another 10–15%, and (3) a browsing zone containing the filter panel, results list, and pagination that extends downward. The background for the main content can be a very light neutral, with the header zone optionally sitting on a slightly pale green panel that runs full width within the central column, mirroring the mockup’s hero panel. Vertical spacing between each zone follows the 24–64px rhythm so no transitions feel abrupt.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered container that defines the grid and padding for all subordinate components; scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form is positioned just below the introductory header text within the central column, visually aligned with the left edge of the 'Articles & Guides' heading but horizontally centered overall because of the wide gutters. It sits about 32px beneath the descriptive paragraph, respecting the mockup’s generous gap between heading and body text. The search bar itself spans roughly 60–70% of the central column width on desktop, creating a wide but not full-width input that feels substantial without touching the edges. The field is a single-line input with a calm, minimal border and ample internal horizontal padding (16–24px) so the text does not feel crowded. Above and below the search form, leave approximately 24–32px of vertical breathing room before the next components (the header above and the filter/ sort controls below) to maintain the low-density editorial tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the main column; it expands or contracts with the central column but maintains centered alignment and proportional margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter panel appears in the browsing zone directly beneath the search and controls band, occupying the left side of the main content area inside the central column. On desktop, it takes approximately 25–30% of the central column width (e.g., around 280–320px), starting flush with the left edge of the column and extending downward. It is rendered as a tall, soft card or panel with a very light green or neutral background and a subtle border or shadow, visually similar in lightness to the hero panel from the mockup but more compact. Internal padding is around 24px on all sides to give filter labels and controls ample room. Filter groups stack vertically with 16–24px spacing between them, making the panel look airy and scannable rather than dense. To the right, a comfortable gap of 24–32px separates the panel from the article results list column, ensuring the panel reads as a supporting sidebar rather than a heavy block.
