The panel extends only as far as its content; it does not stretch full height of the screen, keeping the overall layout light.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static column element that scrolls with the page. On narrower viewports it can collapse into an overlay or off-canvas panel invoked via a filter button, but on desktop it remains visible in place.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control sits in a horizontal controls band directly below the search form and above the filter/results columns. This band spans the full width of the central content column but remains shallow, with about 16–24px of vertical padding. Within this band, the sort control is right-aligned, mirroring the mockup’s right-side navigation in the header. It might appear as a simple dropdown labeled 'Sort by' or a compact segmented control. The control’s width is modest (roughly 160–220px), leaving plenty of empty space around it to avoid crowding. On the left side of the same band, the active filter summary is aligned, creating a subtle left-to-right visual line across the page. The band itself does not have a heavy background; at most, a faint dividing line above or below keeps a clean editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element within the controls band, aligned to the right and maintaining its position as the page scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active filter summary occupies the left side of the same horizontal controls band that contains the sort control. It is positioned immediately below the search form, with about 24px of vertical gap separating it from the bottom of the search area. The summary is left-aligned with the main content column, starting at the same x-position as the page title and search input, and can span up to about 60% of the column width. Individual filters are represented as small, rounded, pill-shaped chips with light outlines and muted green or neutral backgrounds, spaced 8–12px apart horizontally and 8px vertically when wrapping onto multiple lines. This arrangement provides a soft, editorial row of tags rather than a heavy filter bar. The right edge of this group naturally leaves open space between it and the right-aligned sort control, preserving the airy feel of the band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline row that wraps gracefully within the controls band; it grows taller if many filters are active but remains anchored to the left.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article results list begins directly to the right of the filter panel and below the controls band. It forms the dominant column on the right side of the browsing zone, occupying roughly 70–75% of the central column width on desktop. There is a clear vertical alignment: the left edge of the first article card lines up with the right edge of the filter panel plus a comfortable horizontal gutter of about 24–32px. The list is displayed as a single column of full-width article cards (result items) stacked vertically, with 24–32px of vertical spacing between them. The top of the list sits approximately 24px below the controls band, creating a distinct yet soft separation. The vertical flow invites the user to scroll down through cards like an editorial feed, and the background behind the list is the same light neutral as the rest of the content area, keeping the article cards themselves as the primary focal points.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable list that extends downward within the right-hand column; it does not use masonry or multi-column layouts to preserve readability.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each article result item is styled as a soft, editorial card within the results list column. It spans the full width of that right-hand column, with internal padding of approximately 24–32px on all sides to create a reading-friendly block. The card content is arranged in a simple vertical stack: at the top, a left-aligned article title set in a larger, bold type size; below it, a short 2–3 line summary paragraph with generous line height; and beneath that, a small row of metadata (e.g., category, reading time) and optional tags. If a thumbnail image is present, it appears either as a narrow strip above the title or as a small thumbnail on the left with text to the right, but always with enough margin so the text isn’t cramped. Cards are separated from each other by 24–32px of white space, and may have a very subtle border or shadow to distinguish them from the background without introducing visual noise. The overall feel is that each result is a small article teaser, not a dense search result row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block elements within the results list; they stack vertically and respond to the available width of the right-hand column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls sit directly beneath the last article result item in the list, within the same right-hand column. There is a generous gap of about 32–48px between the bottom of the final card and the pagination, allowing the list to feel complete before navigation appears. The controls are centered horizontally within the results column (not the entire viewport), echoing the centered feel of the search form above. The pagination UI itself is minimal: small numbered buttons with ample 8–12px horizontal spacing and 8–16px of internal padding, possibly with simple 'Previous' and 'Next' text links. The styling is light and unobtrusive, with a focus state that is clear but not loud. Below the controls, additional bottom padding (48–64px) ensures that the end of the page doesn’t feel abrupt when the user scrolls to the bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored at the bottom of the current results set; it moves down as more cards are inserted or as the viewport height changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading lists link is placed in the top header zone within the central content column, sharing the same horizontal line as the 'Articles & Guides' heading but aligned to the far right of that column. The heading occupies roughly the left 60–70% of the row, while the reading lists link sits in the remaining right portion with ample empty space between them. The link is styled as quiet text—possibly with a small bookmark icon—using a subtle color that contrasts gently with the background but does not compete with the main heading. Its size is slightly smaller than body text or equal to body text, reinforcing its tertiary status. There is about 8–16px of padding above and below this row so it feels integrated with the header, and it maintains its alignment as the user scrolls past the hero area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline navigation element anchored to the top header region; it scrolls away with the header content and does not remain fixed.

name: Article Detail
title: Article Detail
description: Displays a single conservation article with bookmark and reading list controls, and navigation to related articles.

core_features: Load and display full article content and metadata based on the articleId URL parameter using getArticleDetails.
Show the user’s current bookmark state for the article and allow bookmarking via bookmarkArticle.
Display which reading lists already contain the article (from getArticleDetails.user_state.in_reading_lists).
Allow the user to open a selector of their reading lists (via getMyReadingListsOverview) and add the article to one or more lists using addArticleToReadingList.
Show related articles returned by getArticleDetails and allow navigation to their detail pages via article_detail.html?articleId={related_article_id}.
Provide navigation back to the main article listing (articles.html) and to the My Reading Lists page (my_reading_lists.html) after adding the article to lists.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View an article
1) User navigates to article_detail.html?articleId={id} from another page.
2) The page reads the articleId from the query string.
3) The page calls getArticleDetails(articleId).
4) While loading, the page shows a loading state and hides article content.
5) On success, the page displays the article title, metadata (publication_date, reading_time_minutes, difficulty_level, topic, tags, author_name), and full content.
6) If getArticleDetails fails or articleId is missing/invalid, the page shows an error state and a retry or navigation option instead of content.

Workflow: Bookmark an article
1) After the article loads, the actions toolbar shows current bookmark status based on user_state.is_bookmarked.
2) If the article is not bookmarked, the user can click a 'Bookmark' control.
3) The page calls bookmarkArticle(articleId).
4) While the request is in progress, the bookmark control is disabled to prevent duplicate clicks.
5) On success, the page updates local bookmark state to bookmarked, using the success and already_bookmarked flags, and may show the message from the response.
6) If bookmarkArticle indicates an error (success=false), the page shows an inline error and keeps the control enabled for retry.
7) If the user clicks the bookmark control again and bookmarkArticle returns already_bookmarked=true, the page simply confirms the existing state; unbookmarking is not performed on this page.

Workflow: Add an article to reading lists
1) After the article loads, the actions toolbar shows a 'Save to reading list' control.
2) When the user clicks it, a modal opens and triggers getMyReadingListsOverview() if the overview is not already loaded during this page view.
3) The modal lists all reading_lists from the response, indicating which lists already contain the article using user_state.in_reading_lists from getArticleDetails.
4) The user selects one or more reading lists (e.g., via checkboxes) to which the article should be added. Lists already containing the article may be shown as selected and non-editable or simply labeled as already added, depending on implementation, but no removal occurs.
5) On submitting the modal, the component calls addArticleToReadingList(articleId, readingListId) for each newly selected list that does not already contain the article.
6) For each successful response, the page updates its in-memory user_state.in_reading_lists to include that reading list and may use the message returned to display feedback.
7) After processing all calls, the modal shows a consolidated success message and reveals a 'Manage reading lists' link that navigates to my_reading_lists.html.
8) If any addArticleToReadingList call fails, the modal displays an inline error for that list and allows retry while retaining the user’s selections.

Workflow: Navigate between related articles and back to lists
1) After getArticleDetails returns, the page displays related_articles.
2) The user clicks a related article entry.
3) The browser navigates to article_detail.html?articleId={related_article_id}.
4) The new page load repeats the article loading workflow using the new articleId.
5) At any time, the user can click 'Back to articles' to navigate to articles.html.
6) After successfully adding the article to reading lists, the user can click 'Manage reading lists' to navigate to my_reading_lists.html to manage all lists.
interactions: On page load or when the URL changes, the page reads the articleId query parameter and triggers getArticleDetails(articleId) if present.
User clicks the 'Bookmark' button: triggers bookmarkArticle(articleId), updates bookmark state and shows success or error feedback.
User clicks the 'Save to reading list' button: opens the reading list selector modal and triggers getMyReadingListsOverview() if not yet fetched in this session.
Within the modal, user selects or deselects reading list options (for lists where the article is not yet present): these interactions update local selection state only.
User submits the reading list modal: triggers one or more addArticleToReadingList(articleId, readingListId) calls for newly selected lists, then updates in-page state and shows success messages.
User clicks 'Manage reading lists' in the modal after successful additions: navigates to my_reading_lists.html.
User clicks 'Back to articles': navigates to articles.html (no parameters required).
User clicks on a related article item: navigates to article_detail.html?articleId={related_article_id}, which reloads the article detail data for that article.

If getArticleDetails fails or articleId is absent, user can click a 'Retry' action to attempt getArticleDetails again (if articleId exists) or use navigation links (e.g., Back to articles) to leave the page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver for this page is the articleId URL query parameter (article_detail.html?articleId={id}). When the page loads or when articleId changes in the URL, the page calls getArticleDetails(articleId) and replaces any previously displayed article, bookmark, and related-articles state. If articleId is missing or empty, the page does not call getArticleDetails and instead shows an error or empty-state message with navigation options. The response from getArticleDetails provides: (1) article object: bound to all content and metadata components; (2) user_state.is_bookmarked and user_state.in_reading_lists: used to initialize bookmark controls and to mark which reading lists already contain the article; (3) related_articles: used by the related articles list component. Local UI state (e.g., loading, error flags, and reading list selections within the modal) is kept in memory for the current page view only and is not persisted across reloads or navigation. When bookmarkArticle and addArticleToReadingList are called, their responses are used immediately to update in-memory user_state (bookmark status and membership in reading lists) so that the UI remains consistent with the server without extra API calls. No localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used; any fresh page load depends solely on the current URL parameters and the data returned by the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Parses the articleId from the URL querystring and loads all article detail data. On initialization or whenever articleId changes, this component calls getArticleDetails(articleId). It manages loading and error states and exposes the resulting article, user_state, and related_articles data to child components.

Displays the current loading or error status for the page based on the state of getArticleDetails. While data is being fetched, it shows a loading indicator and hides dependent components. If articleId is missing or getArticleDetails fails, it shows an error message and provides a retry action (when articleId exists) and/or navigation options such as back to articles.

Shows the primary article header information: title, subtitle (if any), author_name (if provided), publication_date, reading_time_minutes, difficulty_level, topic, and key tags. This component updates whenever the Article data from getArticleDetails changes.
Displays the full article.content along with optional summary if available. It is rendered only after article data has loaded successfully and re-renders if a different articleId is loaded.

Provides core actions for the current article: (1) a 'Back to articles' navigation link to articles.html; (2) a bookmark control that reflects user_state.is_bookmarked and triggers bookmarking via bookmarkArticle; (3) a 'Save to reading list' control that opens the reading list selector modal; and (4) a small status summary indicating which reading lists currently include the article, based on user_state.in_reading_lists. The toolbar listens for state changes from bookmark and reading list actions to keep the displayed status current.

Encapsulates the logic for bookmarking the article. When invoked from the toolbar, it calls bookmarkArticle(articleId), disables the control while the request is in progress, and then updates the in-memory bookmark state based on the success and already_bookmarked flags. It exposes updated bookmark state back to the toolbar so the UI reflects the latest status. It handles and surfaces any error message from the API.

When opened from the actions toolbar, this modal loads the user’s reading lists by calling getMyReadingListsOverview() if the data is not already loaded in this page session. It presents all reading_lists with item_count and descriptive fields. Using user_state.in_reading_lists from getArticleDetails, it marks which lists already contain the current article (these can be displayed as pre-selected and non-removable, or labeled as already added). The user selects additional lists and submits the form. On submit, the component calls addArticleToReadingList(articleId, readingListId) for each newly selected list; successful calls update the page’s user_state.in_reading_lists in memory. After all calls complete, the modal shows overall success feedback and exposes a 'Manage reading lists' link that navigates to my_reading_lists.html.

Displays related articles for the current article using the related_articles array returned by getArticleDetails. Each related item shows its title and topic and is rendered as a link to article_detail.html?articleId={article_id}. When the user clicks a related article, the browser navigates using the new articleId, triggering the full article loading workflow for that target article.

Provides simple navigation links relevant to the article detail context. It includes: (1) a link back to articles.html (Back to articles), and (2) an optional link to my_reading_lists.html (Manage reading lists) that is shown after at least one successful addArticleToReadingList operation in the current session, so that users can proceed to manage their lists in more detail.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, max-width layout based on a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters (desktop breakpoint). Content appears within a central column spanning roughly 8–10 grid columns, with generous side margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow. A slim, full-width site header with logo on the left and navigation on the right sits at the very top. Below this, the article header summary occupies a large, pale green hero-like panel that takes up roughly the top 50–60% of the initial viewport, left-aligned within a centered max-width column. Immediately beneath this header panel, the main article content begins, with the opening hero image appearing first and spanning the content column, followed by the body copy. After the article body, a related-articles section appears in the same central column, and finally a low-profile navigation links strip sits at the bottom. Transient states (loader, error) and the reading-list modal sit above this stack as overlays, without altering the underlying vertical order.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow. A slim, full-width site header with logo on the left and navigation on the right sits at the very top. Below this, the article header summary occupies a large, pale green hero-like panel that takes up roughly the top 50–60% of the initial viewport, left-aligned within a centered max-width column. Immediately beneath this header panel, the main article content begins, with the opening hero image appearing first and spanning the content column, followed by the body copy. After the article body, a related-articles section appears in the same central column, and finally a low-profile navigation links strip sits at the bottom. Transient states (loader, error) and the reading-list modal sit above this stack as overlays, without altering the underlying vertical order.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The layout uses a centered max-width content column (approximately 70–80% of the page width), with wide gutters on each side (about 10–15% of the viewport width per side) to preserve an airy, editorial feel. Vertically, the article header summary (title + meta + short intro) in the pale green panel receives generous top and bottom padding (48–64px), occupying roughly the upper half of the first screen. The hero image, as part of the article main content, typically occupies 40–50% of the initial viewport height when visible. Section separations (header → content → related articles → navigation strip) use 48–64px vertical margins, while related elements within a section (heading → paragraph, paragraph → toolbar) use 16–32px spacing based on the 8px scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: All text is kept within a comfortable reading width to maintain low content density: line length around 60–70 characters, ample line height, and generous whitespace around sections. The article header summary presents only essential meta information and a short intro paragraph, avoiding clutter. The article actions toolbar is visually light, with small, well-spaced icons or compact buttons, and does not form a dense control cluster. Related articles are presented with breathing room between items rather than tightly packed grids. Overall, the design favors clarity and calm over information density, aligning with an environmental, editorial tone.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a vertical F-pattern. The eye starts at the top left with the site logo, scans across the top navigation, then drops down to the large article title in the pale green panel. From there it moves downward to the intro paragraph and metadata, then continues to the hero image just below. As the user scrolls, the eye flows down through the main article text, then to the related-articles section, and finally to the navigation links strip at the bottom. The article actions toolbar is aligned toward the right side of the main column near the top of the hero panel, providing a secondary horizontal focal point that the eye catches after reading the heading and intro. Overlays like the loader, error state, and reading-list modal temporarily interrupt the flow by centering content in the viewport, but they remain visually simple and aligned to the central axis.

layout.overall_description: The page reads like a calm, modern environmental article in a digital magazine. A slim, understated header with a logo on the left and subtle navigation on the right spans the full width at the very top. Below it, the main article area is framed by wide, empty gutters on both sides, bringing focus to a centered content column. This column begins with a large, pale green panel that fills most of the upper viewport: on its left, the article title and brief summary are stacked with generous vertical spacing, while a lightweight action toolbar aligns to the right edge of the same column. The typography is airy, with ample line height and short line lengths. As the user scrolls, the green panel gives way to a neutral background where a wide article hero image spans the content column, followed by the article body in a comfortable, single column of text. After the reading ends, a clean related-articles section appears in the same column, offering a handful of spaciously arranged article teasers. At the very bottom, a slim navigation strip with previous and next article links gently anchors the page. When loading, error, or reading-list interactions occur, they manifest as centered overlays on top of this structure, respecting the calm, minimalist editorial aesthetic and never feeling like heavy, commercial UI elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The initial loader appears as an overlay centered within the viewport, covering the entire main content area beneath the global header. It does not displace layout; instead, it floats above the pale green hero region and the central column. A soft, semi-translucent wash (very light green or gray at ~60–70% opacity) covers the content from just below the navigation bar down to the bottom of the viewport. At the center of the screen, aligned to the central vertical axis and within the same horizontal bounds as the main content column, a simple loader indicator (spinner or progress glyph) and a short status label are stacked vertically with 16–24px between them. Surrounding them is plenty of empty space, roughly 25–30% of the viewport width on each side, maintaining the minimalist density even during loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a fixed-position overlay that covers the main content region from below the global header to the bottom edge of the viewport. It is vertically and horizontally centered, does not scroll with the page, and prevents interaction with underlying components while visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When an error occurs, the error state occupies the same spatial region as the article content, presented within the central column directly beneath the global header. The background reverts to the normal page tone (light neutral or very pale green), and a compact error illustration or icon sits at the center of the content column, approximately 40% down from the top of the viewport. Below the icon, an error headline and short explanatory paragraph are stacked with 16–24px between them, followed by a right-sized action button (e.g., “Retry”) centered on the same axis with 24px of top margin. Left and right gutters remain wide and empty, giving the error message a sense of calm rather than urgency. Overall, the error block occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport height, surrounded by generous whitespace above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow, replacing where the article header and main content would appear. It scrolls with the page if content height exceeds the viewport, but typically appears fully within a single screen, with no overlays.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article header summary forms a large hero panel immediately below the global navigation, spanning the full width of the page background but constraining its content to the central max-width column. This panel uses a pale, nature-tinted green fill and includes generous vertical padding of 48–64px at the top and bottom. Inside the central column, the article title is left-aligned near the top, occupying roughly 60–70% of the column width, with enough space to break into two or three short lines. Beneath the title, a small block of metadata (such as category, date, reading time) is positioned, followed by a brief intro paragraph. The metadata and intro are separated by 8–16px; the title has 16–24px of space above the metadata. The entire text stack is offset from the top of the green panel by about 24–32px to avoid crowding. The rightmost 20–25% of the content column is kept visually light, offering room for the article actions toolbar to align without disturbing the clean text block, and there is a clear visual margin between the bottom of this panel and the beginning of the article hero image below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and at the top of the main content flow. It defines the initial viewport’s main focus and scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main content begins immediately beneath the article header summary, separated from it by a vertical gap of approximately 48px and transitioning from the pale green hero background to a neutral light background. It stays within the same central column width, with wide side gutters mirroring the header. At the very top of this component, a large article hero image is presented, spanning 100% of the content column’s width and maintaining a landscape aspect ratio; it typically occupies about 40–50% of the viewport height on first view, with 24–32px of whitespace above and below it. Below the image, the article body text flows in a single vertical column. Paragraphs are left-aligned, with a comfortable line length (60–70 characters), generous line height, and 16–24px spacing between paragraphs or section headings. Occasional inline media (additional images, pull quotes) align to the same column and do not break the visual calm—each is given 24–32px of top and bottom margin to keep the density low. The component extends as far down as the article content requires, ending with a clear bottom margin (48–64px) before the related-articles list begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content that scrolls naturally. It occupies the central column only and never breaks into sidebars or multi-column layouts on desktop, maintaining a singular editorial reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article actions toolbar is a compact horizontal strip of controls (bookmark, share, reading settings as needed) positioned within the pale green article header panel. On desktop, it is aligned to the right edge of the central content column, horizontally aligned with either the baseline of the metadata or slightly below the title, depending on space. It occupies roughly the rightmost 20–25% of the column width and has a height of about 40–48px. Icons or small buttons are arranged in a row, with 8–16px gaps between them, and ample negative space around the toolbar so it does not visually compete with the title. There is at least 24px padding from the toolbar to the top of the green panel and 16–24px to the right edge of the content column. On scroll, the toolbar may condense into a slimmer strip that lightly sticks to the top of the content column, preserving access to key actions without creating a heavy fixed header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially positioned statically within the article header summary. Optionally, once the user scrolls past the hero, it can adopt a subtle sticky behavior: a low-height, semi-translucent strip anchored to the top of the viewport, constrained horizontally to the central column, so that key actions remain accessible while not overwhelming the reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark action handler has no independent visible footprint; instead, its interactive zone is integrated into bookmark-related affordances within the article actions toolbar and possibly near the article title. The bookmark icon or control appears within the toolbar’s horizontal row on the right side of the header panel, using the same spacing and minimal iconography as neighboring actions. This keeps the visual focus on the article title and text while making bookmarking available at a glance. When hovered or focused, the bookmark control may show a subtle label or state change, but it always remains visually compact and aligned with the toolbar’s baseline.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logical component attached to visible controls in the article actions toolbar. It follows the toolbar’s spatial behavior (including any sticky state) and does not create additional layout elements of its own.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When triggered, the reading-list selector appears as a centered modal overlay above the entire page. A soft, semi-opaque backdrop dims the content from just below the global header to the bottom of the viewport, subtly shifting attention without feeling harsh. The modal itself is a white (or very light) card with gentle corner radius, horizontally centered in the viewport and positioned slightly above true vertical center so it feels anchored to the reading context. It occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width (e.g., 480–560px on desktop) and up to 50–60% of the viewport height if needed, with max-width constraints matching or slightly narrower than the main column. Inside, there is 24–32px padding on all sides. A concise title is placed at the top left of the card, with 16–24px below it before a scrollable list of available reading lists. Each list item is left-aligned and spaced 12–16px apart, preserving a clean, low-density feel. At the bottom, a primary action button and a secondary cancel option are right-aligned on the card’s lower edge, separated from the list by about 24px.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay anchored to the center of the viewport, with a full-screen dimmed backdrop that prevents interaction with underlying content. The modal content scrolls internally if it exceeds available height, while the page behind remains static.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-articles list sits below the main article content within the same central column, separated from the last paragraph by a generous 48–64px vertical gap. A subtle section heading like “Related articles” is left-aligned at the top of this component, matching the article’s typographic style but slightly smaller than the main title. The heading sits 16–24px above the list of related items. The items themselves are arranged in a low-density layout: on wider desktops, two cards per row within the central column, each card occupying roughly 48% of the column width with a 24–32px horizontal gap between them; on narrower widths, they stack in a single column. Each card includes a small thumbnail or color block at the top (or left), followed by the article title and a brief one- or two-line description, with 12–16px between these internal elements. Vertically, there is 24–32px of space between rows of cards. The entire related-articles component has 32–48px of bottom padding before the navigation links strip begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section at the bottom of the main content flow. It scrolls like normal content, staying within the central column and respecting the same side gutters and max-width constraints as the article body.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article navigation links strip anchors the bottom of the main content area, immediately following the related-articles list and separated from it by a vertical margin of about 32–48px. It spans the full width of the central column but sits on a subtly differentiated background (slightly lighter or with a faint top border) that visually separates it from the content above. The strip is relatively shallow, about 56–72px tall. Inside, the text links for navigation are placed on a single horizontal line: “Previous article” is left-aligned near the left edge of the content column, and “Next article” is right-aligned near the right edge, both with ample padding (16–24px) from the top and bottom edges of the strip. The typography is understated—smaller than the main body text—with perhaps a muted green accent color on hover, keeping this control obviously interactive but visually tertiary. There is generous whitespace between these links and any surrounding elements, emphasizing that this strip marks the end of the page content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the end of the document flow, acting as a footer-like navigation band within the main content column. It scrolls with the page and does not stick or float.

name: My Reading Lists
title: My Reading Lists
description: Manage your saved environmental articles by organizing them into named reading lists and open articles directly from your lists.

core_features: Load and display an overview of all reading lists with names, descriptions, item counts, and timestamps using getMyReadingListsOverview.
Load and display details of a selected reading list, including its articles and their metadata, using getReadingListDetails.
Load and display all bookmarked articles available to be organized into reading lists using getBookmarkedArticles.
Create new reading lists with a name and optional description using createReadingList.
Rename or update the description of an existing reading list using updateReadingList.
Delete an existing reading list and its article associations using deleteReadingList, with confirmation to prevent accidental loss.
Add a bookmarked article to a selected reading list via an "Add to List" control or drag-and-drop, using addArticleToReadingList.
Remove an article from a reading list using removeArticleFromReadingList.
Open article detail pages directly by clicking article titles within any reading list, navigating to article_detail.html with articleId in the URL.
Maintain the selected reading list via the readingListId URL parameter so users can deep-link to or return to a specific list.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Initial page load and list selection
1. User navigates to my_reading_lists.html, optionally with a readingListId query parameter.
2. Page-state controller parses the readingListId from the URL query string.
3. In parallel, the page calls getMyReadingListsOverview() and getBookmarkedArticles().
4. The reading-lists overview sidebar displays all lists returned by getMyReadingListsOverview.reading_lists.
5. If a readingListId is present in the URL and matches one of the overview entries, the page calls getReadingListDetails(readingListId) and sets that list as selected.
6. If no readingListId is present but at least one reading list exists, the first list in getMyReadingListsOverview.reading_lists is treated as the selected list, the URL is updated to include its reading_list_id as readingListId, and getReadingListDetails is called for it.
7. If there are no reading lists, the detail panel shows an empty state that prompts the user to create their first list; no getReadingListDetails call is made until a list exists.

Workflow: Create a new reading list
1. User clicks a "New List" button in the reading-lists overview sidebar.
2. The reading-list editor modal opens with empty fields for name and description.
3. User enters a name (required) and optional description, then submits the form.
4. The modal component calls createReadingList(name, description).
5. On success, the sidebar list manager receives the new reading_list object from the response and appends it to the overview.
6. The page-state controller sets the new list as the selected list, updates the URL readingListId to the new reading_list.id, and triggers getReadingListDetails(new_id) to load its empty items list.
7. The modal closes, and the detail panel now shows the new list header and an empty items section ready for articles to be added.

Workflow: Rename or update a reading list
1. User selects a reading list from the sidebar; the detail panel shows that list's information.
2. User clicks an "Edit list" action near the list title (either inline or through the same editor modal).
3. The editor is pre-filled with the current list name and description from getReadingListDetails.reading_list or the overview data.
4. User updates the name and/or description and submits.
5. The component calls updateReadingList(readingListId, name, description).
6. On success, both the sidebar entry and the detail header are updated with the returned reading_list values, and the updated_at timestamp is refreshed in the overview.
7. The URL readingListId remains unchanged; other page state is preserved.

Workflow: Delete a reading list
1. User opens actions for a specific reading list (either in the sidebar or detail header) and clicks "Delete list".
2. A confirmation dialog appears, showing the list name and warning that the list and its associations will be deleted.
3. User confirms deletion.
4. The confirmation component calls deleteReadingList(readingListId).
5. On success, the sidebar removes the list from getMyReadingListsOverview.reading_lists in its local state.
6. If the deleted list was currently selected, the page-state controller:
   - Checks if any reading lists remain.
   - If another list exists, sets the first remaining list as selected, updates the URL readingListId, and calls getReadingListDetails for that list.
   - If no lists remain, clears readingListId from the URL and displays the empty state in the detail panel.
7. If the deleted list was not selected, the currently selected list stays active and its details remain displayed.

Workflow: Add an article to a reading list using the Add to List control
1. The bookmarked-articles panel displays all articles from getBookmarkedArticles.bookmarked_articles.
2. For each article, an "Add to List" control is available (e.g., a button that opens a list selector).
3. User clicks "Add to List" for a specific article; a selector appears listing all available reading lists from getMyReadingListsOverview.reading_lists.
4. User chooses a target reading list.
5. The control calls addArticleToReadingList(articleId, readingListId) with the selected article and list IDs.
6. On success, if the targeted readingListId is currently selected in the detail panel, the new reading_list_item from the response is appended in UI order according to its order_index.
7. If the targeted list is not currently selected, only its item_count in the sidebar overview is incremented in the local state to reflect the new item.
8. The selector closes, and the user can continue adding other articles.

Workflow: Add an article to a reading list via drag-and-drop
1. The bookmarked-articles panel exposes each article row/card as a drag source with the associated article_id.
2. Each reading list in the sidebar, and optionally the current list detail area, is a drop target with an associated readingListId.
3. User drags an article from the bookmarked-articles panel and drops it onto a reading list target.
4. On drop, the drag-and-drop manager calls addArticleToReadingList(articleId, readingListId) for the drop target.
5. On success, if the drop target list is currently selected, the detail panel adds the returned reading_list_item to its items collection and re-renders in order_index order.
6. The corresponding list’s item_count in the sidebar overview is incremented in local state to keep counts in sync.
7. If an error is returned (e.g., attempting to add a duplicate), the component shows a non-blocking error message and does not change the UI state.

Workflow: Remove an article from a reading list
1. In the reading-list detail panel, each item row shows metadata from items[].article and a remove control (e.g., "Remove from list").
2. User clicks the remove control for a specific list item.
3. The detail panel calls removeArticleFromReadingList(reading_list_item_id) for that item.
4. On success, the item is removed from the local items collection in the detail panel.
5. The matching reading list’s item_count in the sidebar overview is decremented.
6. If the removed item was the last in the list, the detail panel displays an empty state for that list but the list itself remains.

Workflow: Open an article from a reading list
1. In the reading-list detail panel, each article title is displayed as a link or clickable element.
2. User clicks the article title.
3. The interaction triggers navigation to article_detail.html with the query parameter articleId set to that item's article_id.
4. The current page does not modify reading lists on this navigation; the article detail page can provide its own controls.

Workflow: Switch between reading lists
1. User clicks on a different reading list in the sidebar.
2. The sidebar notifies the page-state controller of the newly selected reading_list_id.
3. The page-state controller updates the URL to set readingListId to the selected ID (e.g., my_reading_lists.html?readingListId=<id>), without a full page reload if using client-side routing.
4. The controller calls getReadingListDetails(readingListId) for the new list.
5. On success, the detail panel replaces its header and items with the returned reading_list and items data.
6. The highlighted/selected state in the sidebar updates accordingly, and bookmarked articles panel remains unchanged.
interactions:
Click: Sidebar list item
- Action: Select a reading list.
- Behavior: Updates selected readingListId, updates URL query parameter, calls getReadingListDetails for that list, and refreshes the detail panel.

Click: New List button
- Action: Open list creation modal.
- Behavior: Shows reading-list editor with empty fields; on submit calls createReadingList and, on success, selects the new list and updates URL readingListId.

Submit: Reading list create/update form
- Action: Save new or updated list data.
- Behavior: For create, calls createReadingList(name, description). For edit, calls updateReadingList(readingListId, name, description). On success, updates sidebar overview and detail header, then closes the modal.

Click: Edit list action
- Action: Start editing current list.
- Behavior: Opens editor populated with the list’s current name and description from either overview or getReadingListDetails.reading_list.

Click: Delete list action
- Action: Request to delete a list.
- Behavior: Opens confirmation dialog. On confirm, calls deleteReadingList(readingListId). On success, removes list from overview and, if it was selected, selects another list or shows empty state.

Click: Article "Add to List" control in bookmarked-articles panel
- Action: Add selected article to a chosen reading list.
- Behavior: Opens list selector, then calls addArticleToReadingList(articleId, readingListId) with chosen target. On success, updates item_count in overview and, if applicable, adds item to detail panel.

Drag-and-drop: Article from bookmarked-articles panel to reading list
- Action: Add dragged article to drop-target reading list.
- Behavior: On drop, calls addArticleToReadingList(articleId, readingListId). On success, updates sidebar counts and selected list’s items if relevant.

Click: Remove article from list
- Action: Remove article from current reading list.
- Behavior: Calls removeArticleFromReadingList(reading_list_item_id). On success, removes item from detail panel and decrements item_count in overview.

Click: Article title in reading list detail
- Action: Open article detail.
- Behavior: Navigates to article_detail.html?articleId=<article_id> using an anchor or router navigation.

URL change: readingListId in query string
- Action: User loads or changes URL with a specific readingListId.
- Behavior: Page-state controller validates the ID against getMyReadingListsOverview.reading_lists and, if valid, calls getReadingListDetails for that list and marks it as selected. If invalid or missing, it falls back to a default (first list or empty state) and updates the URL accordingly.

Page load: No reading lists present
- Action: Initial load where getMyReadingListsOverview.reading_lists is empty.
- Behavior: Detail panel shows an empty state encouraging creation of a first list; New List button is available; no getReadingListDetails is called until a list is created.

Client-side filtering/sorting within panels (if implemented)
- Action: User interacts with filters (e.g., by topic, difficulty) or sorts.
- Behavior: Filtering and sorting are applied client-side on data already returned by getBookmarkedArticles or getReadingListDetails; no additional interfaces are called.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary externalized state for this page is the selected reading list, managed through the readingListId URL query parameter. On initial load, the page parses readingListId from the query string. After calling getMyReadingListsOverview, the controller checks whether the provided readingListId matches any reading_list_id from the returned reading_lists. If it matches, that list is selected and getReadingListDetails(readingListId) is invoked. If the parameter is missing or does not match an existing list, the controller selects a default: the first reading list in getMyReadingListsOverview.reading_lists if any exist. It then updates the URL to include the selected reading_list_id as readingListId (without a full reload when possible). If no reading lists exist, readingListId is removed from the URL or left unset, and the detail panel shows an empty state.

Whenever the user changes the selected list via the sidebar, creates a new list (which becomes selected), or deletes the currently selected list (and another list becomes selected), the URL's readingListId is updated to reflect the current selection. This ensures deep links and back/forward navigation restore the same selected list. The page does not use localStorage; all persisted state is derived from the interfaces and the URL. Transient UI state, such as whether the editor modal is open, which article is currently being dragged, or which filters are applied within the panels, is managed in-memory on the client and is not encoded in URL or stored persistently. All modifications to lists or their items are performed exclusively via the provided interfaces, and the UI state is re-synchronized with the latest API responses after each operation.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, selection of the active reading list based on the readingListId URL parameter, and synchronization between URL state, the reading-lists overview, the selected list details, and the bookmarked-articles panel.
Displays all reading lists with their names, descriptions, item counts, and timestamps, allows the user to select a list, and exposes actions to create, rename, or delete lists.

Shows the currently selected reading list's metadata (name, description, timestamps) and its list of articles, including article metadata, and allows removing articles from the list and opening article details.
Lists all bookmarked articles available for organization, and provides per-article controls to add them to reading lists via a selector or drag-and-drop.
Provides a form to create a new reading list or update the name and description of an existing one, then calls the appropriate interface.
Asks the user to confirm deletion of a reading list before calling deleteReadingList, to avoid accidental removal.
Attached to each bookmarked article, this control presents all available reading lists and adds the article to the selected list using addArticleToReadingList.
Coordinates drag-and-drop interactions between bookmarked articles and reading list drop targets, calling addArticleToReadingList when a drop occurs.
Handles item-level actions within the reading list detail panel, particularly removing items via removeArticleFromReadingList and propagating item_count changes.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, max-width layout based on a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters (desktop breakpoint). Content appears within a central column spanning roughly 8–10 grid columns, with generous side margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a centered, max-width (approx. 1120–1200px) layout on a full-width, very pale green main panel beneath the site header. At the top of this panel, dedicate a broad, hero-like editorial band for the page title ('My Reading Lists') and a short explanatory paragraph, both left-aligned within the central column. Below this textual intro, transition into the functional area: a calm two-column layout where the left column shows the reading lists overview and the right column shows the active reading list detail. Beneath this two-column band, place a full-width bookmarked-articles panel that echoes the role of the large image in the mockup: a spacious, visually grounded section that anchors the bottom of the page.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a centered, max-width (approx. 1120–1200px) layout on a full-width, very pale green main panel beneath the site header. At the top of this panel, dedicate a broad, hero-like editorial band for the page title ('My Reading Lists') and a short explanatory paragraph, both left-aligned within the central column. Below this textual intro, transition into the functional area: a calm two-column layout where the left column shows the reading lists overview and the right column shows the active reading list detail. Beneath this two-column band, place a full-width bookmarked-articles panel that echoes the role of the large image in the mockup: a spacious, visually grounded section that anchors the bottom of the page.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Preserve generous side gutters so that the central content column spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, centered. The hero text band (title + paragraph) should occupy the top 30–40% of the initial viewport height, with 48–64px of vertical padding above and below the text block. The two-column content area beneath it occupies the next 40–50% of the viewport, with the left sidebar at about 25–30% of the central width and the right detail panel at 70–75%. The bookmarked-articles panel sits below with a 48–64px gap from the two-column zone and uses generous vertical padding (32–48px) inside. Modals float above all with ample negative space around them, typically maxing at 40–50% of viewport width on desktop.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, editorial feel: keep lists visually simple, limit the number of simultaneously visible controls, and separate items with 16–24px vertical spacing. Text uses comfortable line height, and components have 24–32px internal padding so that nothing feels cramped. The two-column area feels airy rather than dashboard-like: fewer borders, more subtle separators, and plenty of breathing room between the sidebar, detail panel content, and the bookmarked-articles section.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Follow a strong vertical F-pattern inspired by the mockup: the eye starts at the top left (global logo and nav outside this spec), then moves to the 'My Reading Lists' heading and its paragraph just below. From there, the eye moves down into the two-column functional area, scanning left to right: first the list of reading lists in the left sidebar, then across to the currently selected reading list detail in the right panel. The scan continues downward to the bookmarked-articles panel that spans the width under both columns. Within rows, the main text anchors on the left, with subtle action controls (add-to-list dropdown, list actions) aligned to the right, echoing the secondary focal points of the mockup’s navigation and accessibility button.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the site’s slim top navigation, the page opens into a full-width, pale green backdrop that feels calm and editorial. Centered within this, a wide but not edge-to-edge content column presents the 'My Reading Lists' heading near the top-left of the usable area, with a short explanatory paragraph directly below, both surrounded by generous padding so they sit comfortably in the upper half of the screen. As you scroll your gaze downward, this introductory text gives way to a spacious two-column zone: on the left, a narrow, softly framed list of your reading lists; on the right, a larger, open panel showing the currently selected list and its articles, with subtle actions tucked toward the upper-right. Continuing further down the page, the two columns conclude and a single, broad panel stretches across the central column: the bookmarked-articles section, visually echoing the large image region from the mockup by grounding the lower portion of the layout with a calm, well-spaced list of all saved articles. Modals for editing and deleting lists appear centered over this environment, dimming but not overwhelming the gentle green backdrop, so that the page retains its minimalist, environmental editorial character even during interactions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-state-controller is a non-visual layer that does not occupy any explicit space on the screen. Conceptually, place it 'behind' the main pale green content panel, orchestrating which reading list is active, whether modals are visible, and how drag-and-drop changes are reflected. It does not alter the spatial arrangement but ensures that the central column, two-column content area, and modals update without shifting their overall layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, logical controller; no fixed position or visible footprint in the UI.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-lists-overview-sidebar sits within the main pale green content area, directly below the introductory heading and paragraph, aligned to the left side of the central content column. It occupies roughly 25–30% of the central column width, forming the left column of the two-column functional area. Vertically, it starts about 32–48px beneath the end of the intro paragraph and extends downward to roughly match the height of the reading-list-detail-panel next to it. The sidebar appears as a light, minimally bordered panel (or subtle card) with 24–32px internal padding, a small section title at the top (e.g., 'Reading Lists'), and a vertical list of list names spaced 16–24px apart. The left edge of this sidebar aligns with the left edge of the central content column, maintaining a generous 10–12% viewport gutter from the browser edge. The right edge is cleanly separated from the detail panel by a 24–32px gap, preserving the airy feeling of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally with the page. On taller pages, the sidebar content area can independently scroll within its fixed vertical frame if there are many lists, while the panel itself maintains its left-column position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-detail-panel is the dominant functional surface and sits to the right of the reading-lists-overview-sidebar within the same horizontal band. It starts at the same vertical position as the sidebar—about 32–48px below the intro paragraph—and occupies the remaining 70–75% of the central content column width. A 24–32px horizontal gap separates it from the sidebar, ensuring each column breathes. The panel appears as a broad, light card or subtly elevated area with 24–32px internal padding on all sides. At its top-left, the active list’s name is displayed as a prominent subheading, with a short description or metadata line beneath it. The reading-list-items-actions cluster sits within the upper-right corner of this panel, aligned horizontally with the list name, but visually small and unobtrusive. Below the header area, the list of articles is arranged in a vertical stack: each article row spans most of the panel width, with title and key info aligned left and any per-item controls (such as drag handles and status indicators) placed to the far right. Rows are separated by 16–24px vertical spacing, so the list feels curated rather than dense. The panel’s bottom edge ends 48–64px above the bookmarked-articles-panel below, clearly separating these two logical sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow of the page, expanding vertically as more articles are present. Interactions like drag-and-drop reorder items inside without changing the panel’s horizontal bounds.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmarked-articles-panel sits directly below the two-column area formed by the sidebar and the detail panel, occupying the full width of the central content column. There is a generous vertical gap—about 48–64px—between the bottom of the reading-list-detail-panel and the top edge of this panel, echoing the separation between the intro text and the main content in the mockup. Visually, it appears as a broad, calm section on the pale green background, optionally as a subtle card with 32–48px internal padding. A section heading like 'All Bookmarked Articles' is left-aligned at the top of the panel, with a brief explanatory line beneath it. The articles are arranged in a simple vertical list or a loose grid of cards; on desktop, a one- or two-column card layout can be used as long as elements remain spacious. Each article block spans most of the panel width, with its title and key details aligned left and the article-add-to-list-control aligned toward the right side of the same row, mirroring the F-pattern: eye catches title first, then the control. Vertical spacing between article blocks is 24–32px, allowing the panel to feel like a curated reading gallery that visually anchors the lower portion of the page, similar in weight to the environmental image region in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width (within the central column) section that grows vertically as more articles are shown, pushing the page length downward while maintaining consistent top and bottom padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-editor-modal appears as a centered overlay above the entire pale green page content, dimming the background with a subtle translucent layer while keeping the underlying editorial layout partially visible. The modal dialog itself is horizontally centered on the screen and vertically centered within the viewport. On desktop, it occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport width (capped around 480–560px) and is surrounded by generous empty space to preserve the minimalist feel. Inside the modal, content is arranged in a simple vertical stack with 24–32px padding on all sides: the modal title at the top (e.g., 'Edit Reading List'), followed by spaced form fields (name, description, perhaps color or icon), each separated by 16–24px, and action buttons aligned to the bottom-right. The design avoids clutter, using plenty of whitespace between fields. The modal does not shift or resize the underlying sidebar, detail panel, or bookmarked-articles-panel; it simply rests above them, like a lightweight editorial dialog.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport, independent of page scroll (the background can be locked while the modal is open).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delete-reading-list-confirmation dialog appears above the rest of the interface as a smaller, focused overlay, visually related to but more compact than the editor modal. It is centered horizontally and vertically within the viewport, over the same subtle dimmed backdrop. The dialog width is about 30–35% of the viewport (roughly 320–400px), with 24px internal padding. The message is concise—a short title and 1–2 lines of text—followed by a horizontally aligned pair of buttons (e.g., 'Cancel' and 'Delete') at the bottom-right. Ample whitespace surrounds the text so the confirmation feels calm and deliberate rather than alarming, consistent with the site’s environmental tone. The underlying reading-lists-overview-sidebar and reading-list-detail-panel remain visible but are de-emphasized by the dimmed background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position, centered overlay sharing the same dimming layer as other modals; it does not alter the layout beneath and scroll does typically lock while it is displayed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-add-to-list-control is an in-row, right-aligned dropdown control that appears within each article item in the bookmarked-articles-panel (and optionally within article rows in the reading-list-detail-panel). In each article row, the main text content—title, brief description, and metadata—is left-aligned and occupies the majority of the row width, while this control is positioned near the far right edge of the same row, aligned vertically with the article title line. The control itself is compact, perhaps styled as a pill-shaped button or subtle dropdown with a label like 'Add to List' or the currently selected list name. It does not extend beyond the bounds of its parent panel and maintains at least 16px padding from the panel’s right edge. Vertical spacing around the control is consistent with the row’s padding, so it feels integrated rather than tacked on. This placement supports the F-pattern: users scan the article name on the left, then naturally find the action on the right without overcrowding the central area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element anchored within each article row; scrolls with its parent panel and does not float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The drag-and-drop-manager is an invisible interaction layer associated primarily with the article rows inside the reading-list-detail-panel (and optionally with lists in the reading-lists-overview-sidebar). It does not occupy its own space but adds reordering affordances to existing elements. In practice, each draggable article row within the detail panel may display a subtle handle or grip icon on the left edge of the row, within the row’s existing height and alignment, so that users can drag items up or down without changing the panel’s overall layout. The vertical stack spacing between rows (16–24px) is maintained during and after reordering, preserving the low-density aesthetic. No new visual containers are introduced; drag previews and placeholders adhere to the same width and alignment as the regular rows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller that enables vertical reordering within existing lists; drag interactions occur within the current panel boundaries and do not alter global layout alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-items-actions cluster resides inside the reading-list-detail-panel, in the upper-right portion of the panel’s header area. Horizontally, it aligns with the top line of the active reading list’s title, anchored to the right edge of the panel with about 16–24px internal padding from the panel border. Vertically, it sits clearly above the first article row, separated by 16–24px of space from both the list title and the items below, so the header section feels open. The actions themselves (e.g., 'Rename', 'Delete List', 'Share') are displayed as a short row of subtle text buttons or icons, compact enough not to compete with the main title. This placement mirrors the faint navigation in the mockup: informative but not dominant, discovered as the eye traverses rightward across the header before scanning downward into the list content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the reading-list-detail-panel’s header; scrolls with the panel and remains fixed relative to the list title, without independent floating behavior.

name: Volunteer & Groups
title: Volunteer & Groups
description: Page for finding and exploring ongoing volunteer groups, with filters for location, focus area, and rating, and navigation to group details and the user dashboard.

core_features: Display a list of ongoing volunteer groups using the searchVolunteerGroups interface, with pagination.
Provide a tab or toggle control to focus on ongoing groups on this page and optionally switch to one-time volunteer opportunities on a separate page.
Allow users to filter groups by ZIP/postal code and distance radius, focus area, and minimum average rating, using options returned by getVolunteerGroupFilterOptions and incoming URL parameters.
Allow users to sort groups by selectable criteria (e.g., rating, distance, name) using sort options returned by getVolunteerGroupFilterOptions.
Pre-populate filters and sort order from URL parameters provided by index.html and dashboard.html (postalCode, distanceMiles, focusArea, minRating, sort).
Enable navigation from a group entry to the detailed group page (group_detail.html) with the selected groupId passed as a URL parameter.
Provide a "View my groups" navigation link to dashboard.html so users can access groups they have joined.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Arrive with pre-filled filters from index or dashboard: 1) User clicks a volunteer-related call-to-action on index.html or dashboard.html; 2) The source page navigates to volunteer.html with some combination of postalCode, distanceMiles, focusArea, minRating, and sort in the querystring; 3) On load, the page controller reads these URL parameters; 4) The page calls getVolunteerGroupFilterOptions to retrieve allowed ranges and options; 5) The filter bar initializes its controls using API defaults, then overrides them with any incoming URL parameters; 6) The page calls searchVolunteerGroups with a filters object derived from the merged defaults and URL parameters and the selected sort; 7) The results list displays the groups returned by searchVolunteerGroups along with total_count; 8) The user can immediately scroll and browse the list or refine filters further.

Workflow 2 – Adjust filters and refresh results: 1) User modifies one or more filters in the filter bar (postal code, distance radius, focus area, minimum rating); 2) The filter bar holds the user’s in-progress changes in component state; 3) User clicks an Apply or equivalent action on the filter bar; 4) The page controller converts the current filter state into URL parameters (postalCode, distanceMiles, focusArea, minRating) and updates the browser URL (including resetting page to the first page); 5) The controller calls searchVolunteerGroups with filters synchronized to the updated URL and the currently selected sort; 6) The results list re-renders with the new results and updated total_count; 7) If the search returns no results, the empty state component is shown with an option to clear or loosen filters, which again updates URL params and triggers a new search.

Workflow 3 – Change sort order: 1) User selects a different sort option from the sort control (e.g., rating, distance, name) in the filter bar; 2) The filter bar notifies the page controller of the new sort key; 3) The controller updates the sort parameter in the URL and resets page to the first page; 4) The controller calls searchVolunteerGroups with the same filters but new sort_by value; 5) The results list re-renders to reflect the new ordering while preserving the current filters.

Workflow 4 – Paginate through group results: 1) After a search, user sees a subset of groups and pagination controls; 2) User clicks a next/previous page control or a specific page number; 3) The pagination component notifies the page controller of the new page (and optionally a new page size); 4) The controller updates the page (and pageSize, if applicable) parameters in the URL; 5) The controller calls searchVolunteerGroups with existing filters and sort_by but updated page/page_size; 6) The results list re-renders with groups corresponding to the requested page; 7) User can use browser back/forward to move between previously viewed pages, as page and filters are encoded in the URL.

Workflow 5 – Open a group’s detail page: 1) User sees a list of groups in the results list; 2) User clicks on a group’s name or card; 3) The results list component triggers navigation to group_detail.html with groupId set to the selected group’s identifier in the querystring; 4) The group detail page can then load full information for that group (outside the scope of this page).

Workflow 6 – Navigate to My Groups in the dashboard: 1) From any state of the listing, the user clicks the "View my groups" toolbar link; 2) The toolbar component navigates to dashboard.html, optionally including any desired context parameters if defined by the broader application (not required by this specification); 3) The dashboard page shows the user’s group memberships using GroupMembership data (implementation for that page is out of scope).

Workflow 7 – Switch between ongoing groups and one-time opportunities: 1) At the top of the page, user sees a toggle or tabs for Ongoing Groups vs One-time Opportunities; 2) If the user selects Ongoing Groups, the current page state is set to the groups mode (view parameter in URL) and the searchVolunteerGroups-based listing remains visible; 3) If the user selects One-time Opportunities, the toggle component navigates to the designated opportunities/events page (for example an events listing page) while passing current location-related parameters (such as postalCode and distanceMiles) in the URL; 4) When returning to Ongoing Groups via the toggle, the browser navigates back to volunteer.html with view set appropriately and existing filters reconstructed from the URL.
interactions: User can type or edit a ZIP/postal code in a text input; on change, the filter bar updates its local state, and on applying filters, the postalCode URL parameter and filters.postalCode value for searchVolunteerGroups are updated.

User can adjust a distance/radius control (e.g., slider or dropdown) bounded by distance_range_miles.min and distance_range_miles.max returned by getVolunteerGroupFilterOptions; on applying, distanceMiles is written to the URL and used as filters.distanceMiles in searchVolunteerGroups.

User can select a focus area from a dropdown populated dynamically from focus_areas returned by getVolunteerGroupFilterOptions; on applying, focusArea is written to the URL and used as filters.focusArea in searchVolunteerGroups (or omitted when the user selects an all/any option).

User can adjust a minimum rating control (e.g., slider, stepper, or dropdown) bounded by rating_range.min and rating_range.max; on applying, minRating is written to the URL and used as filters.minRating in searchVolunteerGroups.

User can change sort order using a select control populated from sort_options returned by getVolunteerGroupFilterOptions; when changed, the sort URL parameter and the sort_by argument to searchVolunteerGroups are updated, and page is reset to the first page.

User can click a button or link to clear/reset all filters; this interaction removes postalCode, distanceMiles, focusArea, minRating, and page from the URL (or sets them back to defaults derived from getVolunteerGroupFilterOptions), then triggers a fresh call to searchVolunteerGroups.

User can interact with pagination controls (next, previous, specific page index, and optionally page size selector); these interactions update page (and optionally pageSize) in the URL and cause searchVolunteerGroups to be called with the updated paging parameters.

User can click anywhere designated as interactive on a group row or card (such as the group name or a dedicated "View details" link); this interaction navigates to group_detail.html with the selected group’s identifier encoded as groupId in the URL.
User can click the "View my groups" link in the toolbar; this interaction navigates directly to dashboard.html without modifying filters on the current page.

User can toggle between the Ongoing Groups and One-time Opportunities views; this interaction either updates the local view state and view URL parameter for this page (Ongoing Groups) or navigates to another page (Opportunities) while preserving relevant query parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page controller reads URL parameters including postalCode, distanceMiles, focusArea, minRating, sort, page, pageSize, and view. The controller first calls getVolunteerGroupFilterOptions to obtain dynamic ranges and sort options. It then constructs the initial filter state: starting from API defaults (e.g., distance_range_miles.default, rating_range.min, no focus area), then overriding any values if the corresponding URL parameters are present. The sort state is initialized similarly: defaulting to a sort key chosen from getVolunteerGroupFilterOptions.sort_options, overridden by the sort URL parameter when provided. The current page number and page size are taken from page and pageSize URL parameters when present; otherwise, they are taken from application-level defaults configured outside this page (not hardcoded here). The current view mode (Ongoing Groups vs One-time Opportunities) is determined from the view URL parameter; if it is missing or set to the groups-oriented value, this page displays the groups listing; if set to a value representing one-time opportunities, the controller or toggle component navigates to the appropriate opportunities page. After computing the effective filter, sort, and paging state, the controller calls searchVolunteerGroups with a filters object that includes the applicable properties (e.g., postalCode, distanceMiles, focusArea, minRating) and an isActive flag if supported by the backend, along with sort_by, page, and page_size. Each later user interaction that changes filters, sort, or paging first updates the in-memory state, then synchronizes that state to URL parameters (using browser history APIs), and finally calls searchVolunteerGroups again with parameters derived purely from the URL-synchronized state. This ensures that reloading the page or using browser back/forward restores the same filter and pagination state without using localStorage or other client-side persistence.

components.functionality: Central orchestration component that initializes state from URL, calls getVolunteerGroupFilterOptions and searchVolunteerGroups, manages filter/sort/page state, and propagates data to child components.

Provides a two-option toggle (e.g., tabs) between Ongoing Groups and One-time Opportunities. When Ongoing Groups is selected, ensures view mode is set to groups on this page; when One-time Opportunities is selected, navigates to the designated opportunities/events page with relevant location filters in the URL.

Displays and manages controls for location (postal code and distance), focus area, minimum rating, and sort order. It initializes options and allowed ranges using getVolunteerGroupFilterOptions and incoming URL parameters, and emits filter and sort changes to the page controller.
Displays contextual information about the current search (such as total results count and current filters summary) and includes a "View my groups" navigation link to the dashboard.

Renders the list of volunteer groups returned by searchVolunteerGroups, showing key fields such as group name, description snippet, focus area label, average rating, location information, and distance display. Each row or card is clickable to navigate to the group detail page.
Controls navigation between pages of group results using total_count, page, and page_size returned by searchVolunteerGroups. Updates URL parameters and triggers new searches via the page controller.

Shown when searchVolunteerGroups returns zero results for the current filters. Provides messaging and a control to clear or relax filters, which resets relevant URL parameters and triggers a new search.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, max-width layout based on a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters (desktop breakpoint). Content appears within a central column spanning roughly 8–10 grid columns, with generous side margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a centered, single main column beneath the global header, following a linear top-to-bottom flow. At the top of the main area, a pale green hero-like panel introduces the 'Volunteer & Groups' context (handled by the page controller). Directly beneath this introduction, a compact control band holds the filters, toolbar, and view toggle arranged from top to bottom. Below this band, the volunteer group results list takes over the majority of the vertical space, followed last by pagination. When there are no results, the empty state occupies the same area as the results and pagination, effectively replacing them.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a centered, single main column beneath the global header, following a linear top-to-bottom flow. At the top of the main area, a pale green hero-like panel introduces the 'Volunteer & Groups' context (handled by the page controller). Directly beneath this introduction, a compact control band holds the filters, toolbar, and view toggle arranged from top to bottom. Below this band, the volunteer group results list takes over the majority of the vertical space, followed last by pagination. When there are no results, the empty state occupies the same area as the results and pagination, effectively replacing them.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content is constrained to a central column of roughly 70–80% of the page width, with wide left and right gutters (about 10–15% each) for a calm editorial feel. The hero introduction occupies roughly the upper 40–50% of the initial viewport on desktop, with generous top and bottom padding (around 48–64px).
