The controls band (filters + toolbar + view toggle) is relatively shallow but airy, taking about 15–20% of the initial viewport height. The remaining 35–45% and all subsequent scrollable space are dominated by the results list, with pagination occupying a small strip at the bottom of the content column. Vertical spacing between major sections (hero, controls band, list, pagination) follows 32–64px rhythms.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains low visual density: single-column content, generous line height, and ample whitespace. Filters and toolbar controls are not packed tightly; they have 16–24px internal padding and 24–32px gaps between logical groups. The results list shows a comfortable number of cards per viewport (e.g., 3–5 cards on a laptop) with 24–32px vertical gaps between them. The empty state is treated like a large editorial visual, centered with significant margins so the page never feels cluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F-pattern similar to the mockup. The eye starts at the top left with the section heading inside the pale green hero, moves across the introductory text, then drops down to the filter bar at the top of the control band. From there, the eye scans horizontally across the toolbar and view toggle, then continues vertically into the first cards in the results list and down through the list to the pagination controls. In scenarios with no results, the eye moves from the controls band down to a prominent, centered empty-state panel that acts like the supporting visual. A floating accessibility button (outside these components) remains a subtle secondary focal point in the lower-right corner.

layout.overall_description: Below the global site header, the main 'Volunteer & Groups' page opens into a wide, pale green hero panel that fills about half of the initial viewport. Within a centered content column, the page title sits at the top left of this panel with a calm, editorial feel, followed by a short descriptive paragraph that spans a modest width, creating soft, multi-line text with comfortable line spacing. As the hero panel ends, the background transitions to a very light neutral tone while the content column continues. The first band under the hero is the volunteer group control area: a full-width filter bar sits closest to the hero, and directly below it, a slender toolbar runs across the same column, with the view toggle visually tied to the right side of this toolbar. Under this control band, the results list extends downward as a vertical stack of volunteer group cards, each inset within the central column and separated by generous gaps. At the bottom of the content, pagination controls sit centered, with plenty of breathing space above and below to echo the editorial layout. When there are no matching groups, instead of a list of cards, a centered empty-state panel occupies the space beneath the control band, mimicking the role of a large supporting visual while preserving the calm, nature-oriented aesthetic and airy spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the main content region directly beneath the global site navigation. It stretches full width across the viewport but constrains all inner content to a centered column approximately 70–80% of the page width, leaving generous left and right gutters (about 10–15% each) that remain consistent as the user scrolls. At the top of this controlled area, it renders a pale green hero-like section that occupies roughly the top 40–50% of the initial viewport height on desktop. Inside this hero, the 'Volunteer & Groups' heading is left-aligned at the top of the content column, with a margin of about 64px from the top of the hero panel and 32px from the left edge of the content column. Beneath the heading, a multi-line introductory paragraph spans about 60–70% of the content column width, with 24–32px spacing from the heading and generous line height so the text breaks into short, readable lines. The bottom of the hero panel leaves about 48px of space below the paragraph before transitioning to the neutral background where the controls band (filter bar + toolbar + view toggle) begins. Below the controls band, the controller vertically stacks the results list or empty state, followed by the pagination. All sections follow a linear, top-to-bottom flow with 32–64px vertical separation to maintain the open, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width container with a fixed max-width inner column centered in the viewport. The hero section scrolls away normally; the controller simply orchestrates the vertical stacking and spacing of all child components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view toggle is a compact control positioned in the upper-right portion of the controls band, visually paired with the toolbar. On desktop, it appears on the same horizontal line as the toolbar content: while the result count and any primary actions sit on the left side of the column, the view toggle is aligned flush to the right edge of the central content column, with about 16px of internal padding around its buttons and 8–16px of space between it and any adjacent toolbar elements. Vertically, the view toggle sits about 16px below the bottom edge of the filter bar and about 24px above the top of the results list. It does not span the full width; instead, it occupies only as much horizontal space as needed (roughly 15–20% of the content column width), keeping the layout visually light. This placement allows the eye to track left-to-right across toolbar text and land naturally on the toggle as an optional secondary control.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Floats inline within the toolbar row, right-aligned within the central column. If the toolbar row becomes sticky, the view toggle remains attached to it; otherwise, it scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar sits directly beneath the pale green hero section, forming the top part of the controls band. It spans the full width of the central content column, with 32–40px of vertical space separating it from the bottom of the hero panel. The bar itself feels like a light editorial strip rather than a heavy control panel: it has 16–24px of internal padding on all sides and uses pill-shaped or simple, understated filter controls arranged from left to right. The primary filters (e.g., location, focus area, rating) begin at the left edge of the content column, with 16–24px horizontal gaps between each filter control; if there are many, they wrap gracefully onto a second line while maintaining the same generous spacing. The vertical height of the filter bar remains modest—roughly 72–96px total—so it acts like a clear but unobtrusive horizontal band. Below the filter bar, there is a 16–24px gap before the toolbar row begins, preserving a clear visual separation between filtering and other meta controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow, anchored immediately below the hero. As the user scrolls, the filter bar moves off-screen naturally, keeping the page feeling editorial rather than app-like. It expands in height if filters wrap to an additional row but always maintains consistent side gutters and internal padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The toolbar is positioned directly below the filter bar, forming the lower half of the controls band. It shares the same central column width and aligns perfectly with the filter bar edges above and the results list below, reinforcing the single-column structure. Vertically, it sits about 16–24px beneath the filter bar and has a compact height, created by 12–16px of vertical padding. Inside the toolbar, content is arranged along a single horizontal line: on the left, elements like the result count, active filter summary, or quick links to the dashboard are left-aligned; on the right, sorting controls and the view toggle align to the column’s right edge. There is a clear 24–32px separation between the left and right groups so the center of the toolbar feels open and uncluttered. The toolbar’s bottom edge is placed roughly 24–32px above the start of the results list, ensuring the controls band reads as a distinct, self-contained strip before the list of groups begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Optional sticky behavior: on desktop, the toolbar (with the view toggle) can become sticky once it reaches the top of the viewport, staying pinned just below the global navigation as the user scrolls through the results. If sticky behavior is used, it retains its full width within the central column and a subtle shadow or divider line may emphasize its anchored state; if not, it scrolls normally as part of the linear flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list occupies the main body of the page beneath the controls band. Its top edge starts approximately 24–32px below the bottom of the toolbar, maintaining a clear but modest gap. The list spans the full width of the central content column, but individual group items (cards or rows) can be slightly inset with 16–24px internal padding to enhance the editorial feel. Each group card is stacked vertically with generous 24–32px spacing between cards, allowing the content to breathe and keeping the visual density low. On a typical desktop viewport, the user sees around 3–5 cards at a time, with additional cards revealed as they scroll. Cards maintain left alignment of headings and key information to support the F-pattern, while right-side content within cards (such as ratings or quick action links) aligns toward the right edge of the card without breaking the overall column alignment. The list extends downward, forming the bulk of the scrollable area, and leaves about 32–40px of space above the pagination controls at the bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally within the page flow. The list grows or shrinks vertically based on the number of groups. If the toolbar is sticky, the list scrolls underneath it, maintaining the same left and right column boundaries so the experience feels cohesive.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are placed directly beneath the results list as the final element in the vertical stack. They are horizontally centered within the central content column, visually lighter than the main content but clearly identifiable. A vertical gap of roughly 32–40px separates them from the last result card, so they feel detached from any single group and behave as a section-level control. The pagination UI itself has a low profile, with concise page numbers and next/previous controls arranged in a single row, and 8–16px padding around the clickable elements. Beneath the pagination, the page controller provides an additional 48–64px of bottom margin before the end of the main content area, mirroring the generous spacing at the top of the page and maintaining the calm, open layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the content flow. It does not stick or float; instead, it appears once the user has scrolled past the results list, serving as a natural endpoint and navigation for more content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state occupies the same spatial slot as the results list and pagination when there are no matching groups. It appears directly below the toolbar and view toggle, starting about 32–40px beneath them. Instead of multiple cards, a single, visually calm panel is centered within the central column. This panel can be between 40–60% of the viewport height on desktop, leaving generous 48–64px margins both above and below to keep it feeling like a featured editorial visual rather than a dense alert. Within the panel, any illustration or icon sits near the top center, with a short headline and explanatory text below, all center-aligned. The content width is constrained to about 50–60% of the column to keep line lengths short and readable. The surrounding background remains the same light neutral as the results area, allowing the empty-state panel’s pale green accents to gently draw attention without overwhelming the page. No pagination is shown when the empty state is active, so the eye naturally stops at this centered focal point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered in place of the results list and pagination. It scrolls normally if the content above it (hero + controls band) exceeds the viewport height, but the design aims for the main message of the empty state to be visible without requiring additional scrolling on typical desktop viewports.

name: Volunteer Group Detail
title: Volunteer Group Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a specific volunteer group, shows the user’s membership status and preferences, and allows joining or updating participation preferences for that group.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for a volunteer group identified by the groupId URL parameter using getVolunteerGroupDetails.
Show the group’s mission/description, focus area label, meeting schedule description, location display, and rating label based on data from getVolunteerGroupDetails.
Detect the current user’s membership status in the group (if any) from getVolunteerGroupDetails.user_membership and show an appropriate status indicator.
Provide a Join Group call-to-action that calls joinVolunteerGroup with the current groupId to create a GroupMembership record.
Collect and edit participation preferences (participation_frequency and preferences_notes) for the user’s membership using updateGroupMembershipPreferences.
Confirm successful group membership creation and preference updates using success flags and message fields returned from joinVolunteerGroup and updateGroupMembershipPreferences.
Provide navigation back to the volunteer listings (volunteer.html) and forward to the user’s dashboard (dashboard.html) after joining.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View group details as a first-time visitor
1) User opens group_detail.html with a groupId query parameter.
2) Page reads groupId from the URL and calls getVolunteerGroupDetails(groupId).
3) While loading, the page shows a loading state and hides group-specific components.
4) On success, the page binds the returned group details and display labels to the summary and description components.
5) The page also inspects user_membership; if no membership_id is present, the Join Group call-to-action is shown in its initial (joinable) state.
6) User can read the description, focus area, schedule, rating label, and location display without needing to be a member.

Workflow: Join a volunteer group
1) User is on group_detail.html with a valid groupId and no active membership (user_membership.membership_id is absent or status is not active).
2) User clicks the Join Group button.
3) The page disables the Join Group button and calls joinVolunteerGroup(groupId).
4) On success (success == true), the page updates its in-memory membership state with the returned membership object and message, and refreshes membership-dependent UI (status banner, preferences form visibility, next steps panel, and View this group in My Dashboard link).
5) The page may optionally re-call getVolunteerGroupDetails(groupId) to ensure membership information is consistent with back-end state; if it does, it replaces its local membership state with user_membership from the refreshed response.
6) The user sees confirmation text using the returned message, and can proceed to set or adjust participation_frequency and preferences_notes.
7) User can click View this group in My Dashboard to navigate to dashboard.html.

Workflow: Update participation preferences for an existing membership
1) User opens group_detail.html with groupId for a group they have already joined.
2) Page calls getVolunteerGroupDetails(groupId) and receives user_membership with membership_id and current participation_frequency and preferences_notes.
3) The preferences form is populated with these values and enabled for editing if user_membership.status indicates an editable/active membership.
4) User updates participation_frequency (selecting from allowed values defined for GroupMembership.participation_frequency) and/or updates preferences_notes.
5) User submits the preferences form; the page calls updateGroupMembershipPreferences(membershipId from user_membership.membership_id, participation_frequency, preferences_notes).
6) While the request is in progress, the preferences form shows a submitting state and prevents duplicate submissions.
7) On success (success == true), the page updates its local membership state from the returned membership object and shows a confirmation message using the returned message.
8) If the update fails (success == false or network error), the page shows an inline error message and preserves user-entered values in the form.

Workflow: Handle missing or invalid groupId
1) User accesses group_detail.html without a groupId query parameter, or with a groupId that results in an error in getVolunteerGroupDetails.
2) If groupId is missing, the page does not call getVolunteerGroupDetails and instead shows an error state explaining that a group identifier is required, with a link back to volunteer.html.
3) If groupId is present but getVolunteerGroupDetails returns an error or no group object, the page shows an error state indicating that the group could not be loaded, and provides navigation back to volunteer.html.
4) Join and preferences components remain disabled/hidden when group details cannot be loaded.

Workflow: Navigate back to listings or to dashboard
1) At any time after group details are loaded, the user can click a Back to volunteer listings link.
2) The page navigates the browser to volunteer.html without modifying the groupId or other query parameters.
3) After successfully joining a group (based on joinVolunteerGroup success), a View this group in My Dashboard link becomes visible.
4) When the user clicks this link, the page navigates the browser to dashboard.html, where dashboard-specific logic can display the newly joined group.
interactions: Initial page load: On page initialization, read the groupId query parameter from the URL; if present, trigger a call to getVolunteerGroupDetails(groupId) and propagate the response into group detail, display labels, and membership state.

Join Group button click: When the Join Group button is clicked, validate that groupId is present and that the user is not already an active member, then call joinVolunteerGroup(groupId); handle success and error states, update membership-related UI, and show confirmation messages.

Participation frequency selection: When the user changes participation_frequency in the preferences form, update local form state; the value is later submitted through updateGroupMembershipPreferences using the currently known membershipId.
Preferences notes input: When the user edits preferences_notes, update local form state; the value is later submitted through updateGroupMembershipPreferences.

Preferences form submission: On submitting the membership preferences form, call updateGroupMembershipPreferences(membershipId, participation_frequency, preferences_notes); disable the form during the request; on success, update membership display and show confirmation; on failure, display inline errors and re-enable the form.
Back to volunteer listings navigation: When the user clicks the Back to volunteer listings link, navigate to volunteer.html.

View this group in My Dashboard navigation: When the user clicks the View this group in My Dashboard link (visible only after a successful join, or when user_membership indicates an active membership), navigate to dashboard.html.

Error and retry interaction: If getVolunteerGroupDetails fails, provide a Retry loading button that re-calls getVolunteerGroupDetails with the current groupId; disable other interactive elements until successful load or explicit cancellation by navigating away.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver for this page is the groupId URL query parameter. On load, the page reads groupId from the querystring (e.g., group_detail.html?groupId={id}). If groupId is absent, the page enters a non-operational error state and does not call getVolunteerGroupDetails; it displays an error message and only enables navigation back to volunteer.html. If groupId is present, the page calls getVolunteerGroupDetails(groupId). The result populates two main state segments: group detail state (bound to the VolunteerGroup-related fields and the display object: focus_area_label, rating_label, location_display) and membership state (bound to user_membership.membership_id, status, participation_frequency, preferences_notes). The membershipId used by updateGroupMembershipPreferences is derived from user_membership.membership_id, which is assumed to be stable for the current user and group combination. When joinVolunteerGroup(groupId) is called, its returned membership object immediately updates the local membership state; optionally, the page may re-invoke getVolunteerGroupDetails(groupId) to synchronize state with the server, but this does not change the URL. No membership or preference state is stored in localStorage or other persistent client storage; all persistent state is derived from interface responses on each page load. Navigation away from the page (to volunteer.html or dashboard.html) does not retain internal component state; when the user returns to group_detail.html, the page re-evaluates the state based solely on the new groupId URL parameter and fresh data from getVolunteerGroupDetails.

components.functionality: Reads the groupId query parameter from the current page URL and initializes a page-level state object that downstream components use to determine whether to load group data or show an error for missing groupId.

Uses the groupId provided by url-state-initializer to call getVolunteerGroupDetails(groupId), manages loading and error states, and exposes the resolved group details (group and display objects) and user_membership to child components.

Displays an error message when groupId is missing or when getVolunteerGroupDetails fails to return a valid group; provides a retry action when groupId is present and a simple navigation action back to volunteer.html when unrecoverable.

Shows high-level group information including name, focus_area_label, rating_label, meeting_schedule_description, and location_display derived from the getVolunteerGroupDetails response; remains read-only regardless of membership status.

Displays the group description text from the getVolunteerGroupDetails.group.description field, and any other non-interactive descriptive content supplied for the group to explain its mission and typical activities.

Reflects the current user’s membership state for the group by reading user_membership.status and participation_frequency; indicates whether the user is not a member, has an active membership, or has a cancelled membership, and adjusts the availability of membership actions accordingly.

Provides the Join Group primary call-to-action. If user_membership.membership_id is not present or status does not indicate an active membership, enables a button that calls joinVolunteerGroup with the current groupId. On success, updates membership state and triggers UI changes such as showing the preferences form and next-step guidance.

Allows users with an existing membership to specify or update participation_frequency and preferences_notes. It initializes form fields from user_membership.participation_frequency and user_membership.preferences_notes when available. On submission, it calls updateGroupMembershipPreferences using the current membershipId (user_membership.membership_id), along with the edited participation_frequency and preferences_notes.

Displays confirmation and next-step information after a successful join or preference update. It shows message text returned from joinVolunteerGroup or updateGroupMembershipPreferences and can reference schedule or location information from the group details to contextualize what the user should do next.

After the user has an active membership (either detected from getVolunteerGroupDetails.user_membership or immediately after a successful joinVolunteerGroup call), this component shows a View this group in My Dashboard link that navigates to dashboard.html.
Always provides a Back to volunteer listings link that navigates the user to volunteer.html when clicked, independent of membership status.

Aggregates and displays success and error messages arising from joinVolunteerGroup and updateGroupMembershipPreferences calls (e.g., join or update confirmations, validation errors, or network issues); messages are sourced from the respective interface response message fields or from client-side error handling.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, max-width layout based on a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters (desktop breakpoint). Content appears within a central column spanning roughly 8–10 grid columns, with generous side margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom arrangement with a single, centered content column on a 12‑column grid. A minimal top navigation (outside this spec) sits above everything. Within the main content, the first major section is a pale green hero-like panel that introduces the volunteer group: a back-navigation link, the group’s name and key summary, and a thoughtful descriptive paragraph. Directly beneath this text, within the same hero region, a wide illustrative image (part of the summary component) occupies the lower portion, echoing the mockup’s text-then-image structure. Below the hero, a second section focuses on participation: membership status, join/update controls, preferences, and feedback. A final, low-emphasis navigation area appears at the bottom, linking back to the user’s dashboard.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom arrangement with a single, centered content column on a 12‑column grid. A minimal top navigation (outside this spec) sits above everything. Within the main content, the first major section is a pale green hero-like panel that introduces the volunteer group: a back-navigation link, the group’s name and key summary, and a thoughtful descriptive paragraph. Directly beneath this text, within the same hero region, a wide illustrative image (part of the summary component) occupies the lower portion, echoing the mockup’s text-then-image structure. Below the hero, a second section focuses on participation: membership status, join/update controls, preferences, and feedback. A final, low-emphasis navigation area appears at the bottom, linking back to the user’s dashboard.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The layout centers content within a max-width column (~960–1040px) with wide side gutters (~10–12% of viewport width on each side). The hero section occupies roughly 70–80% of the initial viewport height on desktop, with the upper 50–60% reserved for the group title and description and the lower 40–50% for a wide supporting image. Vertical spacing between major sections is generous (48–64px), and within each section, related elements (heading + paragraph, status + CTA, form fields + feedback) are separated by 24–32px. Forms and panels follow the component padding guidelines: around 24px top/bottom and 32px left/right internally, to keep everything airy.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally sparse, with a calm, editorial feel. Only one primary column is used; there are no dense sidebars. Text blocks are kept to comfortable line lengths and high line-height, with ample white space above and below headings, between paragraphs, and around forms. Membership controls and forms are broken into clear, vertically stacked blocks so the page never feels crowded, even when multiple feedback messages or confirmation panels are visible.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F-pattern. The eye starts at the top left with the logo and main navigation, then moves right across the nav. It then drops to the back-to-listings link at the top of the hero, flows down to the large group name and summary (replacing the mockup’s ‘Biodiversity’ heading), continues into the descriptive paragraph beneath, and then down to the wide environmental/group image. After the hero, the eye continues downward into the participation section: a status banner, the join/update CTA, the preferences form, and any inline feedback or confirmation messages. Finally, attention settles on the low-key dashboard/navigation links at the bottom. Any floating accessibility control (outside this spec) would sit at the lower right and remains visually secondary.

layout.overall_description: The Volunteer Group Detail page presents itself as a calm, editorial-style layout. At the very top, a minimal horizontal navigation spans the full width, with a logo on the left and a light, unobtrusive nav on the right. Below this, the main content sits in a centered column framed by generous side gutters, all resting on a very light, almost white background. The first major block is a large, pale green hero panel that stretches across the central column and takes up most of the initial viewport height. At the top-left of this hero, a subtle text link leads back to the group listings. Just beneath it, the group’s name and key details appear as a bold, left-aligned editorial heading with a few concise lines of metadata. A multi-line descriptive paragraph follows, with generous line height and spacing, giving the text room to breathe. In the lower portion of this hero panel, a wide, soft-toned environmental image related to the group spans almost the full width of the central column, echoing the mockup’s text-over-image composition. Below the hero, a new section begins on the main page background: a compact but prominent membership status banner leads into a left-aligned join/update CTA, followed by a spacious preferences form. Each block is separated by clear vertical gaps, and feedback messages or confirmation panels appear inline beneath the relevant forms without making the page feel crowded. At the very bottom of the central column, a low-emphasis dashboard/navigation panel offers a path back to the user’s broader volunteering area. The entire page feels light, quiet, and intentional, with a clear vertical reading path from group overview, to visual context, to participation options, and finally to navigation away from the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual, structural component that initializes URL-driven state before any visible content renders. It sits logically at the top of the page’s component tree, above the main layout wrapper, but it does not occupy any on-screen space, introduce padding, or alter the visual grid. It has no visible footprint and does not affect the spatial relationships between other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; no spatial behavior. Runs before layout and does not participate in positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component also exists purely at the logic layer. When actively loading, it can optionally trigger a subtle loading state within the main content column (such as a faint text placeholder or skeleton in the hero area), but the loader itself does not create its own separate visual block. Its effect is to temporarily reserve the main content region (hero and subsequent sections) while data is being fetched, keeping the overall centered column and spacing intact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual in normal state; orchestrates loading for the main content area without adding an independent layout element.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When a load error occurs, this panel appears in the primary content column where the hero section would normally be. It is centered horizontally within the max-width column and sits approximately 80–96px below the top navigation, maintaining wide side gutters. The panel itself resembles a soft card: it spans about 70–80% of the central column’s width, with 24px top/bottom and 32px left/right internal padding, and a very light, warm-tinted background that distinguishes it from the page body. There is generous whitespace (48–64px) above and below the panel, so it feels like a singular, important message in an otherwise empty canvas. No other main sections (hero, membership, dashboard links) are shown below when this error panel is visible, keeping the focus entirely on resolving the error.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level component that replaces the hero and subsequent content when visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The group summary panel forms the textual core of the hero section at the top of the page. It sits within a full-width pale green band that spans the central content column. This hero band begins about 64px below the main navigation and extends down to cover roughly 70–80% of the viewport height on desktop. Inside this band, the summary panel is aligned to the left within the central column, starting about 48px from the top edge of the band. It spans roughly 8–10 of the 12 grid columns (about 70–80% of the content width), leaving generous padding on the left and right (32px internal). The group name appears as a large, editorial-style H1 at the top of this panel, with the first line aligning visually with the left edge of the central column and ample whitespace above and below. Below the title, brief summary details (location, frequency, focus area) are arranged in short lines, maintaining a comfortable line length and 24–32px spacing to the description. The lower portion of the hero (about the bottom 40–50% of the hero band) is reserved for a wide, environmental image associated with the group, which is visually part of this panel: it stretches nearly the full width of the central column, with subtle rounding at the corners and 24–32px of space above it to separate it from the text. Overall, the panel dominates the visual hierarchy on first view, mirroring the mockup’s strong title-and-image hero aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width hero content within the central column; scales responsively but remains vertically dominant near the top of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The group description section sits immediately beneath the main title and summary text within the hero’s central column, effectively acting as the multi-line paragraph block seen in the mockup. It is left-aligned with the group heading and spans the same text width (around 70–80% of the content column). There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px between the summary metadata and the first line of the description. The text flows across multiple short lines with a generous line height, and it occupies primarily the upper half of the hero band, leaving substantial open space below. A 24–32px gap separates the last line of this paragraph from the top edge of the wide image that occupies the lower part of the hero. This arrangement creates a calm reading rhythm: heading at the top, a rich but breathable descriptive paragraph beneath, then the supporting visual image below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline text block within the hero section; expands vertically as needed but never competes with the image below for horizontal space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The membership status banner marks the beginning of the second major section, which focuses on participation. This section sits directly below the hero band, with a vertical gap of roughly 64px to clearly separate it from the image above. The banner is aligned to the same central column as the hero content, spanning about 80–90% of that column’s width and centered horizontally. It appears as a low-profile, rounded rectangle strip with a soft tinted background (e.g., a pale green or neutral tone), 16–20px internal vertical padding, and 24–32px horizontal padding. The text is left-aligned and concise, indicating the user’s current membership status. There is comfortable whitespace (24px) above the banner and 24–32px below it before the join/update CTA, so the banner reads as a clear section header for the membership controls without feeling like a heavy alert.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline banner at the top of the participation section; does not stick to the viewport but remains anchored in the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The join-group CTA sits immediately below the membership-status banner within the same central column, forming the first interactive focus area. It is positioned about 24–32px beneath the banner, aligned on the left with all other content. The CTA area can be a compact card or horizontal block that spans roughly 60–70% of the content column, leaving some breathing room to the right, with 24px top/bottom and 32px left/right internal padding. A primary button (e.g., ‘Join group’ or ‘Update participation’) is placed near the top-left of this card, with any brief explanatory copy above or beside it. The block has ample whitespace below (32–40px) before the preferences form, so the CTA is clearly separated and highly scannable as a next step after reading the status. Visually, this area stands out more than the surrounding text through the use of a slightly stronger foreground color on the button while still adhering to the calm, non-commercial aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static interactive block in the main vertical flow; it adjusts its height based on content but maintains its left alignment and relative spacing to the status banner and preferences form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The membership preferences form appears beneath the join-group CTA as a separate but closely related block, maintaining the same central alignment and width. It starts roughly 32–40px below the CTA card, providing a clear visual step-down in the interaction flow. The form spans about 70–80% of the content column, with consistent internal padding (24px top/bottom, 32px left/right) and a very subtle border or shadow to distinguish it from the background without feeling heavy. Form fields are arranged in a single column to preserve the editorial, low-density feel, each separated by 16–24px vertically. Section headings or field groups within the form have 24–32px of spacing above them. The bottom of the form includes its primary action controls (e.g., ‘Save preferences’) aligned to the left, with 24–32px of whitespace below the button before any feedback or confirmation components appear. Overall, the form feels airy and approachable, never cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form block that grows vertically with its fields; remains within the central column and does not scroll independently of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The membership confirmation panel is displayed inline in the participation section once a membership action has succeeded. It sits directly beneath the membership preferences form or join CTA (depending on the flow), with a gap of about 24–32px separating it from the preceding component. It aligns with the left edge of the form and spans approximately the same width, reinforcing their relationship. The panel uses a lightweight, success-tinted background (for example, a very pale green) with 16–24px internal padding and rounded corners, making it feel like a calm, reassuring message rather than a loud alert. There is ample white space (32–40px) below the panel before any navigation or dashboard link content, so the confirmation stands out as the final step in the participation flow before moving on.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline info panel; appears or disappears based on state but otherwise behaves like a standard block-level element in the vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dashboard link panel forms the final section at the bottom of the page’s main content column. It appears after the participation components, roughly 48–64px below the last interactive or confirmation element, maintaining the page’s generous section spacing. The panel spans about 70–80% of the content column and is left-aligned. It may be styled as a simple, low-emphasis text block or a lightly framed card with minimal padding (16–24px) that offers one or more navigation options back to the user’s dashboard or broader volunteer area. The typography is understated and slightly smaller than headings, signaling that this is a tertiary navigation area rather than a primary call to action. Extra whitespace (32–48px) below this panel leads smoothly into the page’s natural bottom margin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static navigational block anchored at the bottom of the main content flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-listings link is positioned at the top-left of the hero section, serving as a subtle breadcrumb. It appears within the central content column, about 24–32px below the bottom edge of the main navigation bar and roughly 32–40px above the group title. The link is left-aligned with the hero’s text column and occupies minimal vertical height, using a lightweight, calm text style (possibly with a discreet left-pointing icon). Ample whitespace surrounds it—there is clear space above (from the nav) and below (to the H1), so it does not compete visually with the main title. When scanned, the eye naturally passes from the global nav down to this link and then to the prominent group name, reinforcing the editorial reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline link, anchored at the top of the hero section within the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inline feedback messages appear contextually near the join-group CTA and membership preferences form, rather than as a separate global block. Within each relevant area, messages are left-aligned and span the same width as the parent component (CTA card or form). They sit immediately beneath the element they refer to—typically 8–16px below a field group or action button—while maintaining at least 24px of separation from unrelated content below to avoid visual clutter. Each message may use subtle color cues (e.g., soft amber for warnings, pale red for errors, pale green for minor success notes) but remains in keeping with the calm aesthetic, with small icons and comfortable line height. When multiple messages are present, they stack vertically with 8–12px spacing between them, still within the parent component’s padding. They do not introduce their own large card or new section; instead they ‘live’ inside the existing blocks, preserving the overall low-density vertical rhythm of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, context-attached elements that occupy space only within their parent components; they do not affect the global layout structure beyond increasing the height of those parents.

name: Lifestyle Quiz
title: Lifestyle Quiz
description: Interactive quiz to assess a user's lifestyle across environmental impact areas, summarize results, and recommend conservation challenges that can be activated and managed via the dashboard.

core_features: Load and display an introduction to the lifestyle quiz, including sections and estimated completion time, using getLifestyleQuizIntro.
Start a new lifestyle quiz session, retrieve all questions and options, and organize them by section using startLifestyleQuiz.
Guide users through the quiz by section, allowing them to select single or multiple options per question and track their progress.
Submit all quiz answers at the end of the quiz to generate a results summary and initial recommended challenges using submitLifestyleQuizAnswers.
Display the results summary, including interpretive scores by section, based on the response from submitLifestyleQuizAnswers.
Retrieve and display a list of quiz-based recommended challenges with optional server-side filtering by category and difficulty using getQuizRecommendedChallenges.
Provide filter controls for recommended challenges, updating the URL parameters and reloading recommendations based on selected filters.
Enable activation of individual recommended challenges, updating the challenge status and dashboard visibility via activateChallengeFromQuiz.
Allow users to navigate from the results view to the dashboard page (dashboard.html) after activating challenges.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – View quiz introduction and start quiz: 1) User navigates to lifestyle_quiz.html (or via header link). 2) Page reads URL parameter step; if missing or set to 'intro', it calls getLifestyleQuizIntro. 3) Intro component displays sections and estimated time from the API response. 4) User clicks 'Start Quiz'; the page updates the URL to lifestyle_quiz.html?step=quiz&section=<first-section-key> and calls startLifestyleQuiz. 5) The questions returned are stored in page state and used by quiz components.

Workflow 2 – Answer quiz questions by section: 1) With step=quiz, page parses the section URL parameter to determine which group of questions to display. 2) Quiz question form shows all questions matching that section from the startLifestyleQuiz response, respecting allow_multiple for each question. 3) User selects options (single or multiple) for each question in the current section. 4) User clicks 'Next Section' or 'Previous Section'; answers for the current section are stored in page state and the URL section parameter is updated to the next/previous section key. 5) A progress component updates to show completion state based on how many sections and questions have recorded answers. 6) On the final section, user clicks 'Finish Quiz', triggering answer validation and a call to submitLifestyleQuizAnswers with the structured answers payload.

Workflow 3 – Submit quiz and view results: 1) When 'Finish Quiz' is clicked, submitLifestyleQuizAnswers is called with all answers gathered from page state. 2) On success, the page stores results_summary_text, impact_scores_by_section, and recommended_challenges from the response. 3) The URL is updated to lifestyle_quiz.html?step=results (optionally removing the section parameter). 4) The results summary component reads the stored results data and displays the human-readable summary and per-section scores/interpretations. 5) The recommendations area initially uses either the recommended_challenges returned from submitLifestyleQuizAnswers or triggers getQuizRecommendedChallenges without filters to load the full recommended set for the latest completed quiz session.

Workflow 4 – Filter recommended challenges: 1) On the results view (step=results), if URL parameters for category and difficulty filters are present, these values are passed as the filters argument to getQuizRecommendedChallenges. 2) When the user changes filter controls (category and/or difficulty), the filter component updates the URL to include or modify category and difficulty, then triggers getQuizRecommendedChallenges with those values. 3) The recommendations list component renders the updated recommended_challenges, including whether each is already activated (is_already_activated). 4) The user can clear filters, which removes category and difficulty from the URL and reloads unfiltered recommendations via getQuizRecommendedChallenges.

Workflow 5 – Activate recommended challenges and go to dashboard: 1) In the recommendations list, each non-activated challenge exposes an 'Activate' or 'Add to My Challenges' control. 2) When the user clicks this control, activateChallengeFromQuiz is called with the corresponding challengeId. 3) On success, the returned activated_challenge object is used to update page state: the corresponding list item is marked as activated; the component may also trigger a refresh via getQuizRecommendedChallenges to update is_already_activated flags. 4) The URL may be updated to include an activatedChallengeId parameter to reflect the last activated challenge for feedback display. 5) User clicks 'Go to My Challenges'; the page navigates to dashboard.html, optionally appending URL parameters (e.g., ?view=my_challenges) if desired by the broader application, without storing state in localStorage.
interactions: On initial page load, parse URL parameters (step, section, category, difficulty, activatedChallengeId) and decide whether to show the intro view, quiz view, or results view, then call getLifestyleQuizIntro, startLifestyleQuiz, or getQuizRecommendedChallenges/submitLifestyleQuizAnswers-derived data accordingly.

When the intro view is displayed, call getLifestyleQuizIntro once; bind the returned sections and their estimated_time_minutes into an intro component; expose a 'Start Quiz' button that, when clicked, updates the step and section URL parameters and triggers startLifestyleQuiz.

When startLifestyleQuiz is called, store the returned questions and question options in page state; build a derived list of section identifiers and their question counts to support navigation and progress indicators.

In the quiz view, for each question in the current section, allow the user to select options: use single-choice interaction when allow_multiple is false and multi-choice interaction when allow_multiple is true; store selections in page state keyed by question_id and option_id(s).

Provide 'Next Section' and 'Previous Section' buttons; on click, validate that required interactions for the current section are complete (if such validation is desired), persist the current section's answers in page state, update the section URL parameter to the next/previous section key, and scroll/focus appropriately.

Provide a 'Finish Quiz' button in the last section; on click, aggregate all stored answers across sections into the format expected by submitLifestyleQuizAnswers, call the interface, handle loading and error states, then on success store results data in page state and update the URL step parameter to 'results'.

On entering the results view, render the results summary text and impact_scores_by_section from page state; if results data is missing (e.g., user navigated directly with step=results), show a functional message and redirect them back to the intro by updating the URL to step=intro.

In the results view, when filters for challenge recommendations are changed (e.g., user selects a new category or difficulty), update category and difficulty URL parameters, call getQuizRecommendedChallenges with a filters object derived from those parameters, and re-render the recommendations list with the returned data.

Within the challenge recommendations list, each item exposes an 'Activate' button when is_already_activated is false; clicking this button calls activateChallengeFromQuiz with that challenge_id, shows an in-progress state, then on success updates the item to reflect an activated state and optionally refreshes recommendations by re-calling getQuizRecommendedChallenges.

Show a 'Go to My Challenges' control in the results view whenever at least one challenge has been activated during this session; clicking this control navigates to dashboard.html without using localStorage, optionally including URL parameters to select the appropriate dashboard tab.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL query parameters to control which functional state is displayed and how data is fetched: 1) step: determines high-level view. Valid values include 'intro', 'quiz', and 'results'. If step is missing or not recognized, the page defaults to 'intro' and calls getLifestyleQuizIntro. For step='intro', no quiz session is active; the intro component is shown. For step='quiz', the page assumes a quiz session should be (or has been) started, calls startLifestyleQuiz if questions are not yet loaded into page state, and shows the quiz question form. For step='results', the page expects results data in memory (produced by a recent submitLifestyleQuizAnswers call) and shows the results summary and recommendations. If results data is not available in memory when step='results', the page falls back to step='intro' by updating the URL. 2) section: used only when step='quiz' to determine which subset of questions to show. The value corresponds to the section identifiers provided by getLifestyleQuizIntro and present in the startLifestyleQuiz questions. When the user navigates between sections, the section parameter is updated, allowing direct linking back to a specific section. Section answers are stored in page state keyed by question_id rather than in the URL. 3) category and difficulty: used when step='results' to filter recommended challenges. If present, these parameters are converted into a filters object passed to getQuizRecommendedChallenges; if absent, getQuizRecommendedChallenges is called without these filters. When the user changes filters via the filter component, the page updates category and difficulty in the URL and re-fetches recommendations. 4) activatedChallengeId: optionally set after a successful activateChallengeFromQuiz call to identify the most recently activated challenge. The page uses this parameter to display contextual feedback (e.g., confirmation message associated with that challenge) and can be cleared if the user dismisses the message, updating the URL accordingly. 5) No quiz results, answers, or other transient data are stored in localStorage; all such data resides in in-memory page state. URL parameters control routing and filtering but not the full quiz content. Direct navigation to lifestyle_quiz.html with different parameter combinations triggers the described logic but cannot reconstruct past quiz sessions without re-running startLifestyleQuiz and submitLifestyleQuizAnswers within that session.

components.functionality: Parses URL parameters (step, section, category, difficulty, activatedChallengeId), determines which high-level view (intro, quiz, results) to show, and orchestrates calls to getLifestyleQuizIntro, startLifestyleQuiz, submitLifestyleQuizAnswers-derived state, and getQuizRecommendedChallenges as needed.

When the current step is 'intro', calls getLifestyleQuizIntro once, stores the returned sections data in page state, and provides it to the intro display and the subsequent quiz navigation configuration.

Displays the intro information for the lifestyle quiz, including the list of sections and their estimated_time_minutes as returned by getLifestyleQuizIntro, and provides a 'Start Quiz' action that initializes the quiz step.

Triggers startLifestyleQuiz when the user starts the quiz or when step='quiz' is entered without existing question data, then normalizes and stores the questions and options grouped by section for downstream components.

Uses the list of sections and answered questions to compute and display quiz progress (e.g., which section is current, how many sections/questions have recorded answers) and allows navigation to specific sections when appropriate.

For the current section (derived from the section URL parameter), renders all associated questions and answer options from the startLifestyleQuiz response, enforces single- or multi-select based on allow_multiple, and maintains the user's selected option_ids for each question_id in page state.

Provides 'Previous Section', 'Next Section', and 'Finish Quiz' buttons depending on the current section; manages section-level answer persistence, validates user input, updates the section URL parameter, and triggers submitLifestyleQuizAnswers when the user finishes the final section.

Displays the results_summary_text and impact_scores_by_section received from submitLifestyleQuizAnswers, or, if these are not available when step='results', triggers a redirect back to the intro view via the router.

Reads the current category and difficulty URL parameters to initialize filter controls, allows the user to modify them, and updates the URL and getQuizRecommendedChallenges filter arguments whenever a filter is changed or cleared.

Responsible for calling getQuizRecommendedChallenges when the results view is active. Builds the filters argument from the category and difficulty URL parameters, calls the interface, and stores recommended_challenges in page state for rendering.

Displays the recommended_challenges collection (whether sourced from submitLifestyleQuizAnswers or getQuizRecommendedChallenges) and shows, for each challenge, its name, description, category, difficulty, default_frequency, and activation status (based on is_already_activated). Provides 'Activate' or 'Add to My Challenges' actions for non-activated challenges.

Shows a contextual confirmation or status message when a challenge has been successfully activated, using the activatedChallengeId URL parameter and/or the most recent activateChallengeFromQuiz response to identify which challenge was affected.

Provides a control on the results view that, once at least one challenge has been activated in the current session, allows the user to navigate to dashboard.html to view and manage their active challenges.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, max-width layout based on a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters (desktop breakpoint). Content appears within a central column spanning roughly 8–10 grid columns, with generous side margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, centered primary content column on top of a very light, pale-green background band that reads like an editorial hero. Content flows top-to-bottom in distinct states: (1) Intro state with a large title and supporting text occupying the upper half of the viewport; (2) Quiz state where a slim progress indicator sits at the top of the column, followed by the question form and navigation controls; (3) Results state where a results summary takes over the top hero space and is followed by filters and a vertically stacked recommendation list. Each state keeps elements in a vertical, linear flow, with one dominant block per screen region.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, centered primary content column on top of a very light, pale-green background band that reads like an editorial hero. Content flows top-to-bottom in distinct states: (1) Intro state with a large title and supporting text occupying the upper half of the viewport; (2) Quiz state where a slim progress indicator sits at the top of the column, followed by the question form and navigation controls; (3) Results state where a results summary takes over the top hero space and is followed by filters and a vertically stacked recommendation list. Each state keeps elements in a vertical, linear flow, with one dominant block per screen region.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The layout uses a 12-column grid with the main content column centered and spanning roughly 8–10 columns (about 70–80% of the page width), leaving generous 10–15% margins on both sides. On desktop, the hero-like bands (intro and results summary) each occupy around 50–60% of the viewport height, with the remaining 40–50% given to the lower functional area (quiz form or recommendations list). Vertical spacing between major sections is around 48–64px, and related elements within a section are separated by 24–32px. Components like forms and lists sit within padded panels (about 24px top/bottom and 32px left/right), giving text and controls room to breathe.
layout_strategies.content_density: The content is intentionally low-density and editorial, with short line lengths, high line height, and generous white space around all interactive elements. Each primary component (intro, form, results summary, recommendation list) is visually isolated with ample padding and margins. Only a few key elements are visible at once in any state, avoiding clutter: a large header with supporting text, then a single question or a structured results block, then a simple row of controls or list cards. There is no tight packing of UI; every group of elements has 24–32px gaps so the page feels calm and thoughtful.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F-pattern: the user starts at the top-left with the page title in the hero-style intro or results summary, then scans rightward across any supporting labels (like progress text) before moving down to body text or main content (question form or results details). From there, the eye continues downward to the interactive controls (navigation buttons or filters), and finally to the larger visual block at the bottom (recommendation list). A floating accessibility button (from the original mockup) remains in the bottom-right as a secondary focal point; bottom-aligned elements like quiz navigation controls and the final dashboard link are positioned so they do not visually collide with that area.

layout.overall_description: The Lifestyle Quiz page sits within a centered, spacious editorial layout on a very light background. Below the site’s global navigation, the main content column—about three-quarters of the page width—is framed by wide, calm margins on both sides. In the intro state, a pale-green hero band fills most of the viewport, with the page title “Lifestyle Quiz” and explanatory copy left-aligned in the upper half of this band. The lower half of that same band is mostly open space, with a single, understated start control sitting near the bottom-left of the text block, reinforcing the quiet, editorial feel. When the quiz is in progress, the top of the column holds a slim, unobtrusive progress indicator, followed by a softly framed question panel that fills the center of the screen. Answers are generously spaced, and navigation buttons rest in the lower-right of the column, with ample margin above the bottom edge of the viewport and clear separation from the floating accessibility button near the page’s bottom-right corner. Once the quiz is complete, the layout transitions to a results state where a new hero-like summary replaces the intro at the top, again occupying roughly half the viewport, while a set of subtle filters and a vertically stacked list of recommended challenges fill the lower portion. A status banner appears just above the list when actions occur, and a quiet ‘Go to dashboard’ link anchors the very bottom of the column, leaving a cushion of white space below. Overall, the page feels calm, ordered, and minimal, with a clear top-to-bottom narrative that moves from understanding (intro), through reflection (quiz), into action (challenges and dashboard).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The step router is a non-visual controller that conceptually sits at the top of the main content column, just below the global navigation. It does not render any visible interface itself and therefore does not occupy any physical space in the layout. Instead, it determines which major content block is shown in the central column at any given time: either the intro hero section, the quiz in-progress layout (progress + question + navigation), or the results and recommendations layout. Visually, it is as if this component swaps the large hero and central content panels within the same fixed, centered column without changing margins, padding, or overall spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual state controller; no rendered footprint, does not affect spacing directly other than which child components are mounted in the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The intro loader is also non-visual by default; it prepares the intro content before the intro hero is shown. When an explicit loading state is needed, it can momentarily display a very subtle skeleton block or a small, centered spinner inside the same central column where the intro section will appear. In that case, the loading indicator sits centrally within a pale-green band occupying roughly the upper 50–60% of the viewport, matching the eventual intro section’s footprint and preserving the large side gutters and top/bottom padding so the layout stays calm even while loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual in normal operation; when loading, it temporarily occupies the hero intro area with a centered spinner or skeleton inside the same max-width column, then is removed without leaving extra gaps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The intro display forms a hero-like, pale-green content band directly beneath the site navigation, centered on the page. It spans about 70–80% of the viewport width, aligned within the main column, and extends vertically to fill approximately the upper 50–60% of the viewport height. Internally, the section has generous padding (around 24px top/bottom and 32px left/right), ensuring the content does not crowd the edges. At the top-left of this band sits the main heading for the page (e.g., “Lifestyle Quiz”), with a large top margin from the nav so it doesn’t feel cramped. Beneath the heading, separated by ~24px, is a multi-line introductory paragraph with high line height, constrained to the same left-aligned text column so the lines remain short and easy to read. The lower half of the band is mostly open negative space; near the lower-left of the text block (not touching the band’s edges), there is room for a primary ‘Start quiz’ control, which appears as part of this component’s content. The right side of the panel remains mostly empty, reinforcing the minimalist, editorial aesthetic. Below the band, a 48–64px gap separates the hero intro from whatever section appears next when the quiz starts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the main scroll flow; occupies the top hero region of the main column. On scroll, it moves up naturally like any other section, without sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The session starter operates behind the scenes when the user triggers the start of the quiz from within the intro hero. It does not have its own distinct visual panel; instead, its presence is expressed as a small, local loading affordance associated with the start control. When active, a subtle spinner or progress indicator appears adjacent to or overlaying the start button within the lower portion of the intro band, without altering the band’s size or spacing. This keeps the large margins, central column, and airy layout intact while making the loading state visible only in the immediate area around the start action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual data loader; manifests only as a small inline loading indicator near the start control inside the intro section, and does not change the overall layout or section dimensions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: In the quiz in-progress state, the section progress indicator occupies a slim horizontal strip at the top of the main content column, just below the global navigation and any minimal page title if present. It spans the full width of the central column (about 70–80% of the page width), with 24–32px of padding above and below any text or progress bar elements. The progress label (e.g., “Section 1 of 5” or “12% complete”) is aligned to the left, starting at the same left margin as the main text in the form below, reinforcing the vertical alignment. If a horizontal progress bar is used, it runs underneath this label, extending toward the right but stopping comfortably before the column edge to maintain airy side margins. There is roughly 24–32px of vertical space between this indicator and the quiz question form beneath it, so the indicator reads as a light, secondary header rather than a heavy bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scroll flow, sitting immediately above the question form. On longer pages, it scrolls off-screen with the content; it is not sticky or fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quiz question form is the primary focal block when the quiz is in progress. It appears directly under the section progress indicator, separated by a 24–32px gap, and centered within the same main column. The form is contained in a softly defined panel or card on a very pale-green or white background that spans about 70–80% of the viewport width and occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport height on desktop. The panel has generous internal padding (approximately 24px top/bottom and 32px left/right). At the top-left of the panel sits the current question text, with a strong typographic presence and a 16–24px gap above any help text or subheading. Below the question, answer options (such as radio buttons or sliders) are stacked vertically, each separated by 16–24px of vertical space so they are easy to scan and tap without feeling cramped. The panel leaves comfortable empty space at its bottom so that the form content does not press against the quiz navigation controls below; there is typically 24–32px of empty vertical space between the last input and the bottom edge of the panel. The right side of the panel is kept visually clean, with no dense sidebars or columns, preserving the simple, editorial flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block centered in the main column. On smaller viewports or longer questions, the form can grow vertically and push navigation controls downward, but it always maintains top and side padding consistent with the spacing scale.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quiz navigation controls sit directly beneath the question form, within the same main column and aligned with its content. They occupy a shallow horizontal band, extending across the width of the central column but with their actual buttons clustered toward the bottom-right portion of that band. There is a 24px gap between the bottom edge of the question form panel and the navigation band, ensuring the controls feel separate but related. Primary actions such as “Next” or “Submit” are right-aligned, with any secondary actions (e.g., “Back”) placed immediately to the left with consistent 8–16px gaps. The band itself has ample whitespace above and below the buttons (around 16–24px), and the bottom of the band sits 48–64px above the bottom of the viewport or page content, so the controls do not hug the edge of the screen. On the far bottom-right of the viewport, a floating accessibility button remains visible; the navigation controls are positioned so their rightmost edge stops slightly left of this floating element, avoiding visual overlap and maintaining a clean, uncluttered lower-right corner.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow, anchored directly under the form. It does not stick to the bottom of the viewport but naturally moves with scrolling, always maintaining a comfortable bottom margin and avoiding collision with the floating accessibility button area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the quiz is complete, the results summary replaces the intro hero as the primary top section of the page. This component forms a broad, pale-green band that begins directly below the global navigation and spans the same 70–80% width central column. Vertically, it occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport height, echoing the intro’s spacious feel. Inside, content is left-aligned with 24–32px padding all around. At the upper-left of the band, a prominent heading such as “Your Lifestyle Impact” is displayed, with a 16–24px gap below leading into a brief descriptive paragraph summarizing the user’s results. Beneath this, there is space for key metrics or tags (e.g., primary impact areas) arranged either in a single column or a subtle grid, but still respecting generous spacing (16–24px between items). The lower portion of the band remains fairly open, giving the sense of a calm, editorial summary rather than a crowded dashboard. A 48–64px vertical gap separates the bottom of this summary band from the filters and recommendations area below, clearly segmenting the results narrative from the action-oriented content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static hero-like section at the top of the results state. It scrolls normally with the page, and does not stick to the top once the user scrolls past it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The challenge recommendation filters sit directly below the results summary band, separated by approximately 32–48px of vertical space. They are left-aligned within the same central column and constrained to roughly 60–70% of the column’s width so they feel like a compact toolset rather than a full-width form. The filter controls (dropdowns, chips, toggles) are arranged in one or two rows, with 16px horizontal gaps between controls and 8–16px vertical gaps between rows if wrapping occurs. The filter row has about 16–24px of padding above and below its elements, preserving the light, airy aesthetic. There is a clear 24–32px gap between the bottom of the filters and any status banner or the top of the recommendations list, helping the user perceive filters as a distinct, but closely related, strip of controls tied to the list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal control band; it stays directly above the recommendations list and scrolls off-screen normally as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recommendations loader occupies the same visual footprint as the recommendations list but appears only when the list content is loading or refreshing. It is centered within the main column beneath the filters, spanning about 70–80% of the viewport width. During loading, it may display a single, pale-green card-shaped skeleton or a small cluster of skeleton list items stacked vertically, each separated by 16–24px, emulating the spacing of the eventual list. This gives the impression of a structured, but still very light, placeholder area. The loader respects the same top margin (24–32px from filters or feedback banner) and bottom margin (48–64px above the page bottom or next section) that the real list uses, so there is no layout jump when content appears.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the recommendations list in its exact location and size. It does not introduce new sections; it simply occupies the list’s panel area while data is loading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The challenge recommendations list is the dominant lower panel in the results state. It begins beneath the filters (or beneath a feedback banner if one is visible), with a 24–32px vertical gap, and spans the entire central column (about 70–80% of the page width). Vertically, it can expand to occupy the remaining viewport height and beyond, allowing for scrolling. The list is composed of individual challenge cards stacked in a single column. Each card is a softly bordered or lightly shaded block with 24px internal padding and 16–24px vertical spacing between adjacent cards, keeping the column of content feeling open and scannable. Within each card, the challenge title and short description are left-aligned, with action buttons (e.g., “Activate challenge”) positioned on the right or aligned at the bottom-right, but never extending beyond the column’s right boundary. The list stops well before the right edge of the page, leaving a wide outer gutter and visual clearance around the floating accessibility button in the bottom-right corner of the viewport. There is a generous 48–64px margin below the final card before reaching the bottom of the page content, which leaves space for the ‘Go to dashboard’ link and a buffer of white space below it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable list within the main document flow. The list itself doesn’t have internal scrolling separate from the page; it grows with content and allows the whole page to scroll vertically.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The challenge activation feedback appears as a slim status banner between the filters and the recommendations list whenever a challenge is activated or an action requires user confirmation. It spans the full width of the central column, aligned with the filters and list (about 70–80% of the page width), and has a slightly deeper green or tinted background to distinguish it from the rest of the content while still feeling soft and integrated. The banner has 16–24px top and bottom padding and 24–32px horizontal padding, with its message text left-aligned and any dismiss icon or subtle action link positioned on the right. There is a 16–24px gap above and below the banner: above, separating it from the filter row, and below, separating it from the top of the recommendations list. When visible, it gently pushes the list down without overlapping it, maintaining the overall calm, stepped vertical rhythm of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Transient, inline banner that appears in the flow between filters and list. It does not float or stick; it shifts other components down while visible and collapses without leftover space when dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ‘Go to dashboard’ link anchors the end of the user’s journey on this page. It is placed near the bottom of the main content column, beneath the last item in the recommendations list. There is a generous vertical gap of around 32–48px between the bottom of the final recommendation card and this link, ensuring it reads as a concluding call-to-action rather than just another list item. The link is left-aligned with the rest of the textual content in the column, occupying only as much horizontal space as needed (it does not stretch full-width), and may be styled as a calm text link or a subtle ghost button consistent with the minimalist aesthetic. Below the link, an additional 48–64px of white space separates it from the actual page bottom, creating a relaxed visual endpoint and preventing the link from feeling cramped against the edge of the viewport, even when the page is scrolled to its lowest point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, bottom-of-flow navigation element. It does not stick or float; it simply marks the natural end of the central column content and moves with the page’s overall scroll.

name: My Dashboard
title: My Dashboard
description: A personal dashboard that consolidates the user's conservation challenges, events, action plan, carbon goals, donation pledges, reading lists, and volunteer memberships, with quick controls to update challenge frequency/status and navigate to detailed tools.

core_features: Load a consolidated overview of the user's conservation activity using getDashboardOverview and display summarized sections for challenges, upcoming events, action plan, carbon goal, donation pledges, reading lists, and volunteer memberships.

Display a detailed My Challenges section using getMyChallengesOverview, allowing users to change the frequency of each activated challenge via setActivatedChallengeFrequency and update the challenge status (active, paused, completed) via updateActivatedChallengeStatus.

Display a My Events section using getMyEventsOverview (and optionally the upcoming_events from getDashboardOverview) that shows bookmarked and registered events, and allows navigation to events.html and event_detail.html?eventId={event_id}.

Show an Action Plan summary using getActiveActionPlanOverview (and action_plan_summary from getDashboardOverview), including total actions, cost, and impact, with navigation to action_planner.html to manage the plan in detail.

Show a Carbon Goal summary using getCarbonCalculatorSummary (and carbon_goal_summary from getDashboardOverview), including latest assessment and active reduction goal, with navigation to carbon_calculator.html to update the footprint or goals.
