Summarize active donation pledges using getActiveDonationPledgesSummary (and donation_pledges from getDashboardOverview), with navigation to projects.html and project_detail.html?projectId={project_id}.
Summarize reading lists using getMyReadingListsOverview (and reading_lists_summary from getDashboardOverview) and provide navigation to my_reading_lists.html to manage lists and articles.
Summarize volunteer group memberships using getVolunteerMembershipsSummary and provide navigation to volunteer.html and group_detail.html?groupId={group_id}.
Offer navigation from My Challenges to lifestyle_quiz.html to retake the lifestyle quiz and discover new recommended challenges.
user_workflows:
Manage activated challenges:
1) User opens dashboard.html (optionally with URL parameter section=challenges).
2) dashboard-initializer component calls getDashboardOverview.
3) my-challenges-panel component calls getMyChallengesOverview to load the full list of ActivatedChallenge records and associated Challenge details.
4) The panel renders each challenge with current frequency and status.
5) User adjusts a challenge frequency (for example, from daily to weekly) in the challenge-frequency-control.
6) User submits the change, triggering setActivatedChallengeFrequency with activatedChallengeId and selected frequency.
7) On successful response, the panel updates the challenge row with the new frequency.
8) User can change challenge status (active, paused, completed) via challenge-status-control, which calls updateActivatedChallengeStatus.
9) After success, the UI refreshes the status indicator for that challenge (and any local filters based on status).

Use My Events section and navigate to event details:
1) User opens dashboard.html (optionally with URL parameter section=events and/or eventsView=registered or eventsView=bookmarked).
2) dashboard-initializer calls getDashboardOverview to retrieve upcoming_events (combined view with registration/bookmark flags).
3) my-events-panel calls getMyEventsOverview to retrieve bookmarked_events and registered_events.
4) The events list is filtered client-side according to eventsView in the URL (for example, show only registered events when eventsView=registered).
5) User clicks an event item, triggering navigation to event_detail.html?eventId={event_id}.
6) From the My Events section, user can click the "Find more events" action, which navigates to events.html (no extra parameters).
7) If the user returns to dashboard.html from event_detail.html, the page reloads data via getDashboardOverview and getMyEventsOverview so the My Events view reflects any new bookmarks or registrations.

Review and update action plan from the dashboard:
1) On dashboard load, dashboard-initializer calls getDashboardOverview and getActiveActionPlanOverview.
2) action-plan-summary-panel shows the active plan name, number of actions, total estimated monthly cost, and other summary metrics from getActiveActionPlanOverview.summary or action_plan_summary.
3) User clicks "Edit action plan" within this panel.
4) The click triggers navigation to action_planner.html.
5) After editing the plan and returning to dashboard.html, getActiveActionPlanOverview is called again so the summary reflects the updated plan.

Review carbon footprint and goal:
1) On dashboard load, carbon-goal-summary-panel calls getCarbonCalculatorSummary and also uses carbon_goal_summary from getDashboardOverview if available.
2) The panel displays the latest assessment baseline_annual_emissions_tons_co2e and any active goal with reduction_percentage and target_emissions_tons_co2e.
3) User clicks "Update carbon footprint".
4) The click triggers navigation to carbon_calculator.html.
5) After completing a new calculation or editing goals and returning to dashboard.html, getCarbonCalculatorSummary is called again and the summary is updated.

Monitor donation pledges and explore more projects:
1) On dashboard load, pledges-summary-panel calls getActiveDonationPledgesSummary and may additionally read donation_pledges from getDashboardOverview.
2) The panel lists active DonationPledge records with project_name, category_label, amount, frequency, and status.
3) User clicks a project name, triggering navigation to project_detail.html?projectId={project_id}.
4) User clicks "Support more projects", triggering navigation to projects.html.
5) When the user later returns to dashboard.html, getActiveDonationPledgesSummary is called again to reflect any pledge changes made on the project or projects pages.

Review reading lists from dashboard and manage them:
1) On dashboard load, reading-lists-summary-panel calls getMyReadingListsOverview and may also use reading_lists_summary from getDashboardOverview.
2) The panel displays each ReadingList with its name and item_count.
3) User clicks "Manage reading lists".
4) The click triggers navigation to my_reading_lists.html.
5) After changes to reading lists and returning to dashboard.html, getMyReadingListsOverview is called again to refresh counts and names.

Monitor volunteer memberships and find more groups:
1) On dashboard load, volunteer-memberships-panel calls getVolunteerMembershipsSummary.
2) The panel lists GroupMembership items with group_name, focus_area_label, participation_frequency, and status.
3) User clicks a volunteer group name, triggering navigation to group_detail.html?groupId={group_id}.
4) User clicks "Find more groups" to discover additional volunteer opportunities, navigating to volunteer.html.
5) After joining or changing groups elsewhere and returning to dashboard.html, getVolunteerMembershipsSummary is called again to update the memberships list.

Discover or refresh challenges via the lifestyle quiz:
1) In the My Challenges section, the user sees a control or link such as "Retake lifestyle quiz".
2) The user clicks this link, triggering navigation to lifestyle_quiz.html.
3) The quiz page may recommend new challenges and activate them (creating new ActivatedChallenge records) using its own interfaces.
4) After finishing the quiz and returning to dashboard.html, getMyChallengesOverview (and getDashboardOverview) are called again so the My Challenges list includes any newly activated challenges with their default_frequency and source=quiz_recommendation.
interactions:
onPageLoad for dashboard.html:
- Triggered when the page is first opened or reloaded.
- Calls getDashboardOverview to retrieve consolidated challenges, upcoming_events, action_plan_summary, carbon_goal_summary, donation_pledges, and reading_lists_summary.
- Calls getMyChallengesOverview, getMyEventsOverview, getActiveActionPlanOverview, getCarbonCalculatorSummary, getActiveDonationPledgesSummary, getMyReadingListsOverview, and getVolunteerMembershipsSummary in parallel or sequence as needed.
- Populates all summary panels according to the current URL parameters (for example, section and eventsView).

Challenge frequency change interaction:
- User changes the frequency dropdown for a specific activated challenge inside challenge-frequency-control.
- User clicks a "Save" or equivalent control for that challenge.
- Event handler calls setActivatedChallengeFrequency with activatedChallengeId bound from the ActivatedChallenge.id and frequency bound from the selected input.
- On success, the response.activated_challenge.frequency is used to update that challenge's displayed frequency; any local validation or error messaging uses response.success and response.message.

Challenge status change interaction (pause/complete/reactivate):
- User clicks a status toggle, button, or select input for an activated challenge (for example, setting status to paused or completed).
- Event handler calls updateActivatedChallengeStatus with activatedChallengeId and the new status value.
- On successful response, the status shown in my-challenges-panel is updated from response.activated_challenge.status, and the challenge may be repositioned or filtered client-side based on filters derived from URL parameters (e.g., challengeStatusFilter=active).

Navigate to lifestyle quiz from challenges:
- User clicks the "Retake lifestyle quiz" control inside my-challenges-panel.
- Event handler performs navigation to lifestyle_quiz.html (no additional parameters required by this specification).

Navigate to event details:
- Inside my-events-panel or the overview events list, user clicks an event row/title.
- Event handler reads the bound event_id and navigates to event_detail.html?eventId={event_id}, matching the outgoing navigation specification.
- No interfaces are called on click; data is loaded on the destination page.

Navigate to find more events:
- User clicks "Find more events" within my-events-panel.
- Event handler navigates to events.html with no additional parameters.
- Any event-based URL parameters (such as eventsView) apply only to dashboard.html and are not forwarded unless explicitly designed to be preserved.

Navigate to action planner:
- User clicks "Edit action plan" in action-plan-summary-panel.
- Event handler navigates to action_planner.html with no additional parameters.

Navigate to carbon calculator:
- User clicks "Update carbon footprint" in carbon-goal-summary-panel.
- Event handler navigates to carbon_calculator.html with no additional parameters.

Navigate to project details or projects list:
- In pledges-summary-panel, user clicks a project name bound to a specific project_id.
- Event handler navigates to project_detail.html?projectId={project_id}.
- In the same panel, user clicks "Support more projects", which navigates to projects.html.

Navigate to reading lists management:
- User clicks "Manage reading lists" in reading-lists-summary-panel.
- Event handler navigates to my_reading_lists.html.

Navigate to volunteer groups and group detail:
- In volunteer-memberships-panel, user clicks a group_name bound to a group_id.
- Event handler navigates to group_detail.html?groupId={group_id}.
- User clicks "Find more groups" to navigate to volunteer.html.

Section/tab navigation via URL parameters:
- User clicks internal dashboard navigation controls (for example, to switch between sections such as challenges, events, plans).
- Event handler updates the browser URL (using pushState or a standard link) to dashboard.html?section={section_key}&eventsView={optional_view_key} without a full reload when supported.
- dashboard-section-router and events-view-mode-router components read the updated query parameters and adjust which panels are emphasized or filtered, without altering API calls (since all relevant interfaces are already loaded or can be re-used).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The dashboard page state is controlled via URL query parameters only, without any reliance on localStorage or other client-side persistence. Primary parameters include:
- section: Indicates which main dashboard section the user is focused on, such as section=challenges, section=events, section=plans, section=carbon, section=pledges, section=reading, or section=volunteering. When section is present, dashboard-section-router ensures the corresponding panel is in focus (for example, scrolled into view or logically marked as active) and may collapse or de-prioritize other sections. This parameter does not change which interfaces are called; they are still called as needed on page load, but affects how results are displayed.
- eventsView: Controls filtering in the My Events panel, for example eventsView=registered, eventsView=bookmarked, or eventsView=upcoming. When eventsView is set, my-events-panel filters the results from getMyEventsOverview and/or getDashboardOverview.upcoming_events to only show the relevant subset. If eventsView is absent, a default view may show combined or upcoming items based on the implementation. No additional server calls are made when this parameter changes; only client-side filtering logic is affected.
- challengeStatusFilter (optional): If used, values such as challengeStatusFilter=active, challengeStatusFilter=paused, or challengeStatusFilter=completed can be interpreted by my-challenges-panel to filter the activated_challenges array returned by getMyChallengesOverview. Updates to challenge statuses via updateActivatedChallengeStatus immediately affect which challenges match the current filter.
Navigation to detail pages passes required identifiers as query parameters as specified: event_detail.html?eventId={event_id}, project_detail.html?projectId={project_id}, group_detail.html?groupId={group_id}. These parameters are not interpreted by dashboard.html itself but are generated by its navigation interactions. When the user returns to dashboard.html (with or without section or other parameters), the page always re-runs its interfaces (getDashboardOverview, getMyChallengesOverview, getMyEventsOverview, getActiveActionPlanOverview, getCarbonCalculatorSummary, getActiveDonationPledgesSummary, getMyReadingListsOverview, getVolunteerMembershipsSummary) so the state reflects the current backend data rather than any cached local state.

components.functionality: On page load, fetches consolidated dashboard data and populates all main sections using the assigned interfaces.
Reads the section query parameter from the URL and determines which dashboard section should be considered active or primary, without altering API calls.
Reads the eventsView query parameter and instructs my-events-panel which subset of events (registered, bookmarked, upcoming, or combined) to display.

Displays a detailed list of all ActivatedChallenge records with associated Challenge details, showing name, description, category, difficulty, current frequency, status, and source, and provides access to frequency and status controls.
Provides a selectable frequency input (daily, weekly, monthly, one_time) for each activated challenge and updates it using setActivatedChallengeFrequency.
Allows the user to update the status of an activated challenge (active, paused, completed) and persists it through updateActivatedChallengeStatus.
Provides a control in the My Challenges section that navigates the user to lifestyle_quiz.html to retake the lifestyle quiz.
Reads the challengeStatusFilter query parameter and local filter inputs to determine which activated challenges to display (for example, show only active or paused challenges).
Displays bookmarked events and registered events using getMyEventsOverview, optionally integrating upcoming_events from getDashboardOverview, and supports client-side filtering based on eventsView.
Provides navigation from the My Events section to events.html and to event_detail.html for individual events, updating the URL parameters appropriately.

Displays a summary of the active action plan, including name, description, status, and summary metrics (total_actions, total_estimated_monthly_cost, average_impact_score) with a link to manage the plan in detail.
Triggers navigation to action_planner.html when the user chooses to edit the action plan from the summary panel.
Displays the latest CarbonAssessment and any active CarbonReductionGoal, including baseline and target emissions and reduction percentage, and offers navigation to the carbon calculator.
Navigates to carbon_calculator.html so the user can update their carbon footprint or goals.
Lists active DonationPledge records, with project names, categories, amounts, and frequencies, and provides navigation to project details or the full projects catalog.
Handles navigation from each pledge's project name to project_detail.html with the projectId URL parameter, and from the "Support more projects" control to projects.html.
Displays a compact overview of the user's ReadingList items with their names and item counts, using getMyReadingListsOverview and getDashboardOverview.reading_lists_summary.
Navigates from the reading lists summary panel to my_reading_lists.html for full management of lists and articles.
Shows a summary of the user's GroupMemberships, including group name, focus area, participation frequency, and status, using getVolunteerMembershipsSummary.
Handles navigation from each membership's group name to group_detail.html with groupId, and from a general control to volunteer.html to discover more groups.
Provides internal navigation between dashboard sections by updating the section and eventsView URL parameters without reloading data.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, max-width layout based on a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters (desktop breakpoint). Content appears within a central column spanning roughly 8–10 grid columns, with generous side margins.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered main content column on a 12‑column grid, spanning roughly 8–10 columns in the middle with wide left/right gutters. At the very top (outside this spec) sits the global site header. Within the dashboard, start with a hero‑like primary section (My Challenges) that plays the role of the editorial intro block from the mockup. Immediately below the hero block, stack the other dashboard sections (events, action plan, carbon goals, pledges, reading lists, volunteer memberships) in a linear top‑to‑bottom flow. Place dashboard‑level navigation controls just under the page title, aligned with the main content column, echoing the subtle top navigation from the mockup. All routing/data-loader components remain invisible, influencing what is shown but not affecting visual layout directly.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered main content column on a 12‑column grid, spanning roughly 8–10 columns in the middle with wide left/right gutters. At the very top (outside this spec) sits the global site header. Within the dashboard, start with a hero‑like primary section (My Challenges) that plays the role of the editorial intro block from the mockup. Immediately below the hero block, stack the other dashboard sections (events, action plan, carbon goals, pledges, reading lists, volunteer memberships) in a linear top‑to‑bottom flow. Place dashboard‑level navigation controls just under the page title, aligned with the main content column, echoing the subtle top navigation from the mockup. All routing/data-loader components remain invisible, influencing what is shown but not affecting visual layout directly.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate the top 50–60% of the first viewport to the My Challenges hero panel, giving it generous padding and vertical whitespace, mimicking the 'Biodiversity' title + intro block from the mockup. The remaining 40–50% of the initial viewport is occupied by the beginning of the next prominent visual section (My Events), analogous to the image area in the mockup. Each subsequent dashboard section is a full‑width block within the central column, separated by 48–64px vertical gaps. Side gutters consume ~10–12% of screen width on each side, keeping the content visually framed and calm. Internal padding within panels is in the 16–24px range (e.g., 24px top/bottom, 32px left/right) to maintain generous breathing room.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low‑density, editorial feel: each panel focuses on a single theme (challenges, events, action plan, etc.), avoiding overly compact widget grids. Content within each section is arranged in short lines with comfortable line height and clear separation between heading, summary text, and controls. Use whitespace generously between controls (16–24px) and between sections (48–64px) so the page feels calm and easily scannable rather than dashboard‑busy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Follow a vertical F‑pattern similar to the mockup. The eye moves from the site logo and global nav (top left to top right) down to the 'My Dashboard' context and the internal dashboard navigation row, then drops to the prominent My Challenges heading at the top-left of the hero panel. From there, the eye scans down through the challenge controls, then continues vertically through each subsequent section (events, action plan, carbon, pledges, reading, volunteer). Within each section, headings anchor the left edge, while right‑aligned navigation link groups and CTAs provide secondary focal points, echoing the subtle left‑to‑right sweeps of the original design. Any floating or sticky navigation remains visually light so as not to compete with the main content flow.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a calm, editorial-style dashboard framed within a wide, pale background. A global site header sits at the very top, beneath which the main dashboard content is centered in a max‑width column with generous side gutters. Just inside this column, a light internal navigation row allows quick jumps between dashboard sections but remains visually understated. Below this, a large, softly-tinted 'My Challenges' panel dominates the upper half of the first viewport, with a strong section heading on the left and a short explanatory text or metrics block beneath it, echoing the hero typography and breathing room from the mockup. Within this hero panel, challenge controls (frequency, status, filters, quiz link) are arranged in a loose horizontal band under the text, with ample whitespace, so they feel like calm tools rather than dense UI. As you scroll, the layout continues in a vertical rhythm: the My Events section comes next as a wide, airy panel with its own heading on the left and sub‑navigation on the right, followed by a sequence of similarly proportioned summary panels for the action plan, carbon goals, pledges, reading lists, and volunteer memberships. Each section reads like its own editorial module—clear heading, brief summary, and a single, well‑placed navigation control—stacked with consistent spacing in a single column. The overall impression is a serene, nature‑inspired dashboard where content is easy to scan and interactions are clearly grouped but never cramped.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dashboard initializer sits logically at the very top of the main content area, directly under the global header and above any visible dashboard component. It occupies the full width of the main content column but has no visible UI; effectively, it is a zero-height layer that runs before anything else renders in the central column. In the visual stack, it is invisible and does not disrupt the continuous, spacious vertical flow of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-height structural component anchored at the top of the main content column; participates in layout only logically, not visually.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dashboard section router is placed immediately after the initializer in the document flow, still above all visible dashboard content. It spans the same central column but remains invisible. Conceptually, it governs which section is treated as the current focus (for example, which section the internal navigation highlights or scrolls to) without occupying any physical space or altering the rhythm of the stacked panels.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller embedded near the top of the main content column; zero-height, no impact on spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events view mode router is logically embedded within the My Events section, aligned near the top of that panel. It spans only the width necessary to register different view states (e.g., list or calendar) but is not itself visually rendered; instead, it influences which child layout appears. From a layout perspective, it sits in the same horizontal band as the events heading and navigation links, without creating additional visible height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-height control co-located with the header row of the My Events panel; has no visual footprint, only behavioral impact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The My Challenges panel is the first and most prominent visible block in the dashboard, occupying the top of the main content column just below the internal navigation. It stretches across roughly 80–85% of the page width (centered), leaving broad, equal gutters on the left and right. The panel itself sits on a very pale green or softly-tinted background, echoing the hero content zone from the mockup. It uses generous internal padding—around 24px top and bottom and 32px left and right—so that its heading, descriptive copy, and controls are all well separated. Vertically, this panel occupies roughly the upper half of the first viewport on desktop: the upper part devoted to the section title and summary text, and the lower part to challenge controls. There is a clear margin of 48–64px above it (from the page title/internal nav) and another 48–64px below it before the next section begins, reinforcing its role as a hero-like editorial module.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element in the main flow; background-tinted hero section that scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the My Challenges panel, the challenge frequency control is positioned in the lower half of the panel content. It appears on a horizontal row of controls roughly one-third of the way up from the bottom of the panel, aligned to the left edge of the content (matching the left edge of the section heading). There is a comfortable vertical gap (16–24px) between the descriptive text above and this control row, and a similar gap between this control and any content below. The control’s width is modest—perhaps 25–30% of the content width—so that it feels like a discreet, well-spaced dropdown or selector rather than a full-width bar, leaving breathing room to its right for the status control and other elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within a horizontal control row; on smaller screens, it stacks above the other challenge controls but remains left-aligned with consistent vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The challenge status control sits on the same horizontal line as the challenge frequency control, to its immediate right, separated by a generous horizontal gap of about 24px (three base units). It aligns vertically so their labels and input areas form a clean, quiet row roughly centered in the lower half of the My Challenges panel. Together with the frequency control it occupies at most the left two-thirds of the content width, leaving negative space on the right that contributes to the panel's airy feel. On narrow viewports, this control drops below the frequency control, with a 16–24px vertical gap, maintaining the relaxed editorial spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline control in a responsive horizontal row; wraps to a stacked arrangement under the frequency control on smaller screens while preserving generous spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The challenges quiz link is positioned toward the lower-right area inside the My Challenges panel, visually separated from the frequency and status controls. It appears either on the same control row aligned to the far right, or just below it with a 12–16px gap, functioning as a textual call-to-action. It occupies only as much width as its label needs and is surrounded by ample whitespace so it feels like a subtle, optional action rather than a primary button. A small vertical margin of 16–24px below the link separates it clearly from the bottom edge of the panel, preserving the lightness of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline or right-aligned text/button element within the My Challenges panel’s control area; scrolls normally and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The challenges filter controls occupy a full-width row within the My Challenges panel, positioned between the descriptive text and the more specific challenge frequency/status controls. They are left-aligned with the section heading and stretch across most of the content width, yet each individual filter chip or input is separated with comfortable horizontal gaps (8–16px). Vertically, a gap of about 16–24px separates the paragraph above from the first filter, and another similar gap separates the filters from the lower control row, creating a clear three-band structure: heading and text, filters, then detailed controls. This reinforces the editorial feel while still offering functional refinement.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a full-width row within the panel; filters may wrap to multiple lines as needed, maintaining even vertical spacing and avoiding crowding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The My Events panel appears immediately below the My Challenges panel, separated by a vertical gap of approximately 48–64px, continuing the stacked, central-column rhythm. It spans the same width as My Challenges (around 80–85% of the page width, centered) but may use a plain very light background (near white) to subtly differentiate it from the hero-tinted section above. Internal padding follows the same pattern: 24px top/bottom and 32px left/right. At the top of this panel, a section heading is anchored on the left, while navigation links and view mode controls cluster on the right, forming a balanced header band. Below this header, the main events content (list or calendar) fills the remaining height, giving the panel a visual weight that roughly occupies the upper portion of the second viewport, analogous to the image area under the text in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard stacked panel within the main flow; scrolls normally and expands vertically as events content grows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the My Events panel, the events navigation links are aligned to the top-right corner in the header row, directly opposite the events heading. They form a compact horizontal group of text links or pills, each separated by 8–16px, and collectively occupy roughly one-third of the panel’s width. There is a comfortable 8–12px inner margin from the panel’s right edge so they do not feel cramped. Vertically, they sit within the top 48–64px of the panel, sharing that band with the heading, and are separated from the event content below by a 16–24px gap. This arrangement mirrors the subtle, right-aligned navigation in the original mockup’s header area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right-aligned horizontal group anchored within the top header row of the My Events panel; remains in place as content beneath it scrolls within the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Action Plan summary panel is stacked directly beneath the My Events panel, with a vertical separation of 48–64px that visually segments the sections. It maintains the same centered width and generous left/right padding as the previous panels, creating a consistent editorial column. At the top-left of the panel is the Action Plan heading and a brief summary or key metric, with ample whitespace above (24–32px) and below (16–24px). The body content—perhaps a short list of upcoming actions or progress indicators—occupies the middle of the panel. The bottom-right region houses the navigation link to the full action plan, giving the panel a clear left-to-right narrative: title and text on the left, call-to-action on the right, all surrounded by plenty of negative space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard stacked summary block; scrolls with the page and scales vertically with its content while preserving top and bottom padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the Action Plan summary panel, the action plan navigation link appears as a discrete call-to-action anchored near the bottom-right corner of the content area. It is aligned horizontally with the last line of summary content on the left or slightly below it, separated by a horizontal stretch of whitespace so the link stands apart as a clear next step. The link occupies minimal width (just its label) and maintains a 16–24px margin from the panel’s right and bottom edges. This placement mirrors the pattern of subtle, right-side focal points in the overall F-pattern flow, while preserving the panel’s airy composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right-aligned inline CTA within the Action Plan panel; scrolls normally and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Carbon Goal summary panel follows beneath the Action Plan panel with a consistent vertical gap of 48–64px, keeping the sections clearly separated yet visually related. It spans the same central width and padding, but can use a very pale green or slightly tinted background to subtly tie it back to the environmental tone of the hero while still feeling distinct. The heading and any key carbon metrics are left-aligned at the top, with 24–32px of top padding and a comfortable 16–24px gap before any supporting text or simple visualization. The lower-right corner of the panel is reserved for a navigation link to the carbon calculator, providing a clear action without crowding the content. Overall, the panel occupies a moderate vertical space—roughly one-third of a viewport—maintaining the low-density, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard stacked section; expands vertically as needed for summary content, maintaining consistent inner padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the Carbon Goal summary panel, the carbon calculator navigation link is positioned in the lower-right quadrant of the panel. It is slightly separated from the main text or metrics by a horizontal gap of at least 24px and a vertical gap of 16–24px, so it reads as a distinct but secondary action. The link width is minimal, aligned to the right edge with a small 16–24px inset from the panel border. This mirrors the placement pattern of other section-level action links, reinforcing a predictable visual rhythm along the right edge of the content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right-aligned CTA within the Carbon Goal panel; scrolls with the panel and does not maintain any fixed position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Pledges summary panel appears next in the vertical stack, separated by another 48–64px from the Carbon Goal panel above. It continues the same central alignment and width, with 24px top and bottom padding and 32px side padding, sitting on a neutral very light background. The section title 'Pledges' (or equivalent) anchors the top-left of the panel, with a concise summary of total pledges or recent activity directly beneath it. The main body of the panel might show one or two key pledge items or a short listing, but these are spaced with ample line height and gaps so the content never feels dense. On the right side of the panel’s header or lower body, a navigation link group to related projects offers deeper navigation while maintaining the quiet, editorial layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard stacked block-level section; scrolls normally and grows vertically with its summary content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the Pledges summary panel, the projects navigation links form a small, right-aligned group either in the upper header band (opposite the Pledges heading) or slightly below it aligned to the right. The links are arranged horizontally with 8–16px between them, and collectively occupy no more than one-third of the panel’s width. A consistent 16–24px inset from the right edge keeps them visually balanced. Vertically, there is at least a 16–24px gap separating these links from any list of pledge items below, so the UI retains a clear hierarchy and open feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right-aligned horizontal link group; remains within the Pledges panel header area and scrolls as part of that panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Reading Lists summary panel is stacked beneath the Pledges panel, again with a 48–64px vertical separation. It uses the same central width and padding scheme, reinforcing the consistent editorial column. A heading such as 'Reading Lists' anchors at the top-left, with a short descriptive line or count of saved articles placed directly below, separated by 8–16px. Below this, one or two highlighted reading items may appear, each with generous vertical spacing to keep the panel light. The navigation link to the full reading lists sits unobtrusively on the right side, typically toward the lower half of the panel. Overall, the panel occupies a moderate vertical space, ensuring this section feels like a calm, curated library rather than a dense list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard stacked summary panel; scrolls in sequence with the other sections and expands as needed for a small set of items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the Reading Lists summary panel, the reading lists navigation link is placed on the right side of the panel, aligned horizontally with either the summary text or the last featured item. It is set in from the right edge by about 16–24px and from the bottom by a similar margin. The link is intentionally minimal—just text or a subtle button—so it does not overpower the reading list summaries. A clear 16–24px gap separates this link from nearby text, ensuring it reads as a clean, singular action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right-aligned inline CTA in the Reading Lists panel; static within its panel and scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Volunteer Memberships panel is placed near the bottom of the dashboard’s main vertical flow, directly beneath the Reading Lists panel with a 48–64px gap. It continues the same central, max-width column with equal side gutters and generous internal padding. A heading like 'Volunteer Memberships' sits on the top-left, followed by a concise overview of current memberships or opportunities beneath it. Any membership cards or items are displayed with large vertical spacing—similar to short editorial snippets—avoiding a cluttered card grid. The top or lower-right edge of the panel hosts a small group of volunteer-related navigation links, letting users dive deeper without overwhelming the summary. The panel’s height is moderate, rounding out the page with a tranquil, well-structured final section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard stacked section at the bottom of the main column; scrolls normally and can grow taller as membership information increases.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the Volunteer Memberships panel, the volunteer navigation links are grouped together on the right side, either aligned with the heading at the top or positioned in the upper-middle portion of the panel, depending on the amount of summary text. The links are arranged horizontally or in a short vertical stack with 8–12px spacing between them, occupying no more than one-third of the panel’s width. They are inset from the right border by about 16–24px, and separated from any listing of memberships by a clear 16–24px vertical gap, keeping the layout airy and easy to scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right-aligned navigation link group within the Volunteer panel; stays within the panel’s flow and does not float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dashboard internal navigation sits just below the global page title for 'My Dashboard' and directly above the My Challenges hero panel, spanning the full width of the central content column. Visually, it appears as a thin horizontal row of section tabs or pill-shaped links (e.g., Challenges, Events, Action Plan, Carbon, etc.), centered or left-aligned within the column. Each item has generous horizontal padding and 8–16px spacing between them, with 16–24px of whitespace above and below the row so it feels integrated yet light. This navigation row occupies only a small vertical band near the top of the dashboard, but plays an important role in orienting the user, echoing the subtle top navigation of the mockup while remaining subordinate to the primary hero content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Horizontally scrolling or wrapping navigation row anchored in the normal flow beneath the page title; on desktop it may become sticky to the top of the viewport after scrolling past the hero, but it retains a slim, unobtrusive presence.

name: About
title: About
description: The About page loads and displays the organization’s mission, vision, focus areas, and approaches to education, tools, and community engagement using dynamic content, and provides contextual navigation to related pages.

core_features: Retrieve About page content (mission, vision, focus areas, and approaches) using the getAboutPageContent interface when the page loads.
Display mission and vision text in dedicated sections that can be deep-linked via URL parameters.
List all conservation focus areas returned by getAboutPageContent as an interactive collection (e.g., expandable items) that can be deep-linked or pre-expanded via URL parameters.
Present the approaches to education, tools, and community engagement as structured content sections that can be navigated directly via URL parameters.

Provide context-aware navigation controls to contact.html and projects.html, passing URL parameters indicating that navigation originated from the About page and, where applicable, from a specific section (mission, vision, focus area, or approach).
Synchronize scroll/section highlighting state with URL parameters so that direct links to about.html can open the page at a specific section without using localStorage.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View mission and vision
1. User opens about.html.
2. about-data-loader calls getAboutPageContent and stores the mission and vision.
3. mission-vision-section renders the mission and vision text once data is available.
4. If the URL contains section=mission or section=vision, about-section-router scrolls to and highlights the corresponding subsection.
5. The user may click a contact link in this section; about-navigation-ctas generates a URL to contact.html including from=about and about_section=mission or about_section=vision.

Workflow: Explore a specific focus area from a deep link
1. User follows a link to about.html with URL parameters such as section=focus_areas and focus_key=<some-key>.
2. about-data-loader fetches content via getAboutPageContent.
3. Once data is loaded, about-section-router reads section and focus_key from the URL.
4. focus-areas-accordion receives the selected focus_key and pre-expands the matching focus area whose key equals focus_key.
5. The page scrolls to the focus-areas-accordion section and visually indicates the selected focus area.

Workflow: Navigate from About to support projects
1. User opens about.html and scrolls to the mission, vision, or focus area that mentions projects.
2. User clicks a projects-related call-to-action rendered by about-navigation-ctas.
3. The click handler constructs a URL to projects.html including from=about and about_section set to the currently active section (for example, about_section=focus_areas and optionally focus_key=<current-focus-key> if a specific area was active).
4. Browser navigates to projects.html with those URL parameters, letting the projects page optionally tailor its presentation based on the source context.

Workflow: Contact the organization after reading About content
1. User opens about.html and reads the mission, vision, or approaches sections.
2. User clicks a contact-related link or button within mission-vision-section or approach-sections.
3. The click handler generates a URL to contact.html with parameters from=about and about_section describing the section where the user clicked (e.g., about_section=approach_education, about_section=mission).
4. Browser navigates to contact.html; the contact page can read these parameters (if implemented there) to pre-fill context such as the reason for contact.
interactions: On page load, about-data-loader automatically calls getAboutPageContent with no parameters and updates the page state when the promise resolves.

On URL change (e.g., a user pastes a different URL with new parameters or uses browser navigation), about-section-router re-reads URLSearchParams and updates which section should be active, which focus area should be expanded, and where the page should scroll.

When the user clicks an internal anchor or navigation control to jump to another section of the About page, about-section-router updates the section parameter in the URL without a full page reload (for example, changing section from mission to focus_areas).

When the user toggles a focus area item in focus-areas-accordion, the component updates its internal expanded state and also sets or clears the focus_key URL parameter to match the currently selected focus area key.

When the user clicks a contact-related control in any section, about-navigation-ctas generates and navigates to contact.html with query parameters conveying the origin (from=about and about_section=<current-section-id>).

When the user clicks a projects-related call-to-action, about-navigation-ctas generates and navigates to projects.html with query parameters that include from=about and any relevant context such as about_section and focus_key (if a specific focus area is active).
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page uses URL query parameters as the sole mechanism for externalizing and restoring state. The primary parameters are: section, which indicates the main subsection to activate (for example, mission, vision, focus_areas, approach_education, approach_tools, approach_community); focus_key, which aligns with one of the focus_areas[i].key values returned by getAboutPageContent and determines which focus area item should be pre-expanded and highlighted; and from and about_section, which are added to outbound navigation URLs to contact.html and projects.html to identify that the user navigated from about.html and from which logical section. On initial load and on any URL change, about-section-router reads URLSearchParams and informs relevant components of the active section and focus_key. Components update the UI (scroll position, highlighting, expanded items) accordingly. When users interact with section anchors or focus area toggles, components update the URL parameters in-place (without reloading the page) to keep the URL in sync with the current in-page state. No state is stored in localStorage; all shareable/restoreable state is encoded in the URL.

components.functionality: Fetches About page data by calling getAboutPageContent on initialization, handles loading and error states, and exposes mission, vision, focus_areas, approach_to_education, approach_to_tools, and approach_to_community_engagement to child components via a shared state or context mechanism.

Manages synchronization between URL query parameters and the active section/focus area on the About page. On load and on URL changes, it reads section and focus_key from URLSearchParams and informs mission-vision-section, focus-areas-accordion, and approach-sections which section to activate and which focus area key to pre-expand. It also updates URLSearchParams when the user navigates between sections or toggles focus areas.

Displays the mission and vision text loaded from about-data-loader. Supports deep-linking and section highlighting based on the active section reported by about-section-router. Provides a contact-related interaction that passes context to about-navigation-ctas when the user wants to reach out after reading mission or vision.

Renders the list of focus areas provided by getAboutPageContent.focus_areas as an interactive list where each item can be expanded to show its title and description. The component pre-expands the item whose key matches the focus_key URL parameter. When a user expands or switches focus areas, it updates the focus_key parameter via about-section-router so that the current focus area selection is represented in the URL.

Displays the organization’s approaches to education, tools, and community engagement using the corresponding fields from getAboutPageContent. It can present them as separate subsections that can be activated individually. The active subsection is controlled by the section URL parameter (values such as approach_education, approach_tools, approach_community). When users navigate between these subsections via in-page controls, the component requests an update of the section parameter via about-section-router.

Provides navigation controls to contact.html and projects.html. When a user clicks any contact-related or project-support-related control originating from other components (for example, mission-vision-section, focus-areas-accordion, or approach-sections), this component constructs the target URL with context parameters such as from=about, about_section=<current-section>, and, when relevant, focus_key=<current-focus-key>. It then triggers navigation to the target page.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, max-width layout based on a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters (desktop breakpoint). Content appears within a central column spanning roughly 8–10 grid columns, with generous side margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow with a single dominant central column. Below the global header/navigation, the About page opens with a calm, hero-like Mission & Vision block (serving the same role as the 'Biodiversity' section in the mockup) followed directly by a more visual/structured block explaining the organization’s approaches. Below that, focus areas are revealed in an accordion format, and the page concludes with low-key but clear navigation CTAs to related pages. All visible components sit within a centered content column on a 12-column grid, typically spanning about 8–10 columns, with wide side gutters that keep the layout airy and focused.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom editorial flow with a single dominant central column. Below the global header/navigation, the About page opens with a calm, hero-like Mission & Vision block (serving the same role as the 'Biodiversity' section in the mockup) followed directly by a more visual/structured block explaining the organization’s approaches. Below that, focus areas are revealed in an accordion format, and the page concludes with low-key but clear navigation CTAs to related pages. All visible components sit within a centered content column on a 12-column grid, typically spanning about 8–10 columns, with wide side gutters that keep the layout airy and focused.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Mission & Vision occupies the upper ~50–60% of the initial viewport, with generous top and bottom padding inside a pale green panel that echoes the mockup’s main content area. The Approaches block occupies the next ~40–50% of the visible area on first scroll, acting as the ‘visual’ supporting section. The Focus Areas accordion and navigation CTAs sit below as full-width sections inside the same central column, each separated by 48–64px vertical spacing. Internally, each section uses 24–32px padding on all sides, and content is constrained to a max-width central column (~70–80% of page width) with 10–12% gutters on each side.
layout_strategies.content_density: The content is intentionally low-density and editorial. Text blocks are kept to comfortable line lengths within the central column, with generous line height and spacing between headings and paragraphs (24–32px). Each section breathes: there is clear separation between Mission & Vision, Approaches, Focus Areas, and Navigation CTAs. Interactive elements like the accordion are not crammed; they have enough vertical spacing between items to feel calm and uncluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F-pattern: users start at the top-left with the logo in the site header, scan to the right over the navigation, then drop into the left-aligned Mission & Vision heading. Their gaze moves down to the mission/vision paragraph(s), then continues downward into the more structured Approaches content. From there, the eye moves down again into the Focus Areas accordion, and finally to the bottom Navigation CTAs. Supporting system components (data loader and URL state manager) are non-visual and do not interrupt this flow. Any accent or CTA elements are understated and anchored within this vertical progression, not competing with the main text blocks.

layout.overall_description: Below the global header, the About page opens into a broad, pale green panel that fills most of the initial viewport height. Centered within this, a single, left-aligned Mission & Vision heading and its descriptive text sit in a comfortable central column that spans roughly 70–80% of the page width, leaving wide, calming gutters on both sides. There is ample air above and below the text so the section feels like an editorial hero rather than a marketing banner. As you scroll slightly, this textual introduction gives way to a more structured Approaches section that still lives in the same central column but may break content into subtle sub-blocks, echoing the mockup’s shift from large text to a more visually organized area. Continuing downward, the page maintains the same column alignment and spacious rhythm as the Focus Areas accordion appears, offering collapsible details without adding clutter. Finally, the layout tapers into a quiet band of navigation CTAs, horizontally aligned but compact, giving pathways to related pages while preserving the minimalist, environmental tone. All elements align vertically in one strong, central axis, with consistent padding, generous vertical spacing between sections, and a clear top-to-bottom narrative flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is a non-visual, structural component that anchors at the very top of the main content area, directly beneath the global header/navigation and above the Mission & Vision section. It occupies no explicit visual space but conceptually spans the same central content width as the other sections (roughly 70–80% of the page width, centered) so that any loading states are aligned with the main column. If loading indicators or skeleton states are used, they appear as faint, full-width (within the central column) placeholders mimicking the shape of the Mission & Vision text blocks, with 24–32px padding on all sides and 48–64px spacing above and below, ensuring the page still feels spacious even while data is loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logically positioned at the top of the main content flow, just below the site header, but typically non-rendering or invisible. It does not affect layout once data is loaded; any temporary skeletons or spinners occupy the same vertical space and central column where the main sections will appear, then collapse seamlessly when data is ready.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is also a non-visual component, positioned conceptually just above the first visible content section (Mission & Vision), aligned with the top edge of the main content column. It serves as an anchor for in-page navigation and deep linking. When a specific subsection is addressed in the URL (e.g., focus areas or approaches), the viewport scroll positions such that the corresponding visible section (e.g., the Approaches group or Focus Areas accordion) aligns near the top of the central column, just below the global header. This maintains the clean vertical flow: the user always lands with the targeted section’s heading sitting comfortably in the upper third of the viewport, surrounded by 48–64px of breathing room above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-blocking; it does not create visible UI. It controls scroll behavior and section focus, effectively using the top of the central content column as the reference point when routing to specific parts of the About page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
The Mission & Vision section acts as the primary hero-like block of the About page. It sits immediately below the global header (with a 48–64px top margin to create separation) and spans the full browser width as a pale green background panel. Inside this panel, content is constrained to a centered column aligned to the grid: roughly 70–80% of the page width, with generous left and right gutters (about 10–12% each side). Within this central column, the main heading (e.g., 'Mission & Vision' or 'About Our Work') is left-aligned and positioned high in the panel, occupying the top third of the visible area. The type is large and prominent but still understated, echoing an editorial title rather than a marketing headline.

Directly below the heading, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space, a multi-line paragraph or short set of paragraphs explains the mission and vision. These paragraphs are also left-aligned, with a comfortable line length and increased line height, allowing the text to wrap into multiple short lines similar to the mockup. The vertical padding inside the green panel is generous—roughly 64px above the heading and 64–80px below the last paragraph—so that the whole section occupies approximately 50–60% of the initial viewport height on desktop. The overall effect is a calm, spacious introduction that anchors the page.

If the design includes an associated visual (e.g., a subtle background illustration or faint texture related to nature), it should sit low in the panel or bleed behind the text with very light contrast, ensuring the text block remains the primary focal point in this section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the normal document flow. The pale green background panel extends full-width, while its content remains centered; the section does not stick or float when scrolling but serves as the first major anchor of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
The Approaches section group begins immediately below the Mission & Vision panel, separated by a clear 48–64px vertical gap so it reads as the next major chapter. The background returns to either pure white or a very light neutral, creating a subtle visual break from the green hero panel while maintaining the overall calm aesthetic. Like other sections, Approaches content is housed in the same central column: about 70–80% of the page width, centered on the page with wide outer gutters.

At the top of this group, a left-aligned section heading such as 'Our Approach' or 'How We Work' appears, with 24–32px of space below it. Beneath this heading, the section may be divided into 2–4 stacked sub-sections (e.g., 'Education', 'Tools', 'Community Engagement'), each presented as a full-width block within the central column. Each sub-section uses 24–32px internal padding and 32–48px vertical spacing from the next, ensuring each approach reads as its own editorial slice rather than as a crowded list. On larger screens, these sub-sections can optionally use a very light two-column structure (e.g., label on the left, descriptive paragraph on the right) while still fitting within the central column; even then, no hard sidebars are introduced, keeping the layout consistent with the single-column feel.

Visually, this group occupies roughly 40–50% of the viewport once reached, creating the sense that it is the second major band of content following the introductory text. There is no heavy graphic distraction; instead, the emphasis is on structured, readable explanations with comfortable whitespace between each approach.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal vertical flow. All sub-sections stack vertically and respond fluidly with the central column; no sticky or floating sub-elements are used to preserve the calm, editorial character.

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
The Focus Areas accordion sits directly below the Approaches group, separated by approximately 48–64px of vertical space, maintaining the rhythm of distinct but related sections. It remains within the same central column width (about 70–80% of the page width, centered), with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides of the accordion container.

At the top of the accordion, a left-aligned heading like 'Focus Areas' introduces the section, with a 16–24px gap before the first accordion item. Each accordion item spans the full width of the central column, forming a vertical stack of clickable headings. Items are separated by 8–16px, keeping them clearly distinct but still part of a coherent group. When an item is expanded, its content appears directly beneath its heading, indented slightly (or simply offset by additional padding) but still aligned to the same left text edge as the rest of the page, reinforcing the single editorial column feel.

The accordion’s vertical footprint is allowed to grow naturally as items are opened, and there is ample 32–48px padding at the bottom of the accordion container before the next section begins. The overall density remains low: labels are not cramped, and descriptions inside expanded items use large line spacing and limited line length, matching the visual calm of the Mission & Vision text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, expanding and collapsing vertically within the main flow as users interact. It does not overlay or float; instead, it pushes content below it down when items expand, preserving the smooth vertical reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative:
The navigation CTAs form the closing section of the About page, positioned beneath the Focus Areas accordion with a 48–64px gap so they read as a gentle conclusion and next-step prompt rather than a competing main section. This block is contained within the same centered column (roughly 70–80% of page width), with 24–32px internal padding, and may optionally sit on a very subtle tinted or off-white background band that stretches full-width to distinguish it softly from the body content above.

At the top of this CTA band, a small, left-aligned label or heading such as 'Explore More' or 'Related Pages' appears with 16–24px spacing below. Beneath this label, 2–4 navigation CTAs (e.g., 'Our Projects', 'Get Involved', 'Resources') are laid out horizontally on desktop where space allows: each CTA appears as a minimalist text link or understated button, aligned in a row within the central column, with 24–32px of horizontal spacing between them. On narrower screens, these CTAs stack vertically while maintaining the same left alignment and generous vertical spacing.

The CTAs are visually lightweight—no overly large buttons or heavy color blocks—so they sit clearly as tertiary navigation, not a hero. There is 48–64px of padding below the CTAs before the page footer begins, allowing the page to visually taper off in a calm, intentional way.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and anchored at the bottom of the About page content, just above the global footer. It does not stick or float; users encounter it naturally after scrolling through the main narrative content.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Provides a configurable contact form and alternative contact details so users can submit questions, feedback, or issues and access FAQs.

core_features: Load contact configuration (support email, mailing address, contact categories, FAQ link label) via getContactPageInfo and bind it to page components.
Provide a contact form that lets users enter name, email, subject, category, and message, and submit these via submitContactForm.
Display submission feedback (success or error) using the response from submitContactForm, including any returned ticket_id and message.
Offer alternative contact methods (such as email and mailing address) using data from getContactPageInfo.
Link to the FAQ page (faq.html) using the FAQ label from getContactPageInfo, optionally passing the selected contact category as a URL parameter.
user_workflows: Access contact page from site navigation: 1) User clicks the footer "Contact" link or an external direct link. 2) Page initializes and invokes getContactPageInfo with no parameters. 3) The page populates the category selector, FAQ link label, and alternative contact details from the returned data. 4) Any supported URL parameters (such as category or subject) are read to prefill the form state.

Submit a contact request: 1) User views the contact form after configuration is loaded. 2) User optionally selects a category from the dynamically loaded contact_categories list. 3) User enters name, email, subject, and message. 4) User clicks the form submit button, triggering submitContactForm with {name, email, subject, category, message}. 5) The page disables the submit button or shows a loading indicator while awaiting the response. 6) On success, the page shows a confirmation using the returned success flag, ticket_id, and message, and can update the URL to include status and ticket_id parameters. 7) On failure, the page shows an error message using the returned success flag and message, and can update the URL to include an error status parameter.

Navigate to FAQs instead of submitting the form: 1) User notices the "View FAQs" link displayed near the form. 2) User clicks the link. 3) The page navigates to faq.html, optionally appending a category query parameter that matches the currently selected contact category key in the form. 4) The FAQ page can use this parameter to focus on relevant content (handled by faq.html).

Deep-linked, pre-populated contact request: 1) Another page or an external system links to contact.html with URL parameters (for example, category, subject, or email) that reflect the user’s context. 2) When contact.html loads, it calls getContactPageInfo and then aligns any URL category parameter with one of the returned contact_categories keys; if matched, that category is preselected. 3) Form fields such as subject or email are pre-populated from the URL query parameters. 4) User reviews or adjusts the pre-filled information and submits the form, which calls submitContactForm as usual.

Reviewing form status after page reload or share: 1) After a successful submission, the page can push or replace a URL that includes status=success and ticket_id as query parameters. 2) If the user reloads the page or opens the same URL in a new tab, the page initialization reads the URL parameters. 3) A status component interprets status and ticket_id and reconstructs a success message, optionally combined with text from the last known submitContactForm response if still in memory. 4) The user can dismiss the status message and continue to use the contact form.
interactions: Click footer "Contact" link: Navigates the user to contact.html with no additional parameters or with optional context parameters added by the referring page.
Page load: Triggers a data-loading component that calls getContactPageInfo with no parameters and stores the result in page state for use by the contact form, alternative contact panel, and FAQ link.

Form field input: When the user types into name, email, subject, or message fields, onChangeField handlers update the internal form state; they also ensure that any URL parameters used only for initial prefill do not overwrite subsequent user edits.

Category selection: When the user selects a category from the dropdown, an onChangeCategory handler updates the form’s category state using the selected contact_categories item’s key; this value is then used when calling submitContactForm and can also be used when building the FAQ navigation URL.

Form submission: When the user clicks the submit button, an onSubmit handler validates that required fields are filled, then invokes submitContactForm({name, email, subject, category, message}). The handler processes the returned object (success, ticket_id, message) to update the status display and optionally synchronize the URL query parameters with the new status.

Submission success feedback: After submitContactForm returns success=true, the page displays a confirmation status message and may push or replace URL parameters (e.g., status=success and ticket_id set to the returned ticket_id). A dismiss action can hide this message without reloading.

Submission error feedback: If submitContactForm returns success=false, the page displays an error status message using the returned message value. A retry button or simply re-editing fields allows the user to attempt submission again without losing their input.

Click "View FAQs" link: Invokes an onClickFaqLink handler that builds a navigation URL to faq.html. If the user has a category selected and it matches a key from contact_categories, the handler appends this key as a query parameter (e.g., ?category=<selectedKey>) before navigating.

URL parameter initialization: On first render, a URL-state component reads query parameters such as category, subject, email, status, and ticket_id. It initializes the form state (for category, subject, email) and status state (for status and ticket_id) but does not continually override user edits.

Status dismissal: If a status banner (success or error) is visible, a close or dismiss control triggers an onDismissStatus handler that hides the banner and may optionally remove status-related query parameters from the URL using history APIs.
page_functionality.state_logic: The contact page relies on URL query parameters and API responses for its state. On load, the page reads query parameters from contact.html, such as: (1) category: if present, the page waits for getContactPageInfo to return contact_categories, then attempts to match the category parameter value to a contact_categories item.key. If a match is found, that category becomes the initial selected category in the form. If no match is found, the form leaves category unselected. (2) subject: if present, this value pre-populates the subject form field on initial load only. After the user changes the subject, the URL parameter is not reapplied. (3) email: if present, this value pre-populates the email field on initial load only and is then treated as user-controlled state. (4) status and ticket_id: if status is present (for example, 'success' or 'error') the status component uses it to determine whether to show a success or error state on page load or reload. If ticket_id is present and status indicates success, the status component includes the ticket_id in the confirmation display. These status parameters can be set or updated after submitContactForm completes successfully or fails. The page never relies on localStorage; instead, state is reconstructed from URL parameters plus fresh results from getContactPageInfo and the current in-memory form state. All data needed for form labels, contact categories, support email, mailing address, and FAQ link labels is retrieved exclusively via getContactPageInfo, so no static configuration values are encoded in the page. submitContactForm is used only when the user actively submits the form, and the returned object directly controls transient UI states (loading, success, error) and optional URL updates.

components.functionality: Initializes the contact page by invoking getContactPageInfo with no parameters and storing the returned support_email, mailing_address, contact_categories, and faq_link_label for use by other components. It also coordinates with the URL-state component so that form initialization from URL parameters occurs after contact_categories are available.

Manages reading and writing URL query parameters that affect the contact form and status display. On initialization, it parses the current URL for keys like category, subject, email, status, and ticket_id, then exposes these values to components such as the contact form and status banner. After form submission, it can update the URL to reflect the new status and ticket_id without reloading the page.

Provides input fields for name, email, subject, category, and message, using contact_categories from getContactPageInfo for the category selector and optional prefilled values from URL parameters. On submission, it calls submitContactForm with the current field values and handles the response by updating form-level status and passing success, ticket_id, and message to the status banner and URL-state manager.

Displays feedback messages related to contact form submissions. It listens to both the latest submitContactForm response and URL parameters for status and ticket_id, then renders a success or error state accordingly. On success, it can show the ticket_id and any message returned by submitContactForm. On error, it shows the error message. The user can dismiss the banner, which may also clear status-related URL parameters via the URL-state manager.

Shows non-form contact options retrieved from getContactPageInfo, such as support_email and mailing_address. If support_email is available, it can be presented as a mailto link; if mailing_address is available, it is displayed as structured text. This component is read-only and updates automatically when getContactPageInfo is reloaded.

Displays a link near the contact form that navigates to faq.html using the faq_link_label from getContactPageInfo. When clicked, it constructs the navigation URL and optionally appends a category query parameter corresponding to the currently selected contact category key so that the FAQ page can focus on relevant content.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, max-width layout based on a 12-column grid with wide outer gutters (desktop breakpoint). Content appears within a central column spanning roughly 8–10 grid columns, with generous side margins.
layout.layout_pattern: The contact page uses a single, centered main column running top-to-bottom, inside a full-width, pale green main panel similar to the Biodiversity hero. The top of this panel is a quiet editorial intro block (page title and short paragraph), followed by the interactive contact form as the main focal element. Directly beneath the form comes the form status feedback (when present) and then a stacked secondary information area: first the alternative contact panel as a soft card, then the FAQ link block as a simple text/link row. The data-loader and URL state manager sit on top of or behind this flow: the loader briefly overlays the central content region when needed; the URL state manager is non-visual.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The contact page uses a single, centered main column running top-to-bottom, inside a full-width, pale green main panel similar to the Biodiversity hero. The top of this panel is a quiet editorial intro block (page title and short paragraph), followed by the interactive contact form as the main focal element. Directly beneath the form comes the form status feedback (when present) and then a stacked secondary information area: first the alternative contact panel as a soft card, then the FAQ link block as a simple text/link row. The data-loader and URL state manager sit on top of or behind this flow: the loader briefly overlays the central content region when needed; the URL state manager is non-visual.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The pale green main panel fills most of the viewport height on load. Its content column spans roughly 70–80% of the page width and is centered, leaving wide gutters of 10–15% on each side. The intro (heading plus short text) occupies the top 25–35% of this panel’s height with generous top padding. The contact form occupies the central 40–50% of the panel and is allowed to be tall, pushing the page to scroll if needed. Below the form, the alternative contact panel and FAQ link block share the remaining 15–25% of the initial viewport area and can extend below the fold. Vertical spacing between these major blocks follows the 48–64px section margin pattern, with tighter 24–32px spacing between closely related items like the heading and the introductory paragraph, or between the form and its status banner.
layout_strategies.content_density: The overall density is intentionally low. Each component is given comfortable breathing room on all sides, with no elements edge-to-edge. Text lines are relatively short due to the narrow central column and use a generous line height, mirroring an editorial article rather than a compact web app. Form fields are grouped with clear gaps between field clusters to avoid visual clutter. The alternative contact panel and FAQ link block present minimal, scannable information with ample whitespace rather than detailed, dense content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye begins at the global header/logo and top navigation, then drops into the pale green main panel where it immediately lands on the large left-aligned Contact heading. From there the gaze flows down to the brief explanatory paragraph, then naturally continues into the contact form fields, which dominate the central region. When a form status message exists, it appears directly above or immediately below the form header area so it’s seen before or as the user begins scanning the fields. After the form, the eye continues down to the alternative contact panel, then finally to the FAQ link block. A floating accessibility control (global, outside these components) sits in the lower-right, acting as a subtle secondary focal point once the main content has been scanned.

layout.overall_description: Below the slim global navigation, a full-width, very pale green panel stretches across the page, occupying most of the viewport. Inside it, a single centered column of content sits, framed by generous side gutters that keep the layout airy. At the top of this column, the Contact heading is large and left-aligned, with a short, multi-line paragraph beneath it explaining how users can get in touch; both are surrounded by ample vertical padding so they feel like a calm editorial hero rather than a marketing banner. As you scroll your eyes downward, the content column transitions smoothly into the main contact form, which fills the central portion of the panel, spanning most of the column’s width but still leaving comfortable margins on the left and right. Any feedback about the form’s state is shown tightly coupled to the form area so it is read in the same flow. Below this, the layout remains linear and light: a soft, bordered alternative contact panel appears as a secondary block, followed by a simple, understated FAQ link line aligned with the main column. The entire page feels restrained and calm, with lots of breathable negative space, a clear vertical reading path, and no competing sidebars—just a quiet sequence of well-separated sections on a soft, nature-toned backdrop.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact page loader appears as a subtle overlay centered within the pale green main content panel. When active, it covers the main content column from just below the global navigation down to the natural bottom edge of the panel’s visible area, slightly dimming the underlying content while keeping the full-width pale green background visible. A small, centered loading indicator (spinner plus short text) sits in the middle of the central column, aligned vertically around the midpoint of the viewport. Side gutters remain visible to preserve the framed, editorial feel, but user interaction with the form and other contact components beneath is disabled while the loader is present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay relative to the main content area; it sits on top of all other contact-page components when active, centered both horizontally and vertically within the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is non-visual and occupies no visible space in the layout. It lives behind the scenes, associated with the contact page container, and does not affect the positioning or spacing of any other component in the central column.
